One of my favorite games, period. Character creation is really robust, the story is relatively simple but well-written and solid (I actually CARE about these characters and care about solving the mystery), and of course the stars of the show, the monsters, are as awesome as ever. A lot of my grievances from World were answered by this game (the option to choose non-sexualizing armor for fem hunters, much better character creation, the handler isn't irritating as hell, etc.). I especially appreciate that the major recurring characters, except for Nata, are all women. And they're *characters*. Simple characters, yes, but they have personalities and feel like they actually had thought put into them. Gemma isn't just "the smithy", she feels like a character I've actually bonded with over the course of the story. I love whenever Olivia rejoins the picture and helps on a hunt.

I love how much screentime the hunter gets in cinematics, and I'm constantly finding myself taking screenshots. Really my only complaints with this game are the sheer size (over a 100 GB... surely it could've been optimized a LITTLE more?), the ever-present cosmetic DLC, the fact that even the buffest fem hunter body is still pitifully small, and the weird limitation on adjusting character appearance after creation. Yeah, you get two free hunter re-customization vouchers, but you have to pay for more beyond that, and why lock character re-customization behind a barrier like that anyway?

Overall, an extremely enjoyable game.

I play monster hunter from the first series, played through the MH dos in japanese on PS2 with friends and translate each characters from japanese to english to notes that we carry all around. Played all the series except the MHO (chinese release).

As a fan there are things that i love on MH wilds but much more things that i am disappointed with, even after the latest patch.

What i love :
1. This game gives me the nostalgic feelings of MH4u and MH dos (in a way).
2. The animation, and combats are awesome especially offset and perfect guard.
3. Cooking, fishing, mining, endemic and details (corpses, mining minerals, etc) are amazingly done.
4. Armor and weapon design are better than MH world and it brings back some old design.
5. Cinematic cutscene of monsters introduction.
6. AI support hunter makes it great alternative for the solo hunter to fight siege or combating apex with helps.

What i am disappointed with ;
1. I was expecting more immersion with the seasonal change system like what you did with MH dos. The monsters migrated and changes not only in numbers but some species and resources.
2. Talking about the season, immersion wise, i was expecting to have to wait for the season to change before you can take specific quest on specific season, this will give limitation but better complexity in game that brings up immersion.
3. Lack of small monsters variant and alpha variant of small monsters. Even herd of large monsters are rarely appear. I was expecting alpha balahara, alpha hirabami and other variant carnivorous small monsters.
4. I was expecting more requests and more side quests from villagers and help development of each village and the villager to progress through the story, IMO it will gives the end game much more rewarding and meaningfull.
5. The story almost all the time rush you towards completing it, it is not encouraging any exploration, finding new things, and appreciate the ecosystem of the world.
6. Poor optimization on performace and visual, most texture on armor and other things look worse than the MH world.
7. Most items that supposedly help the hunt are useless or easily ignored. Mostly only use flashpod, large dung pod and traps for hunts.

I love MH series, but for me personally MH wilds doesn't lived up to the hype and expectations of some old fans. I hope once the G rank expansion out they will fix most of the problem and brings out new experience.

In 43 hours, I only enjoyed 3 hours. The game is so boring; it’s literally the same gameplay: fight the same monster with a different skin and loot, and repeat the same thing again and again and again.
The online in this game is so bad we couldn't handle it. One of you starts the quest and others join; you can't roam around with your friends without a mission, and it always returns you to your world after you finish or lose it. Then, you have to go through the loading screens again to rejoin your friend.
Gameplay isn't fun when you repeat it. The fight is: go to the monster, hit it a billion times, then it runs away; follow it, repeat that two times, and there you go—that’s the entire game. You can cook and capture monsters, sure, but you paid what, $60–$70 for that?
You might ask, "If the game isn't fun, why did I keep playing after the first 3 hours?" Because after level 100, there are new hunts, builds, and stuff. I thought that was going to be fun, but no, it was still the same thing—repeat again and again and again. A truly awful and unbearably disgusting repetition.
And before you say anything about me, I did play Warframe for around 700+ hours; I don't mind repeating stuff if it is actually fun to do. The story, as usual, is trash—nothing special. Some characters are hella annoying and make no sense at all, while others are amazing, but that depends on your rating.
All of that aside, you can see my specs in the comments. I literally needed another software to shut down my second screen and everything else because the optimization is crappy. I can barely play at 80 FPS with AMD Frame Gen on the lowest settings for everything. I really wish I could refund this game because it was a waste of money and time. I wish I had spent my time reading a book or counting rice instead of playing this. I got it on sale for $30, and I still regret it so much.

Optimization: 1/10
People say Monster Hunter: World is better, but I won't try it myself because I’m not risking my money on that again.

For a start, I keep it simple: Yes! I can recommend Monster Hunter Wilds!

Now to the why:

I never really had any performance issues besides very few crashes right after release. I also know that my rig is non-standard, so take it with a grain of salt.

Story:
The Story in MH Wilds actually has some weight, which is kind of new to MH. It is not the most heartwarming or faszinating story, but it gets you interested in the lore and world of MH.

Soundtrack:
The music is beautifully crafted. The main theme gave me chills and every monster gets its own little theme, matching the musical style with the monster's type.

Visuals:
I think the game looks beautiful. Capcom does rely a bit too much on upscaling and frame generation, yes. But especially after a turbulant year they managed to bring it to a really good state.

Gameplay:
I fell in love with the complexity of MH combat. Being able to do so many different moves with each of the 14 available weapon types is amazing! Not just heavy and light attack spam. And they improves the combat even fuirther in Wilds. You are more agile, movement feels smooth, attacks can alter with directional input and you are less locked in place like before. I actually really appreciate the new focus mode system. It allows you to play more with camera orientation, and for some weapons it trivializes the execution of some moves that used to be much more difficult. You are not dependent on focus mode, and can absolutely play without it or in an on-and-off way.
I also appreciate the new moves and updates each weapon received!

Difficulty:
It is true, the first Monster Hunter you play is the most difficult. I believe, that MH Wilds is the best newcomer entry of the franchise. For someone who is a veteran or already experienced in MH, the game is not really a big challenge. Later on, in upper high rank, the difficulty takes a strong leap. I think this enables most new players to actually get a hang of the game before getting too frustrated. So when they then reach high rank and endgame, they are not overwhelmed with the actual difficulty. We are also aware that the upcoming G-rank expansion (Master Rank) will be a slap in our faces regarding difficulty.

Conclusion:
MH Wilds is not a perfect game, but in the current state it is absolutely fun and challenging. Also, fashion hunting was never that good! (we know this is the TRUE endgame :D)

Assuming you've played a Monster Hunter game before, this is more of the same with a few mechanical differences sprinkled about including mounts, finer precision control and some great looking weather effects. There are, however, a few glaring issues.

Firstly, the story is an absolute load of dry wank. It attempts to be incredibly melodramatic yet the characters are two dimensional at best, and barely receive any coherent development. This is, of course, only a problem pre-endgame (where the majority of the game is to be had), but even then it is an annoying chore to sit through.

Secondly, the game is pretty easy up until the very endgame, and even then it doesn't offer the kind of challenge more seasoned players will be looking for. I'm personally not very good at any of the MH games but even I found the start of the game to be pretty unengaging. I was well into High Rank before I carted for the first time.

Lastly, the performance and optimization is hot poo on toast. Frame drops are frequent even on acceptable hardware, and crashes are frequent enough to become annoying enough to cause me to write this review at 3 in the morning after the 3rd crash in the last 2 hours.

At this point, if you're new to the MH franchise then I'd consider picking up one of the other options available on the platform (World+Iceborne if you're after something of a more traditional MH game, or possibly Rise+Sunbreak if you fancy something a bit more action-y to sink your time into). Both are considerably more stable, challenging and feature complete than Wilds in it's current state.

Bad story. Good gameplay. Infuriating grind.

"This review assumes you're already somewhat familiar with the MH formula, especially Monster Hunter World"

0. The Performance

9070XT AMD GPU and not a single crash in about 60 hours of gameplay, which is probably lucky considering the amount of players reporting severe issues on that front. Otherwise, framerate is pretty good, about 95-100 FPS 1440p high settings with FSR4 enabled (and about 70-80 FPS without upscaling).

1. The Story

Absolutely miserable to go through. If you hate annoying characters, especially arrogant little kids (think Ava from Borderlands 3), this game's story is not for you. I'll avoid spoilers as much as I can but the events of the main story are centered around a little boy that is treated like the most precious, most important little king of the world by everyone who interacts with him (even by your voiced player character). It's insufferable. You're supposed to be part of a guild of monster hunters and you're all beholden to the whims and feelings of a small child. I've played a ton of games, experienced so many stories, and this is the only one where the dialogues made me physically cringe.

2. The Gameplay

The combat is really good, with many fun additions to the classic weapons movesets. Focus mode is great (especially on mouse and keyboard), and it makes fighting so much more comfortable than in previous entries.

3. The Grind

They made the endgame gacha. The only way to get to the final meta builds is to farm a set of materials that you can roll for a 0.05 chance to get a good weapon which completely warps your build as it massively synergises with your armor sets and their bonuses. It's so good that you can't really swap to another non-gacha one (with a different element for example) on the fly during a hunt because your build would collapse. If you like switching it up, playing different elements, or even different weapon types sometimes, you will always be weaker and feel weaker than on your main build.

And the only way to get the materials to craft these weapons is to farm for days on end what is probably the most badly designed and frustrating monster fight in the whole game. Examples of this dreadful design are :

- 400k HP, and you're expected to deal most of that damage with a special type of slinger ammo and environmental hazards.
- The monster is absolutely massive. If you want to go melee, you will be mostly blind to his movements as he will tower over you and obstruct your vision. He's also extremely fast and mobile for his size so you'll need to chase after him more than you can actually hit him.
- The monster can fly and can shoot lasers while flying, forcing you to look up in order to dodge these one-shot rays... except he will also throw a ton of explosive blobs on the ground that you can step on, that will also take 90% of your health bar. You're supposed to look up and down and the same time, constantly under pressure to survive, but you also need to damage him or he will launch a special attack that will instantly wipe you and your whole party.

This is not Monster Hunter. Half of the damage you deal doesn't even come from your weapon. This is a brutal gimmick fight that you need to repeat over and over to farm the endgame weapons.

Of course, you can skip all this and just not bother. Play suboptimally, play for fun, whatever. But it's still frustrating to finish your time with a Monster Hunter game still rocking a so-called "budget" or "poverty" build.

I'll ultimately recommend you play Monster Hunter Wilds as it is still a good game overall, and maybe the endgame can be fixed in the upcoming major expansion ? Story's unfixable though, good luck with that.

This isn’t exactly an easy review to write, admittedly. As of the time of this review, Monster Hunter Wilds is currently at the end of its base game update cycle. The game’s optimization has improved significantly over time, which makes recommending it far easier than it used to be. 

Monster Hunter Wilds is the latest installment of the Monster Hunter series. Its shining (and now decently optimized) visuals are complemented by a series of quality of life features that fundamentally change the way the game plays compared to previous titles. The first notable change lies in Focus Mode. 

Focus Mode allows players to hold down the L2 button to aim their attacks with infinitely more control than ever before. Greatsword users are no longer committed to minute movement while building up a True Charged Slash. As a Sword and Shield user, I practically held down the Focus Mode button for 90% of the fights. The control of movement is truly insane.

The next notable change involves the inclusion of the Seikret, which is functionally similar to the Palamute from MH Rise. However, the Seikret does not engage in combat. But it will get the player out of risky positions. Did you get hit and need to heal safely? Call the Seikret. Need to sharpen your weapon while not staying in place? Call the Seikret. Don’t know where the monster is? Call the Seikret. Actually, that one is kind of a falsehood. You’ll never not know where the monster is. It’s tracked at all times. You start a hunt and immediately know where your target is. Moreover, the Seikret will automatically move you to the monster’s position without any further input from you. You’re given an autopilot that removes the joys and pains of exploration and discovery. And that doesn’t feel right.

I say this because I’ve been playing the older games in the series lately. Specifically, I’ve been playing MH2, a game that seems to actively loathe the player. You actually have to find the monster (a quality of most Monster Hunter games, admittedly). Smaller monsters will incessantly interrupt you as you fight the main monster. Paintball markers conveniently fade away the moment a monster decides to move to another zone, causing you to run all over the map if you don’t already know the monster’s movement habits. (That one isn’t actually a design flaw, I think my luck is just awful. Speaking of which…) Drop rates feel brutal, especially after slaying 55 Congalala and getting only one Vibrant Pelt, which allegedly has a 21% drop chance upon breaking the head. Upgrades are remarkably costly. Hitboxes are nonsensical. Plesioth exists.

And I love it.

(The game, not Plesioth).

Playing MH2 so close to MH Wilds shows the wide disparity between the two titles. But is that such a bad thing? Is convenience such a damning quality? Is a series’s design supposed to be set in stone? 

Probably not. Kind of. And definitely not. 

Long before I even started playing these games, a discussion has raged on regarding the difficulty of the series diminishing over time. I’m contributing to this discussion by bringing up MH2 in a discussion of MH Wilds. And I still don’t have a conclusive feeling about it, ultimately. MH Wilds feels easy to me, but I’ve definitely gotten better at the games with the hours I’ve put into each game. The quality of life features merely exacerbate the sentiment of “this game is too easy.” 

I’ve seen some people online praising this game’s story. I’m grateful that it was so pleasing to those people, but I didn’t care for it much. Cutscenes were barriers that held me back from slaying monsters. Fortunately, those cutscenes are skippable. And you can hammer the confirm button through every dialogue section if you are a bloodthirsty freak like me. The characters might be good. I wouldn’t really know because I was much more concerned with the hunts than the characterization of the NPCs. Shrug. 

I wish there were more monsters in the game, but I also recognize the painstaking labor involved with modeling and programming these high quality models. The new monsters are pretty great. Arkveld rules and is a really fun monster to fight. Bullying Chatacabra will always be fun. Rey Dau is also plenty of fun.

Monster Hunter Wilds is a fun game, admittedly. I enjoyed my time with it. Offset Attacks and Guard Clashes are hilariously effective. Hunting Horn is ludicrously good. The soundtrack is better than solid. The graphics look great, but I’m the kind of guy who is still satisfied by the graphical fidelity of a PS2 game. So, you know, make your own aesthetic determinations for yourself. 

If you haven’t played a Monster Hunter game, you might really enjoy this game. The quality of life features won’t feel as incongruent with the philosophy of the series when you have no frame of reference to compare to. Maybe you’ll really like the characters. Maybe Artian Weapons will look aesthetically pleasing to you. But if they don’t, Layered Equipment will let you change the appearance of your equipment so you can be stylish without losing your precious equipment skills.

If you’re curious, I would recommend this game. But I’d be more inclined to recommend some of the earlier titles before recommending this one wholeheartedly. MH2 is brutal, but insanely satisfying as a result. MHGU is flashy, fun, and challenging (eventually). MH4U is wonderfully fun and charming, only held back by it being a 3DS exclusive. I didn’t like MH Rise when I started playing it, but I grew to like it more and more as I played it.

So, sure. Play Monster Hunter Wilds. You might just like it. And don’t be afraid to play with seasoned veterans. Generally speaking, Monster Hunter veterans tend to be so happy to help out newcomers. You’d be hard-pressed to find someone with more patience for rookie mistakes than a Rank 999 hunter. 

Maybe you’ll find yourself engrossed in the same obsession that already lies in the heart of so many Monster Hunter players.

I've been sitting on this opinion for awhile, and finally felt like doing it now for some reason. Basically, this game is mid in the purest sense. It's definitely not good, but if it was exceptionally horrible it would at least be something. I was lucky enough to not be hit by the horrible optimization, and I do have some compliments. I like how diverse they tried (emphasis on tried) to make the monsters, and even added cephalopods! It also has some of the theoretically funnest weapon kits so far. Other than that, this game is disappointing. I want to go over every point I have, for no other reason than that I wanna. I'll also be comparing it to World, as that is the best good monster hunter game to compare it to. It's not trying to be Old Gen, and Rise (which I actually like) is trying to be a modernized portable entry and not a spectacle like wilds is. 

POINT 1: ART & ENVIRONMENT
While the monster designs are generally okay, they're also rather boring. I'm happy they're adding new skeletons, but very few of the monsters are memorable. They also kinda forget to add all but the baseline ecological implications. While World's designs were often called boring, there was also a delicious amount of care put into their biology and ecology. It gave a sense of pseudo-realism to the monsters that gave even boring designs a little bit of edge. Wilds only cares about the superficial idea of ecology. Like, Great Jagras was a pack hunting secondary consumer that was a predator of Aptonoth while also prey to tertiary consumers like Anjanath and Rathalos. Chatacabra is just kinda... there. It's also confusing why an organism in a dry savannah wastes so much water on saliva production. The environments themselves are also just boring. Everything is just muddled browns, greens, blues, and greys with no distinct flow. The palatte itself is also oddly muted, and half the monsters kinda blend into the environment. The maps are bigger, but there is less in them. There is just zero personality or direction to the environment. There's also no ecology in the environment itself, especially compared to World. World took immense care on how the environment works, and that purposefulness makes both the gameplay and design feel a lot deeper. WIlds tries to mimic this with it's seasons, but all that system does is make farming more annoying. None of the species use the inclemency in an interesting way, other than the apex coming out. Even without the Seikret (we'll get there), the environments don't have nearly as much care put into them.

POINT 2: GAMEPLAY & COMBAT
Combat is too easy. While I said the weapon movesets were theoretically really good, it's held back by the monsters folding too fast 90% of the time to need any skill. The focus system is also horrible. There's no reason to use it aside from wound breaking, which just autostuns the monster and does way too much damage. There's also the fact only one player can hit them at a time, and some wound attacks are way better than others which leads to fighting over them. Guarding also feels way more OP with a giant deflect window that negates the main drawback of shields and power clash can free knockdown. As long as the monster isn't twoshotting you for daring to not have a perfect set you spent 400 hours making max level, you are never at risk. There's also "take two weapons at once" thing, which is kinda useless except for the need to carry two elements at once. Speaking of carrying, the Seikret is the worst. As alluded to, the environment is built to force you to spend all non-combat time on it. This makes the already empty and boring environments feel even emptier as you are automatically brought to the monster. Tracking had it's annoying moments, but it made you engage with the environment more and felt like, y'know, monster hunting. Every aspect of non-fighting has been made so frictionless you start to forget the environment around you even exists. Then there's the split between weapon and armor skills, which is super frustrating. The game is also super grindy in an unfun way. Having literally everything cost those stupid hunting tickets that are way too rare for something literally every endgame weapon costs. In world, my main limit was a rare material from the monster I was hunting (which could also be gotten for melding tickets if I was bored). In Wilds, I'm constantly missing that one item that is just a chore to get. This compounds with the fact you can't just do quests anymore. Unless it's in optional quests, you just have to wait for the monster you want to show up. Monsters also just drop less resources to account for the gear from wound breaks, but that just makes grinding even more of an RNGfest. It also has both randomized talismans and decoration, which weren't exactly popular features when separated. There's also the fashion game, which forces you to get every piece of armor. It also brings back the issue world had with most weapon designs being boring, but at least World had the benefit of the barebones design adding weapon gameplay variety. Each weapon in Wilds only has like half of the monsters for design options, and the armor is really boring. I really like how they removed gender lock, but having only like 3 sets that are interesting looking makes it a moot point. Imagine if we had Namielle, Savage Jho, Kulve Taroth, or the entire Rise roster. So the gameplay is more unfun, more annoying, and more randomized for less drip.

POINT 3: STORY & CHARACTERS
The fact they call this a story is cute. It's trying out some sort of serious environmentalist narrative, that is at odds with the fact you are mass hunting rare species. This wouldn't be a problem if it wasn't so focused on having the narrative take center stage. World's (and literally every game before it) story was unabashedly an excuse plot to explore and hunt. Wilds wants to be serious and thoughtful, but is also terrified of doing everything even remotely controversial. It brings back the cut lore of the ancient civilization, but also doesn't bring in any implication of humans being in the wrong. It wants to have unique human native cultures, but none of them do anything and are somehow ignorant over basic facts of the world. Azuz not knowing about Nu Udra makes about as much sense of a Canadian not knowing what a bear is. It's commitment to it's tone also just takes away the silliness that has always been apart of the series. We don't even get poogie or funny palico animations. This wouldn't be too bad if the story was any good. Can anyone remember anything that actually happened in it, even with how much they shove it down your throat? It's just thin pseudo-philisophical ramblings even a 14-year-old could see through. Then there's the characters. Oh, the characters. They're extremely safe and inoffensive, but are under the delusion that they're somehow deep. I'll say it: I liked handler from the very start. Sure, she's a lil stupid, but at least she had personality and free will. Alma feels like a robot, always praising you, supporting you, and making sure to never get in your way. It's like they saw a handful of losers complaining about handler, and needed to "fix" her. Were people really so angry that handler saying "we did it" when she does all of the research, paperwork, scouting, and maintenance for you? Nata is also an absolute nothingburger who contributes nothing to the story despite being a main character. Gemma is also just a pair of tits, and nothing more. It's not that I want to feel that way about her, but she does literally nothing and has no personality. I don't wanna sound like I think World had perfect characters, but at least they weren't so pretentious about it.

Everything can be summarized as this series trying to be "Safe." No vision, no plan, just corporate metrics. Unless they do something crazy for the DLC, this game is dead.

Okay so let's start this by saying that I am not an experienced hunter in worlds,rise and even wilds. I solo play most of the time with the new addition of support hunters and sometimes join in on SOS flares. So my opinion is based on a casual's perspective as many of the reviews here are from really experienced hunters and casuals like myself may not see the perspective of some.  For context, My HR is 125 and poured in around 100+ hours in wilds also 100+ hours in Worlds and HR rank miles from reaching a 100. I watch youtube and guides to get the best possible weapon and armor for myself. So if you are looking for an experienced hunter's review look on. That's definitely not me.

The Good:
- Support Hunters
I loved the addition of the support hunters as they are actually aggressive and will attack the monster. As a solo player, I can hunt without worries (as I have "backup") and if I cart I can just blame myself. Unlike Worlds, where its you alone or if you are lucky and someone joins you after you release your SOS flares. It is also helpful in Omega fights with its BS mechanics. For Low rank, Kai and Alyssa is a must for the 3rd support anyone else will do. For High Rank, you cannot go wrong with Fabius, Nadia and Kai. 

- Focus System
The focus system is definitely OP especially if you are using bow. But I welcome it. I guess it keeps things fresh for the MH installation and also to me it looks pretty cool when the wounds appear on the monster.

- 2 weapons
I love that you can bring 2 weapons now in a hunt. You could bring two of the same type of weapon but different elements or a short and long range combo. Its fun trying out new weapons mid battle especially when your experimenting to see what suits you. However do build your armor to your main weapon as you only have 1 set of armor and cannot counterchange mid battle.

- Gameplay
This could either be for you or not for you. For me it being easier than worlds is a plus for me as a casual standpoint. Do note when I say easy it means you can relatively play solo or with the support hunters with no problems up to HR100. After that, it takes abit of skill but with practice you can relatively do alright. It does get pretty grindy though especially if you want specific armor. (Omega Nodule arghhh) Because the true endgame is fashion.

- Shield Block
If you have a shield with activate perfect guard and you engage in a power clash it just looks so cool. Also, with lvl 3 guard up you feel like ironman when you tank omega's lazer attack.

- Blacksmith Gemma
She is bae.

The Bad:
- Price point
MH has been a relatively affordable game. Until now. It is definitely not worth the full price but do get it in a sale if you are thinking about it.

-Graphics
I do experience some fps drops at times to the point of lagging. My rig is pretty basic as you can see, and it managed to play Worlds really well with little to no fps drops or lagging even though worlds has more environmental content. It is mind boggling weird to me.

-The Environment
The environment compared to Worlds and Rise is bad. There is a tone of desert space in the plains with nothing but sand a few cliffhangers its not even an exaggeration its just sand. Compared to Worlds with a similar environment called the Wildspire waste that has more texture and vegetation and interaction with the environment and monsters. Also it just feels that wilds is just dull, not much color compared to the freshness and brightness of worlds and rise.

- CAPTURING A MONSTER
I love to capture monsters due to its increased rewards. But this isnt about that. In Worlds, when you capture a monster it appears bounded back in the base after the quest like a real captured animal. In Wilds, this doesn't happen especially when Wilds is being compared to Worlds mostly.

-Changing of weapons and accessing seikret pouch
This is pretty retarded. They should have a button for a simple change of weapon or accessing the pouch. Why should I mount the seikret to only change a weapon this destroys the fluidity of the game especially, when you mount the seikret it runs randomly and you have to control it while also changing a weapon or accessing the pouch.

- Nerfing of Insect Glaive
Basically Insect Glaive can't fly much anymore haiz.


Overall 6.5/10 for me.  Get this if you are very solo player based. But stick to worlds if you have friends that can hunt with you. Either way, and I cannot stress this enough, only get it in a sale. Im just gonna give this a thumbs up for now as I really love the support hunters mechanic.

In my experience of playing the game I'm neutral on it, for the game being 70$ its not worth the price, I did get the game when it was on sale but still its not worth buying if your looking forward to playing for a longtime especially after storymode. Pros: New seikret companion (its cute, and an amazing addition to the game it was the main reason I got the game), graphics (amazing quality when I played it on my steam deck but I did have to tweak the settings a little to stop the lagging), lastly I loved the end mission interaction’s with my seikret and palico.  
Cons: Not enough monsters (for a new addition to the monster hunter game franchise there are not as many monsters in the game, for example in monster hunter world there are 34 large monsters in total in the base game and in the ice born DLC there where 35 more monsters added, meanwhile monster hunter wild gets 29, which is very disappointing), Small map, not enough places to explore (The map gets small fast after story mode, and it becomes boring), Boring story mode (The story mode is just all around fast and boring, there are not memorable moments or anything, it would have been better if it was like monster hunter rise), not being able to opt out (out of all the things I have an issue with the biggest one is the fact you CANNOT remove Alma for following you after story mode there isn't even an option to have her invisible or stop talking)
Nit picks: More pet interaction’s (unlike monster hunter rise you cant just sit down and chill with your pals, especially the palico that is just saddening), world DLC’s (There need to be a expansion dlc that is affordable and makes the world bigger, adds more monsters and maybe even story mode but it can be optional), more places to go (I wish you can just climb on anything with your seikret), Jumping (just in general I wish there was jumping and on your seikret too, its very stiff not being able to jump when you want and only when your character is scripted to jump.)
-rant over

Final thoughts- the game is not worth the money, it’s not as fun or creative as monster hunter rise, or as diverse monster hunter worlds, the game is lifeless and stiff, after a 5 days of finishing story mode and being able to play the game freely it became boring, Maybe a expansion DLC will fix this but until then my opinion still stands. Just get monster hunter rise or worlds.

the bad reviews are 1/3 performance issues/optimization, 1/3 "the game is just too easy," or 1/3 supposedly bad story

[h3] PERFORMANCE ISSUES/OPTIMIZATION [/h3]
As far as I know, they have been fixed. But yeah, the pc optimization could be a little better but the game is still good without this.

[h3] DIFFICULTY [/h3]
The wounds system allows for lots of damage along with a bunch of staggers/knockdowns. Also, firing an SOS flare will let support hunters join, which is just extra damage. But remember, folks, the game doesn't need to be challenging to be fun.

[h3] STORY ISSUES? [/h3]
The story was good. From what I'm seeing, most people hate the "walk-and-talk" sections that aren't able to be skipped. If you are complaining about that, then you probably just mash the buttons during dialogue and skip as many cutscenes as you can to get to the fighting parts. Also I've seen people complain that the player character isn't a "newbie hunter"??? Like ok I guess the player can't have a backstory then :/
Also people complain about Nata, and while yeah, they could've wrote his character a little better, he wasn't that bad. You gotta remember, he's like, eleven or twelve or something and has (quite literally) been living under a rock his whole life.
The story was really good for a Monster Hunter game and I enjoyed it a lot.

I give this game an 8/10, it's great and I enjoyed it, but there's room for improvement. If you're interested in this game but you're debating whether to buy it or not, wait to get it on sale.

I never expect it to be like any game before it or after it. they all have there own different and distinct identities. Monster hunter 1 was the base, Monster Hunter Dos had a season and home village building systems, MH 3/3u had underwater combat, MH 4/4u had verticality, MH g/gu had hunter styles and arts, MH World had semi-open worlds and clutch claw, MH Rise had wire bugs, and MH Wilds has focus mode and weapon swapping. For as easy as the game is, a lot of that can be explained by the improvement of the player's movement. As a lance main, this is easily the biggest updates to your move set, and the addition of focus strikes takes it from a counterpoking beast into a surgical monster-skewering behemoth with the biggest drawback of the weapon removed- the inability to aim pokes after initiating.

The monsters are a bit of a mixed bag, frontloading all of the baby-easy monsters into low rank. This is fine, but the early game monsters all have a feeling of being designed for earlier gen games and didn't get any new tools themselves to compete with the hunters' overall improved movesets and mobility. The late game is where the Wilds shines because it remembers that it has mechanics that they can take advantage of in fights, with Seregios and Arkveld being standouts for being incredibly quick and having tricky movesets as well as opportunities for quick players to take advantage of, and it makes me excited for the DLC.
The story does suck though. I get wanting to have the story be more of a focus, but I feel something closer to MH4/U or World would have been better than the on-rails sections that are quite prevalent throughout the low rank questline, and forcing me to listen to Nata and Alma yap away while we make our way towards the hunt target or next village at the blistering speed of 4MPH. Being held hostage by them only made me end up disliking them.
As for performance, I never experienced an issue, but my PC is also kind of overkill. I did however hit a few moments where the cutscenes would play at normal speed, but the audio would desync heavily during a few of the more loading-intensive ones that just snap you to a new area or need to load in all of something like Gogmazios. I would also get LOD models when the game would hard change areas mid-cutscene, but as far as actual gameplay is concerned, I experienced no issues.
Overall, it feels great to play once you escape the story and get to the challenging fights, but the early game is kind of braindead easy and babysitting Nata and Alma in the story feels tedious.

I am indifferent on my experience with this game. On one hand, I've had a fair amount of fun with it for about 58 hours. On another, I've been frustrated by the performance and just overall jank.

I am an ultra casual MH player. I don't play these games to get HR100+ and do all of the crazy G rank hunt stuff like previous games. I kill some monsters for 40-50 hours and the grind causes me to throw in the towel. I'm pretty sure I'm already in that phase with the game now.

I guess my biggest issue is, I don't recall there being such a massive control response issue in MH Rise or World, which were the last two MH games I played on Steam. There is some absolutely insane level of input buffering or animation locking going on where when you press buttons things just DO NOT HAPPEN some times. Biggest example is me trying to put my gun away to drink a potion. I have to hit the "E" button twice at least to holster my bowgun, but I'm running from the monster and my character might have stepped on a small incline so the animation of that happening basically cancels my holstering, so I have to hit the button again to make that happen. During that, I am mashing "E" to drink my Mega Potion but my character is yet again in some sort of small animation that is preventing that from happening.

I know MH games have a commitment to every single thing you do but it was NEVER THIS BAD in the last two games. I have tested this with and without Frame Generation and it doesn't matter. It's not input delay, it's just straight up animation locking and buffering.

The performance of this game is not good at all. I have a fairly good computer and his game's optimization is in the trash. I have to lower settings down to fairly mediocre levels, run DLSS Balanced and turn off all the nice bells and whistles like Ray tracing in order to get an acceptable framerate at any time. I shouldn't have to compromise the game's image quality so much just to get 60+ FPS but I do. 

I have settings dialed in right now that look "acceptable" but honestly the game has this really washed out, fuzzy look to it and I think World, a game from 2017 actually looks better. I don't really understand that but it's definitely something. I think MH Wilds has better animations and environment detail, but as far as graphical fidelity and art direction are concerned, World was better by a lot, and Rise had more color and pop to it.

Despite all of that, I still think I enjoyed the game enough to recommend it. I am glad that I bought it on sale, and that is where I'd recommend anyone to do as well is wait for a sale like 40% or something. It's definitely worthy enough for that. Hell, they might even make the game better with future updates.

I might come back to this one eventually but I think I'm just already tired of the HR grind (Hit HR63 as of review) and I'm not having as much fun anymore because I have to grind so much for materials and fix my build and I'm just not motived at all to do that.

Preface: I have beaten every monster and most events that are/have been active. Performance has been spotty the entire duration of playing this game with some updates resulting in my game crashing frequently. This game has also encouraged me to revisit some previous titles and try MHS2: Wings of Ruin.

Visually: I love what this game could be through use of RE Framework, manual editing of the configuration files located in the local installation of the game. Unfortunately, this game is visually boring, oversaturated, and most visual graphic settings have little to no impact on the appearance of the game that mods can allow with the benefit of better performance while having more visually distinct biomes.

Gameplay: It's fun. The gameplay is much better than previous titles while having the main mechanics be fun without being a requirement to fully understand them. Most of the monsters are fun to hunt, while most story hunts feel short compared to World, or extremely easy compared to Rise.

Overall the game can be fun, but I have played a lot less of this game compared to World (even pre-Iceborne). The environments are less visually interesting. They feel less impactful considering the lack of verticality and truly original ideas that aren't ankle deep. The story is weak, no true impact of the environment that isn't noticeable through tracking or shown beyond slightly more aggressive weather that retroactively explains the fauna that exists in a region.

[h1] Worded thoughts [/h1]
So I want to start out by saying that I am a lifelong monster hunter fan, I am a fair bit younger than most but my first experience with the series was 4u on the 3ds and later with Tri on my Wii and have played every game in the series though only a few to absolute completion, if you are a fan of specifically old gen monster hunter you likely won't like this game it basically does away with a lot of pre 5th gen stuff making things like aiming your weapon and prepping for hunts is minimized and made easier here. However this is not to the games weakness especially as a monster hunter game, there are 7 (not including G rank expansions and including Frontier) old gen games with hundreds of hours of content Monster Hunter as a series should not be tried to be held to the standards that it hasn't kept itself since World and especially as a newer game the Gameplay is a meaningful step forward from 5th gen. Keep in mind this is only talking about gameplay, I personally abhor Capcom as a company and the microtransactions are VERY bad though i'd say a bit better than Rise. Ultimately MH is a series dominated by which one you like the most but this game is one i personally will reccomend.

I have played all the mainline and portable Monster Hunter games in the series since Tri, and this is the game that I have enjoyed the least. In my opinion, this game is moving away from the hunting and preparation aspects, and cutting down on the experience and immersion of the older generations of the franchise. 

This is because of a lack of consequences of the player's choices, here are some examples of design choices that contribute to this problem:

1) Melee weapon classes losing their distinct identities - the weapon classes have their movesets expanded to include counters and perfect guards. Whilst people who only play one weapon type and drop the game might not see the issue here, this design choice cuts down on the replayability of the game that was in previous Monster Hunter games; I am having less fun fighting monsters with different weapons classes because they feel similar to one-another.

2) Offset attacks, slinger and wounds - with all these different methods of flinching and staggering the monsters, the importance of learning the monsters' movesets and playing around the monsters' strengths are diminished; I no longer feel like the monsters are forces of nature to be reckoned with, I can just go toe-to-toe and stunlock them.

3) Maps and seikret - the locales in this game are massive and filled with details and wildlife, but with the seikret's autopilot feature you are being transported directly to the monsters, there is no incentive to explore these locales, making the gameplay stale. Moreover, there is no longer any need to learn about the monsters and prepare before hunts, that is you no longer need to bring hot / cold drinks etc. because every location in the maps spoonfeeds all the quality of life that you ever need.

This is a tenative review. As of writing this review, it is Post-Title Update 4, but before any news of the inevitable expansion.

For the short version, I'd recommend waiting until the game is on sale. While it's in the best state since launch, I still can't in good faith recommend the game at the full $70, especially when all the other games also go on amazing 90% sales. 

For the long version, I will divide this review into 3 sections: The Problems, The Solutions, and The Successes.

The Problems.
- There are a ton of really cool mechanics, but most of them feel half-implemented. Monster corpses rot, there are Pack Turf Wars, environmental destruction leading to new ways to stun and damage monsters. Yet most of these mechanics fall under the radar in favor of the other problems.
- Seikret. I both love and hate these things. Cool, I get MH chocobos. Not cool, they auto path, making most of the beautiful traversal pointless. It often feels like the game was designed around ignoring most of the cool stuff it does, and I really dislike it. 
- Slinger. I don't think this gets talked enough, but the Slinger is forced. It feels like it was added to the game just for the sake of mirroring World, and it's hard not to notice it. You get so many different damage types, yet most of them are useless except for lure pods, flash pods, and dung pods, which all could have been handheld items on their own. I also feel like it ruins the feel of natural gathering that previous games had, as you just spam one button on your Seikret as you race by. It also really annoys me that so many environmental hazards are so reliant on using the slinger, particularly against Jin Dahaad who has the most egregious fight for this. Unless you play SnS, the slinger is just really clunky to use in the scope of actual battle.
- I know this is a common gripe, but I think my issue with the balance is that the difficulty of the game didn't increase in proportion to the tools each weapon gained. Especially with offsets, perfect dodge, perfect guard, immunity frames, etc., there's too many ways to avoid damage, which the monsters, which feel the same in difficulty as World and Rise, don't really burst through. The Arch-Tempereds especially showcase this issue, where even with faster attacks, their attacks are clear to read, which when you're given a free immunity button on most weapons makes them really easy to counter. You're not in a 1v1 with monsters, like in past games, you're bullying them. For better or for worse. 

The Solutions.

With the expansion on the horizon, I have hope that the continued passion they're showing will let them fix some of these things, so here are my thoughts from my personal experience. 

- Give us more options regarding Seikrets. I want to be able to turn off my mini map, the scoutflies, and the auto pathing. I want missions where I'm not allowed to use my seikret or palico, forcing me to change my strategies and learn new techs. I know some people actually prefer this style or just want to go straight to the fight, and I get that alot of MH Wilds's design is around giving newer players to the series a sense of awe. But at the same time, I do want options to play the game in my own way, and experience the overall design of the game. 
- Some map changes would be in order. I think what we have is great, but at the same time there's more I'd like to see. I would love to see more of an underground layer in the plains, lava tunnels in the basin that burn you, and an overall increase in the map size for the cliffs and ruins. I think for a game about "wilderness" and expansiveness, the camps themselves feel one dimensional, and the biomes themselves feel claustrophobic. I'm sure people would complain about "a whole lot of nothing" but I want that nothing if it's scenic, especially since travel is so easy with camps and seikret. 
- Make terrains breakable by weapons. Have land and aerial obstacles to fight monsters with, but you should be able to break them with a bow or smash them with a lance to cause an effect. It's fine if there's a potential for accidents too! I want an excuse to need to be more aware of my surroundings and use my knowhow to beat up a monster. You can then add an additional way of creating obstacles by using slinger ammo. Would be cool if I need heavy piercing and fire ammo to fight Jin Dahaad in cliffs for example, and would make fighting him less annoying. It's like I have Breath of the Wild's TP and travel system with none of the map size to justify it.
- The logbook should note the times a monster spawns. The changing weather is amazing and one of the best features in the game, but it can be annoying to look up info that should be in the game in a wiki.
- On that note, the Weather should affect you in some way. It feels weird that I can see through a sandstorm, drink pepper juice to survive a blizzard, have my footing during a rainstorm, etc. I want the game to FORCE me to live with nature. That way then the Apex fights would also just that little bit more difficult, since you're not fighting in the same conditions as always. 
- More vertical combat? I don't know, it's weird to add in the scope of wilds, but like wedge beatles are useless and a lot of weapons have fallen short solely due to the loss of verticality from Rise (looks at switchaxe and insect glaive). In general though, I think some reanalysis needs to be on weapons other than Bow, Greatsword, or Lance. The rest have some real problems that while they do insane amounts of damage just feel worse to use. 
- Artian RNG. You know what I'll say. There needs to be a better way to reroll an rng mechanic. There's already plenty of grinding in the game, there doesn't need to be more. 
- A more fluent way of connecting between regions. The game would feel much more immersive if it was one gigantic map, rather than a bunch of separate maps. Would make the invasion of dosahguma into and out of the forest much cooler.
- More packs. Add more packs. You have a cool system, and legitimately there's only 2-3 packs in the entire game, with Hirabami being the only "real" one since Yian Kut-Ku scatter and Doshaguma aren't real packs. Facing more "alpha" monsters would also just be cool, which you can imagine my disappointment when Doshaguma was the only one with this concept. 
- Make rotting corpses more essential? Like, I almost find no reasons to gather the items on the corpses, and to be honest, don't even notice them because of the aforementioned problems. Would help if the game was all one map, like noted above. You don't have to load it all at once, but it would be a lot more immersive if racing across the map meant that I also saw my previous kills, and maybe even smaller monsters eating the corpses I leave behind. 

The Successes
- The Apexes holy s**t they're cool. All 4 (+ Arkveld) are incredibly sick. 
- I actually really appreciate the lack of Elder Dragons. I know there'll be more added in DLC, but I appreciate that they don't drown out the already small ecosystems of the base game. 
- The music is insanely good. So much love and passion went into it and I appreciate it for that.
- The armor and weapons look insanely good. I wish there were more goofy ones, but that'll come with time and events. The character creation being one of the best I've ever played with is also a nice touch. 
- Perfect Dodge and Perfect Guards are insanely fun. So are offsets. Just being able to dodge a monster's fire breath WHILE IN THE BREATH gives so many cool points, I can't express this enough. 
- I actually really enjoy the scripted boss fights. While they make the fights less difficult, it does make them really fun. Gogmazios and Zoh Shia are just fun to fight, and to be honest I don't get to fight them enough. 

That's all I have for now. I love Monster Hunter and the game is in a good state, but it absolutely could be better.

Once again, I have dusted off my hunting gear and ventured into the Forbidden Lands. And while the scale is impressive, I find myself looking back at my time in World with a certain aristocratic nostalgia. You see, being a Lord of my stature means I have a refined taste for things that actually work.

Don't get me wrong, Wilds has its charms. The monsters are fierce and the world is vast, but it lacks the "mature" elegance of its predecessor. Monster Hunter: World felt like a masterfully tailored velvet suit, it fit perfectly, it moved with grace, and it ran smoother than a greased pig at a country fair.

The developers seem to have forgotten that a Lord does not appreciate his vision being interrupted by technical stutters and unoptimized performance. World was a testament to what happens when devs truly polish their craft until it shines like the gold in my baroque chambers. Wilds, on the other hand, feels like a rough diamond that someone forgot to buff. It’s a bit clunky, a bit shaky, and frankly, my noble eyes deserve better frame rates than this.

I expected a revolution, but instead, I find myself missing the stability and the sheer "finished" feel of my previous hunting grounds. It is a good game, certainly, but it lacks that final touch of excellence that made World a true masterpiece of the genre.

And Capcom, I shall say it once more: The lack of a certain pale, electric gentleman is an oversight that even my Longwood cannot ignore.

Final Verdict: 7/10 – Entertaining, but I prefer my hunts as smooth as my silk sheets.

To preface this, I have been playing MH since Tri. The original Tri.
I'm surprised by this game in a positive way. The way people talked about it made it seem like Satan himself, but it does have some redeeming factors. Rise being available on PC also makes this game irrelevant however. If you want MH, buy Rise/Sunbreak, and thank me later. It's an okay game, and a mediocre MH in the end.

Some weapons, like the Gunlance, got a nice make over. Really fun to use now. Overall the combat feels pretty good. The entirety of low rank and most of high rank is way too easy however. 
Some of the new Monster design are also quite nice, including the introduction of a brand new group of them, the squidies, is welcomed as well.
It's also a very pretty game...if your rig is a monster. Mine is, buuuut...going to the bad points...

It doesn't look much better than World and has no excuse running this badly on most systems. My old Laptop was able to run Iceborne on very high and now struggles with Wilds. The steam deck just dies, while World, again, runs fine. It's pathetic. And this is after the performance patch.

Another thing I am baffled at is how they cut so many armor designs compared to the previous titles. Usually there are two design per gender (4 in total), and now there are only 2 in total. Fashion Hunters on the brink.

The open maps themselves are a mixed bag as well. The desert is fine, and the scarlet forest is pretty, but the rest is completely forgettable. The Oilwell Basin is a good idea, but completely underused and Wyveria? It's the ruins of a ancient civilization with building blocking out the sun...so its a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ circle without much Colour to it. Boring.
The story is forgettable , and both Low Rank and High Rank have almost the same structure, with the same monsters being fought in the same order. High rank does have a random MH4 plotline thrown in for like a quest and then it was forgotten about. Friendly reminder that frenzy monsters are in this game, I don't blame you if you forgot.
There are also not that many monsters to hunt, even after the updates. Some of them also seem like a copy of an already existing monster. Rey Dau is just TEMU Astalos. I even exclaimed "Is that a f-ing Astalos?" when it showed up in a cutscene.
 Guardian Monsters are just C/P so having Rathalos and Guarding Rathalos etc. is pointless.
The Flagship, Arkveld, has to be the most forgettable monster in this series. Especially after the two Rise Flagships being amazing

There's more but I save my breath here. Get it if you have nothing better to do, but if you want MH, get Rise/Sunbreak.

After an entire year, the game *finally* runs at more than 15 FPS and has a Final Boss Of The Story with a craftable armor and weapons:
[h1]an okay game, a bad Monster Hunter[/h1] and sadly, I expected a Monster Hunter

The gameplay is fun and all but *way* too easy, resulting in a BIG problem in term of vibe and setting imo.

Monster Hunter was always about how monsters were forces of nature, dangerous, and how humans lived in settlements while the world felt wild (lmao) and perilous.
Here, you can find one of the dozen of spots in the forest, shoot a flair up and have a UberCamp delivered immediately with no reaction from the monsters - they MIGHT destroy the camp if they walk by later but just pay some points and it automatically pops back up.
Sorry but the sense of danger is kind of out the window when I can literally cook a meal during a fight and not have any drawback.

With World & Wilds, Capcom decided to have a waaaay bigger focus on the story - and by that I mean having the story actively being in the way of playing the game.
I'm not going to talk about the story itself too much because, honestly, it didn't have that much of an impact on me - but the fact that the CORE GAMEPLAY feels almost secondary during low rank is weird.

Honestly, I think one of the core problem I have with the game can be explained with one quote from Our Hunter : [spoiler]By my own order, I'll slay Zoh Shia[/spoiler]
Cool quote. [b]Really[/b] not the licence or even game it should be in.
We spend the ENTIRE game having Alma give the authorisation to start the quest - which, in setting, is accurate and understandable because she IS our handler. 
But because Capcom wanted A Cool Moment™, they decided to go against that core concept of the Guild/game world.

Writing this review also made me realise the "human power-creep" present since World, and how it changes the setting and the vibe
- In MH4U, you were part of a caravan and helped villages with their problems while searching for The Plot - you're a Hunter, one of the category of people capable of being safe where monsters walk free ;

- In World, you're part of an expedition to discover The New World and one of the FIRST CUTSCENE is how there are monsters LITERALLY AT THE GATE of the base but somehow, they never bother attacking it ? The Handler walking around unarmed and getting "attacked" (she never actually get hit tho) during multiple cutscene doesn't help the "is there really any danger ?" vibe. Also, the starts of the DLC makes it feels like you just build an entire basecamp in barely a few days without any issues in, again, the middle of an unknown continent ;

- In Rise, you're basically a Naruto character - I'm certainly not the first one to make the parallel but it feels accurate : both series, at their beginning, were about strategy and trying to out-manoeuvre your opponent but ended up with "The Protag Is So Cool And Awesome And Will Explode Everything" ;

- And finally, Wilds. You can literally walk out of the main camp of the local and see a Large Monster 5s after. Gemma and Wagner, 2 of the engineers, can just show up in the middle of the map and say "HEEEEEEY, YE WE SET UP OUR CONTRAPTION IN THE MIDDLE OF THE ECOSYSTEM WITH A LOT OF MONSTERS - RIGHT HERE, HEY, YOU HEAR ME". You can fire a UberCamp without any problem. Cooking in the middle of nature without attracting any unwanted attention. For a game that was supposed to be focused on the ecosystem, the STORY is but the monsters themselves almost don't feel like "Large Weird Animals" anymore but just "Boss walking around and waiting to get attacked" ;

[hr][/hr]

[h2]Edit[/h2]

After writing this review, I went back to check MH4U and some other things bothered me :

[h3]The quests and the relationship with the world[/h3]
In the "old games", you got a lot of quests from villagers and travellers who had troubles with monster roaming near villages or roads, but in World & Wilds, we're CLEARLY the one encroaching and hunting monsters who are in our way - which, again, plays A LOT in the "human power-creep" vibe

Another points that feels weird in term of "vibe": the presence of SOOOOOOOO MANY PEOPLE outside of proper established settlements - the entire LICENCE is based on the idea that, outside of the civilized lands, you [b]WILL[/b] get mauled by a random Arzuros. 
In Wilds ? Somehow it has been decided that The Guild will go ahead and colonize some land with [b]multiple unprotected outposts[/b] and never have any problem while [b]the natives of the region, with a deterrent that worked until now[/b] SOMEHOW get attacked

[h3]The colors[/h3]
In World and Wilds, they're gone. 

Capcom decided to go "Ooooh, Big Serious Time, we need to go Realistic Desaturated As ♥♥♥♥ And Boring" so even with ReShade, it still barely reaches the level of color MH4U had.

And when I say "colors", I also mean in a metaphoric way : those games lost SO MUCH of their whimsy - the design of the ship in MH4U is silly but *fits* in MH universe, and Wilds is better on that part but lets not forget the "Copy-Paste The Bone Weapon But Add Two Feather And Say It's A New Design" of World

[h3]The "technological vibe"[/h3]
Together with the previous point, I feel like there has been a shift from the previous games in term of "vibe" for the technology in World/Sunbreak/Wild.
In MH4U, the sand-ships looked weathered, the ship looked silly and cobbled together - giving a bit of a vibe of "maybe don't bother polishing the floors when a Dah'ren Mohran could wreck your ship next time you go out";

In World ? The Smithy somehow have a more technologically advanced forge than any other seen in the licence before *by a good margin*, despite being on another continent

And in World/Sunbreak/Wilds, the ships seen are perfectly constructed and are really just Classic Boring Three-Masted Western Ship *but with some bones on the lower part* to say "we didn't just copy-paste a preexisting design"

Performance is still a massive issue. The team are doing great working through it, but if you like testing your skills in speedrunning the monsters, ~40 fps gives a 20% damage boost for bows due to how logic is tied to FPS, and basically if you want a free 20% damage bonus you can just play really really choppy and it feels just terrible. Framegen doesn't make up for the fact those frames don't exist and make the game feel clunkier and less intuitive. Dunno if this affects the other weapons, but anything that uses piercing or multiple hits like LS Helmsplitter are all affected and get more ticks at 38 fps. 

The early story is quite railroaded, but it acts as a tutorial for all the systems as you gradually unlock them, and properly integrates most of the systems like our auto-farm is actually explained through a quest which helps the world feel alive. 

Artian and Gogmazios weapons are universal pieces of gear across all weapons and are the best weapons you will find for every purpoise, which massively narrows your grind and for me undoes the feeling of building my own gear as it's collecting tokens to exchange from a  magic cauldron as opposed to feeling like I'm commissioning a weapon from the blacksmith with parts I've hunted.

Depending on how you like, the artian weapon system is less of a grind than decos in World/IB, but remove a lot of the variation in the grind and for me I would prefer the monster weapons being the best and artian being a patch-up as they also look REALLY bad for the most part, and layering weapons is kinda awkward so it's a lot of faff just to not wield toilet bowl dual blades or a bow with 5 strings for no reason...

The game itself is really really fun. Weapons feel good, they are generally very fun, and monsters don't feel cheap with lots of variation and amazing dance-style fights to enjoy. It's just a shame that the design philosophy of going for universal weapon upgrades that discourage fighting different monsters, and the general performance issues make the endgame a bit of a chore. It's still MUCH better than World endgame, but that's not really saying much...

As a long time Monster Hunter fan I couldn't be more happy with Monster Hunter Wilds since launch. It was certainly a rough start with optimization however I've been playing on a cheap laptop with medium to high graphics with little to no issues outside of the first few days after each major title update. The replayability feels so much more fluid than both World and Rise with the jump in difficulty spiking quite high post HR 100. Whether you're new or not this title does feel far more easy and the story isn't as long since it basically concludes in low rank. Nothing too bad as this kind of 'downside' feels redundant to complain about as previous titles have been similar. The borderline seamless multiplayer sessions are very nice however I do find link parties to crash more so than just having a hunter friend lobby so do keep that in mind. The large monster roster is possibly the 3rd most diverse in the entire series as well and breathing new life into older monsters with improved fights, looking at you Gypceros. For $70 you're going to get so many hours worth of your money and we haven't even received the G-Rank/Master Rank expansion. As someone whose played since 3U I can confidently say Monster Hunter Wilds earns its spot in the top 3 titles among 4U+GU and Rise. The weapons and focus system can indefinitely make learning combos far easier than previous games and the wound system is extremely busted I do have hopes that the expansion will make these systems more impactful in a difficulty matter. In the end whether you're looking for a game to play with a small group of friends or prefer a solo experience I highly recommend Monster Hunter Wilds or even any of the other games that are available for purchase World, Rise, the Stories Trilogy. They're all fantastic games that offer drastically different experiences around the same precedent; hunt, forge, learn, repeat. And a final message to clear any outstanding questions, yes Monster Hunter will feature monsters in it.

[b]TLDR:[/b]
- Recommendable, but just barely.
- Nowhere near as engaging and immersive or epic-feeling as World.
- Easier to get into than Rise in terms of mechanics and monster difficulty due to some very noticeable streamlining for better or worse.
- To this title's credit, the linear progression early on is extremely frustrating because the core gameplay itself is actually enjoyable.

[b]The Bad:[/b]
The story (if that's what it can be called) is garbage. The dialogue in said "story" is garbage. The NPCs (except for the merchants) and their poorly cobbled roles in the "story" are all garbage. Even some of the side quests are nonsensical garbage. Being constantly roped around by those very same garbage NPCs into a train of seikret riding tours and walking simulators with said garbage story and having to listen to their garbage dialogue is maddening as hell! It's annoyingly linear, literally and figuratively, for hours and hours on end unless you manage to get a break and just explore on your own. But then of course your HR is capped, so you have to deal with that along with other limitations until the "story" is almost complete. Even having to constantly deal with Alma pushed my patience to the limit. You have to get through at least 4-5 chapters of this ridiculous garbage before the "story" finally starts wrapping things up, stops leading you around by the nose, and actually hands you the bloody reins so you can really play the damn game without babysitting a bunch of pointless NPCs.

Yes, I realize how many times I repeated the word "garbage", because I found myself constantly cursing at how aggravating these production and UX elements were done, so anything else would be bound to be censored. And honestly, due to the manner in which they put the player through such an exceedngly exasperating user experience, it deserves that degree of severe criticism.

[b]The Good:[/b]
Now, finally. With that out of the way, if you have the patience to get through all of that mind-numbing, cliche, campy, nonsensical garbage (and it's going to take A LOT of patience), the game itself is actually good. The size of the world, the map designs, the environments, the visuals, the variety of monsters, as well as the monster fights themselves and the mechanics therein, are all great!

There's the occasional crash, especially when trying to move the camera around during panoramic shots or while riding and harvesting at the same time, but so far it's been less than ten times in all the hours I've played at the time of this writing. In terms of performance and stability, at least on my machine, it's been pretty good.

This game has a ton of potential to be fun but right now its just not...

So first the good: The fighting can be very fun at times, every weapon has its own fighting style and the potential for crazy combos and explosive specials will have you experimenting for hours. The wound system is clever. The armor and weapon forging can get very addictive, theres tons of different options and they are all genuinely unique. 

Now the bad: By far the worst part of this game (and also the easiest thing to fix) is how tanky the monsters are. You can easily spend 15-30 minutes (no exaggeration) just wailing on one monster. Secondly, the monsters will all run from you 4-5 times per fight and you have to chase them down. Every hit knocks you flat on your ass and it takes 5-10 sec to get back up, so some fight can feel like your just getting ragdolled and stumbling around more than actually fighting. So after all that when the thing doesnt drop what you need you want to put a hole though the monitor. Theres lots of other smaller things: The game doesnt run great, it doesnt particularly look good either. The story and characters are flat and one dimensional. combat feels sloppy, sometimes you need to hit the button 4-5 times before the game registers the action. theres no lock on, your hunter will just swing in whatever direction they are pointing with no regard for the monsters position. you have to eat constantly or your max health and stamina are lowered. youll need to sharpen your weapon 3-5 times per fight. it all gets very tedious. the UI/hud is confusing as hell and the controls for a lot of stuff will have you on your mount, running from the monster while you fumble around in your item menu.

All in all, once the addictiveness or the armor and weapon forging wore off, my desire to play the game completely evaporated.

Don’t play this if you're expecting engaging combat that stays fun. While the animations and attack concepts are good, the execution is incredibly mediocre just like the Clutch Claw in Iceborne

From Low Rank to most of endgame, monsters are extremely easy to crowd control. Wounds and Focus Strikes are overabundant, making combat repetitive and braindead for about 90% of the game. Endgame "fixes" this by inflating monster stats, which does let monsters express themselves more but it still feels like you're a demi god slaughtering helpless bunnies because even with all these adjustments, hunters still feel overpowered with too many tools. It does feel less braindead... atleast.

Whether it's worth the high price is up to you. The game does some things well and some not:

Fashion / Character Creation: 10/10 (I LOVE this)
Soundtrack: 5/10 (I just rarely heard good tracks in this game but there are gems in it)
Locales / Areas: 6/10 
Characters: 3/10
Story: 3/10
Traversal Mechanics: 2/10 (Atleast in previous games I had a more intimate connection with the areas 
                                             around me thanks to this, not just about knowing where to go)

Quests (Outside Main Story / Events): 1/10
Sidequests: 10/10

After all this the game still has a crapton of microtransactions... sigh

100% achievements completed, and I still do not recommend it.

This is not a negative review from someone who barely touched the game. I finished the content, got all the achievements, and gave it more than enough time to convince me. In the end, it did the opposite.

The game is not without strengths. The combat still carries the core appeal of the series, and hunting can still feel satisfying in the moment. Visually, it also has strong presentation. But those strengths are not enough to make up for the overall disappointment.

The biggest issue is that the excitement fades far too quickly. The longer you play, the more the experience starts to feel like it is asking for your time without giving enough meaningful depth, challenge, or long-term satisfaction in return. Once the initial appeal wears off, the lack of staying power becomes much harder to ignore.

I got all the achievements because I like the series and wanted to see everything through, not because I believe this is a game I can confidently recommend to most players.

If you are a longtime fan, you may still find some enjoyment in the combat and the name alone. But if you are simply looking for a game worth fully investing in, I do not think this one justifies that commitment.

Getting 100% does not mean I recommend it. For me, it just means I gave it every chance possible, and it still fell short.

I've had Monster Hunter Wilds since its release. I own Monster Hunter World and Rise, as well as their respective DLCs. I put 450 hours into World, and about 100 into Rise. When I tell you this game has had many ups and downs, it's 100% in truth.
On release it was a hot mess to the point where I gave up on it half way through the story just due to the sheer unplayability. The monsters would completely jank out, the textures were broken everywhere, and the bugs were mountains.
As of where it stands now. Capcom has done a lot of work to fix Wilds. The monsters have much less "laggy" actions, and in most cases are very readable on what they're going to do. The environment no longer randomly turns into a pile of nintendo 64 textures. 
Beyond that however, Monster Hunter Wilds has issues in comparison to the previous two titles. When you compare Wilds to World, it has completely lost its identity as a "monster hunting" game. There is no "hunting", the autopathing takes you directly to your target, and the scoutflies know where the monster will go before it has even arrived there. When you compare Wilds to Rise, it lost the unique mechanics that were introduced that made every weapon feel customizable and unique to each player. Wilds ended up a strange middle ground where the combat overall is spammy. I have been an Insect Glaive main since it came out in Monster Hunter World, and Wilds out of the three titles to include it so far has a significantly spammy playstyle. Adding in the occasional wound pop like any other weapon, and you've got a pretty repetitive combat. I've also noticed in my recent time of hunting Gogmagzios and Arch Tempered Arkveld that the monsters themselves have extremely predictable move sets, most combats even following the same exact patterns including when they move to what locations. This itself is significantly different from the previous titles, monster fights always felt "fresh" where with the current highest tier fights I find myself feeling exhausted and frustrated when I have to fight them over and over instead of feeling like a proper "challenge" due to the level of repetition involved. 
Final note is, the story. Monster Hunter has never been the best story driven game out there, far more about the whole hunting the monster than learning about the characters. So I understand that this was a new thing for the franchise. However, the story is painful in the, sitting there waiting for the gameplay to happen kind of way. There's too many scenes where you sit there twiddling your thumbs waiting for dialogue to end, to do a 10-ish minute hunt, to go through another story segment longer than the hunt you just did. Capcom tried to make an impressive story, and honestly I think it did the exact opposite of its intended goal. It was too much of a slog, and too little gameplay or interactivity during it to make it memorable.
Overall, it's not a bad game, I would still recommend it to someone who has interest in the Monster Hunter series cautiously and likely to someone who has enjoyed previous titles, but I do genuinely have high hopes that the upcoming DLC will do rather significant changes to the game to make it feel as exciting as the previous two titles.
