[h1]Monster Hunter Wilds TU4.5 Review[/h1]

[b]TLDR:[/b] It has been a year since its release, and the game is great and now highly playable. There is so much content you can do in the game. Even if you are new to the series or a veteran try it out. If you played during launch and dropped it because of the performance issues give it a second chance because you might really like it now.

[hr][/hr]

[h2]Introduction & The State of the Game[/h2]
* [b]The Performance Turnaround:[/b] Launch performance was completely abysmal and left a horrible taste in many players mouths. It really should have been in a good state from the beginning. Thankfully after multiple optimization updates leading up to TU4.5 I can finally say the game runs smoothly and is very playable now.
* [b]Value:[/b] The content is so abundant it is almost overwhelming. No matter what price you buy this game at it is an absolute bang for your buck.

[h2]The Good: Combat, Monsters, and Story[/h2]
* [b]The MH Identity & Combat:[/b] The core gameplay has changed so much since MHP3rd but the overall combat is still as satisfying as ever. Optimized gameplay is buttery smooth and the combat is chef's kiss. It is a great evolution for the traditional MH Hack&Slash. Some veteran purists might prefer the original pacing and combo commitment but all I can say is try it and if it clicks then great.
* [b]Phenomenal Music:[/b] The music is phenomenal and fits every situation and environment. The music for each major monster is different and unique making it quite the spectacle.
* [b]Monster Roster & Story:[/b] The roster has amazing variety making it incredibly fun to fight new and returning monsters. The harder endgame bosses are just PEAK—wonderful cinematic and challenging fights blending set pieces and raw gameplay. There is more story now to progress the narrative and introduce the roster, and older fans will definitely love the characterization of returning characters.

[h2]Hunters, Gear, & QoL[/h2]
* [b]The Core Loop & Grind:[/b] The core loop of hunting monsters is satisfying and the endgame respects your time. You can avoid the RNG gear mechanics entirely and still clear the game. The base craftable Artian set and craftable charms are great upgrades if you do not want to farm for god roll talismans. Grinding for perfect stats is not for everyone but it is still a fun goal.
* [b]Equipment Freedom:[/b] There is so much cosplay and cool fashion available. It is a great blend of cool, cute, and silly/funny perfect for different tastes. Color customization is flexible and you are no longer restricted by gendered armor sets. If you want a fully armored gal or a sexy muscle man now you can.
* [b]Character Customization:[/b] The creator is incredibly detailed for making characters from fiction or reality. Even if you are not creative you can just search online for peoples character codes.
* [b]Support & QoL:[/b] You can have NPC support hunters help in battle and their carts do not count towards the quest failure. QoL changes make gameplay less tedious like easily reposting quests. Some might be divided on changes like Focus strike. Some love that you can adjust yourself during combos while others don't like it because it makes it easier to fight monsters.
* [b]Bonus Charm:[/b] The devs added small hidden things like the grandhub diva who gives a small buff after her performance or the secret bird's nest on the grandhub that gives you stuff from time to time.

[h2]The Bad: UI/UX, Network, & Nitpicks[/h2]
* [b]UI/UX Negative:[/b] The menus are confusing to navigate. It might be a skill issue in navigating the ancient forest on my part but it needs a touch up to be more digestible.
* [b]Cluttered Pop ups:[/b] Notification messages are messy. I want to know if I have a specific buff activated but it gets convoluted by notifications for things I don't care about right now. A filter would be great.
* [b]Default Controls:[/b] The default auto run for the Seikret and radial pop up for items feel clunky and need changing. Sometimes you do not even know there are much better settings because the UI/UX is convoluted, and I only knew about it because of youtube shorts on my feed. 
* [b]Investigation Quests:[/b] Great for saving world encounters but the limit of 50 is too small. Getting other players quests is pure RNG making it hard to get specific duo monster arena fights that you want to get. I hope we get a Custom Quest Maker.
* [b]Gathering and Delivery Quests:[/b] These shouldn't have been mostly removed as they gave players a breather to enjoy the environments. MHRise style event quests exploring the map would give more excuses to go straight from the Windward Plains to the Scarlet Forest without needing to fast travel.
* [b]Network Issues:[/b] I am not sure if it is because of crossplay or just server stability but I sometimes get disconnected suddenly leading to carts. We have decent internet and this doesn't happen in other games.
* [b]Missing Personal Rooms:[/b] I loved room customization in MHWorld and MHRise. Now it sucks that catching rare endemic life is just a number and a picture on my profile instead of something to display.

[h2]The Ugly: DLC & Monetization[/h2]
* [b]Overpriced DLC:[/b] Optional cosmetics are great but prices are a bit much. It also did not help that cosmetic DLC were released before the optimization updates which annoyed a lot of people.
* [b]Lore Behind Paywalls:[/b] Items like Alma's table give unique lore dialogue. It sucks that you have to pay to learn this lore. The basic version should have been free.
* [b]Character Edit Vouchers:[/b] Limiting edits to a maximum of 3 times and hiding further changes behind a paywall is very scummy. This should have been a completely free feature.

[hr][/hr]

[b]The Ultimate Verdict:[/b] Despite the issues and nitpicks I have this is a wonderful experience to pick up. Monster Hunter Wilds is a great game held back due to its rough performance and available content on release. The game was clearly rushed but as you can see with the constant updates the devs poured a lot of care and passion into this game. This is my biased view as a fan of this series since I was a kid, but I encourage you to try out the game. If you dropped it during launch because of the performance issues give it another chance to shine as you might just really enjoy it now.

I’ve been a fan of Monster Hunter since the very beginning, and I can honestly say that MH Wilds is very good. The improvements are noticeable compared to the first release. The gameplay feels much smoother now, and I’m not experiencing any lag or delays anymore. Great job on optimizing the performance. ⚔️

However, I noticed that the hammer weapon skins are a bit underwhelming. As a hammer main, it feels disappointing to see other weapons getting better and more detailed designs while the hammer skins seem less impressive. Would it be possible to create better hammer skins something similar in quality and style to the Shattering Greatsword and make them available as cosmetics? It would also be great if more hammer weapons could be added to the list in future updates.

I also have a few suggestions that I think could make the game even more exciting:

1. Guild House System
It would be amazing to have a guild house feature where the guild leader can purchase different house locations (such as Windward, Basin, etc.). Only the guild master, vice master, and officers should have permission to edit the main guild interior.

Additionally, each guild member could have their own personal room inside the guild house. Members could design their rooms however they want one could have a waterfall theme, another a desert theme, and so on. I think this would add more personality and make guilds feel more alive and interactive.

2. Large-Scale Hunt Mode (6–10 Hunters)
It would be exciting to add special quests designed for 6 to 10 hunters fighting a massive monster with better loot rewards. There could also be a competitive mode where teams compete based on how fast they defeat the monster. The top-ranked teams (Rank 1, 2, or 3) could receive exclusive rewards such as rare skins or special loot. This would encourage teamwork and add a competitive endgame activity.

3. Quality of Life Improvements
It might be more convenient to move the melding old woman to the Grand Hub so players can access her more quickly. The same goes for the NPC in Windward who brings the loot (I believe his name is Dogard). Having them closer together would reduce the need to travel back and forth repeatedly.

It would also be great to add mining spots for armor spheres and other materials, similar to how it worked in Monster Hunter World.

Overall, I really enjoy MH Wilds and appreciate the improvements so far. I’m just hoping to see more love for hammer users and some additional features that could make the game even more immersive and engaging.

I don’t think I’ve ever played a game that disrespects my time the way Monster Hunter Wilds does when it comes to RNG. I’ve hunted the same monster so many times I could draw its attack patterns from memory. I’ve broken every single part. I’ve captured it. I’ve carved perfectly. And somehow? STILL no drop.

How is it possible to do everything right and walk away with nothing but scales and disappointment? The “rare” materials don’t feel rare, they feel mythical. Like they don’t actually exist in the loot table and the game is just laughing at me behind the scenes.

You want that one gem? Cool. Be prepared to grind for hours. Days, even. Not because the hunt is challenging. Not because you’re undergeared. But because the drop system feels completely detached from effort. Skill doesn’t matter. Strategy doesn’t matter. Preparation doesn’t matter. You’re just spinning a slot machine after a 15-minute boss fight.

And don’t even get me started on getting duplicates of the same useless material over and over again. Oh great, my 27th plate that I absolutely do not need. Fantastic. Meanwhile, the ONE thing required for progression might as well be coded at 0.0001%.

The combat? Great. The monsters? Incredible. The world? Beautiful.
But the RNG? Absolutely brutal. It turns what should be an exciting loot moment into pure resentment.

At some point it stops feeling rewarding and starts feeling manipulative. Grinding should feel like progress. This just feels like punishment.

Fix the drop rates. Add some form of pity system. Respect players’ time.

Because right now? The real monster isn’t in the hunt. It’s the loot table.

Story: Welcome to the forbidden lands. Find this lost boy's parent and hunt each monster in ascending order of danger while we drag you from locale to locale- Um... You're not supposed to fall asleep during my explanation. (Absolute snoozefest)

Combat: You can stagger or down the monster by using a special move in a new focus mode! What did it cost? Everything got a little quality of life, but general movement and responsiveness went down as a result. If you're fighting multiple monsters, you're at their mercy and will be thrashed around and you will thank Capcom for this traumatic experience.

Performance: I'm having to rewrite this entire segment due to old stability issues resurfacing with a menace. I will put this bluntly so I don't need to rewrite this again, even if you have the top of the line, the threat looms of the gpu driver crashing. I have had a plethora of hunts end abruptly due to my GPU giving out leading to my vexing conclusion that the team behind the driver compatibility must have vibecoded it into being acceptable for the time of testing, only to be a liability later. Of course, this could be wrong, but it doesn't stop the game from throwing the gpu fail error up and wasting my investigation charges. Even on the lowest of settings, this game still manages to find a way to go "Oh oh! I can't wait another millisecond for this pixel to get rendered from this particle system! Time to ruin the experience!". When the game works, it can hold a stable framerate. A shame that it's at the cost of inexplicable crashing unique to this game.

Overall: If you want me to get the future expansion, don't expect to wow me with eye candy and sparkles, as all I'll see are more sources for GPU failures exclusive to the game. You can raise the ante all you want, but if it means the handler is nagging me about how to do the fight, I expect to be able to throw the handler in the fight and have them handle it in my stead. If you're gonna play this game, you'll have to look past the ugly bits and what you'll find is good. You'll need to enjoy it in bits and pieces and there's beauty and dopamine to be gotten in learning the combat (Especially the power clashes and parries). The sound design for ambience is very nice and sometimes it impresses me with how life-like nature looks that it makes me want to go outside.
I won't stop people from enjoying what the experience has to offer, though I would be remiss not to mention the bad.

Monster Hunter Wilds is not worth your time. Go play World. Or rise for extra charm and a faster pace. 

On launch, the performance was completely unacceptable. Textures not loading on low settings and models popping in as floating eyeballs during cutscenes. You could watch details load into the environment step by step during gameplay. Inexcusable. I'm told it's been improved significantly, in part by a patch that required players to manually update shader files in the file explorer.
Monster Hunter has always been weak on story (except 4U), but this is a joke. [Spoilers, I guess], but there's a kid whose entire culture is wiped out by a monster who then constantly places you in more and more danger, first by agitating said monster when multiple people warned him not to and with no plan to win or escape. Then you find out the monster didn't do any of the things the kid said but it is trying to kill you now and the kid stands in the way to defend it and almost gets you both killed again. The kid is wrong and spends all his time placing you in danger, but is also the main character and you're meant to care. I also find the concept of artificial life as described in this game completely uninteresting. [End Spoilers] The overall pace of the story is grueling. Walk and talks are constant and consistently uninteresting. Because you can gather instantaneously from range at full speed on a mount, I assume the walk and talks are meant to force you to smell the roses. I smelled way more roses spamming circle on a crack in the wall than I did listening to some guy (/girl/non-binary individual) spout off about how the ecology is actually really interesting instead of being encouraged to explore and discover all the little things the devs tried to put in an engine that can't handle them.
The gameplay is disappointing, to say the least. I appreciated the methodical pace of earlier Monster Hunter games and Wilds strays about as far from that rhythm as possible. Combat is fast and fluid to the point of becoming effortless and mindless. In the base game, there is nothing that can challenge the player in any way. The wounding system is significantly over tuned to the point that at any time during a fight, you have access to an instant knockdown button. Health values are low too, so a lot of fights feel like they just kind of end before they really even heat up.
Later title update monsters are more difficult. I fought seregios and lagiacrus and both were more challenging than anything up to that point, but neither got there in particularly exciting ways. Seregios felt weightless and the mandatory underwater section with lagiacrus is a pale imitation of 3U underwater combat (which I enjoy though most people do not). You can only really use your slinger to grapple onto lagiacrus, where he hits you no matter what move he does. The player doesn't really get to influence how the section plays out, when choice and personal style should be the thing that keeps you coming back to the same fights.
Speaking of grinding, there was none in the base game. Materials are so plentiful and gear costs are so low, that full armor sets and multiple weapons from any monster could be achieved in just a couple fights. Monster Hunter's gameplay loop has always been the grind and that simply doesn't start until endgame. It feels as if there is nothing to achieve or do until endgame.
I also strongly dislike the randomized artian and now gogmazios gear that are apparently the only gear that matters or is remotely difficult to obtain. There was a sense of purpose in fighting rathalos over and over to get one ruby and upgrade a sword, but the randomized rewards completely eliminate any relationship between the unique properties of monsters and the gear you use. Having a great poison weapon no longer requires mastering the fight with a poison monster and understanding what it's like to be on the other end of the stick. Now it just requires that you pull the slot machine 5 more times.
The minute to minute combat and the rewards for mastering that combat are completely alien to the core that I loved about this franchise. I've been told that archtempered monsters don't wound as easily and one shot you if your gear isn't at the endgame level, but I honestly can't be bothered to care. The reward is slot machine pulls, so why bother. The increased challenge is based on removing access to mechanics that were poorly implemented to begin with, but that the game itself is built around. The game got written into a corner and there's no way out that appeals to me.
I am aware that 64 hours of gameplay is actually a decent amount of time, but I'm telling you, as a 10+ year veteran, I only finished the game out of obligation to my favorite media franchise of all time. I gave the story a chance and listened to the characters. I was bored with hands off the controller for 20 hours. The rest was an unsatisfying substitution for something I should've loved. And for context, I have 200 hours from world and rise each, on steam, not to mention 373 hours on base rise on the switch and 777 hours on ps4 world. I bought both of those games multiple times despite thinking they were already losing some of the old charm and I played world for more than 10 times as long as wilds. The difference is that I could still boot up world and have goals to accomplish, but there's nothing at all pulling me back towards wilds. 64 hours, from someone who was willing to make a new MH game my only hobby for months on end, is an indictment. If you still want more context, I played 4U on the 3ds for 750 hours and 3U on the 3ds for 1000 (+ some wii u hours, but who's counting) and I'm about to boot it up after sending this review. If you're hardcore, my secret alternate recommendation for a better Monster Hunter game is 4U, it's the only game in the franchise with a story that's actually well written and makes a salient point about ecology, namely that rabies exists. That game also maintains the methodical pace I love with a lot of qol that previous titles lacked or honestly spit on. If it was on steam, I would go blue in the face telling you to pay any price for it.
There are some upsides, though. I appreciate the ability to use gendered armor sets on either masculine or feminine character models. Some pieces are a little too bulky or skimpy for my taste, so the flexibility is greatly appreciated. I was also very excited in the leadup to the game's release that they included some real freaks in the roster. The cephalapods are rad designs and some of the other monsters have a sort of alien feel that lets me at least keep a candle lit for the creature designers on the team. There's a dragon with fur, a viking helmet, and chains on his arms, a raptor/mosquito thing covered in air sacs, and a chicken that's just wrinkly and allegedly spanish. Hope the franchise doesn't die its seemingly deserved death before they come back in a title that's actually fun.

MHW is the red-headed stepchild of the recent entries in the Monster Hunter universe. It's attempted to take most of the highlights from the previous games and mend in into some form of a cohesive concept. However, it ends up resulting in a garbled mess that struggles to find a new identity whilst rewarding challenges in any form that makes progression feel warranted or unique. This is not a new issue with the Monster Hunter games, however the traditional MH sense of evolution feels all over the place in Wilds; making some areas (gem hunting is the most obvious with the new investigations) a joke to access while the classic RNG aspects of creating a perfect set have been relegated to a hodgepodge quest hopping for useless jewels and imperfect talismans galore.

In addition, this game only recently found its sea legs in terms of optimization. Most of my friends who played early on, some new to this series, gave up entirely because of how awful this game ran. It took the game devs over a year to address these concerns, but I suppose it's better late than never. This improvement largely came from the massive outlash from fans and newcomers alike, and they've tried to remedy this issue with new monsters and event quests.

With this new addition is Gogmazios. Every MH has "that" hunt which makes or breaks the game. It was Fatalis in Worlds, and Primal Malezeno or Risen Valstrax in Rise. There's even an argument for Behemoth in Worlds before master rank dropped. Wilds fumbles horribly here by making EVERY OPTIMIZED BUILD reliant on fighting Gogmazios. Unlike any of the previous games, you can now craft "Artian" weapons for every weapon in the game. That is to say, you can generally skip out on the massive weapon tree and its large grocery list of required hunts by inevitably heading towards a one stop shop craft for generic looking weapons. There is no reason to fight or hunt monsters for their weapon parts directly. It all goes into a forge and turned into oralbullshite before being consumed by Gemma for a milquetoast advancement of RNG riddled upgrades. It reminds me of the odd parts of an MMO's weapon progression, and it just feels so off especially when THIS PARTICULAR FIGHT is so damn obnoxious and seemingly required if you want to attack any of the big bad evil monsters within a normal time frame. 

Ultimately, this identity crisis leads to a subpar story (they all are bad but this one annoyed the hell out of me with the loud kids), some decent combat, great hunt variety (arguably the best for where its at by comparison to other games), okay weapons, sub-par layered armor, and a very confusing and non-productive gameplay loop which was once the meat and potatoes of "grinding" in these games. The monster parts just don't have as much necessity, which will be doubly true once master rank comes out. Factoring in that it's taken over a year for it to finally get some form of advancement beyond the Arkveld armor set, I'm left scratching my head with what they wanted to do here. They expanded on guiding lands  from World in a big way, but without making the environments worth exploring. They added a two weapon inclusion for each quest start, but there is currently no rhyme or reason for why you'd want to switch between weapons at all. The jewels don't carry over from one weapon to the other, so again it just feels like some small QoL instead of THE mechanic which this game focused on (as compared to wirebug or slinger/mantle customizations). 

Personally, I'd wait even further if you're on the fence. The story progression is ass and I'd - at this point - probably recommend Rise over this one just because its arcade combat felt so much more rewarding. The Great Sword feels amazing in this game if you're a sucker for unga bunga big slams. MHW is ultimately off kilter, and a weird entry that makes me question why they went so hard in some places and completely missed the mark elsewhere. Fun if you can play it with some friends, but the late game is not for casuals, or certain builds, at all; even more so than some of the other entries.

Gameplay is absolute top tier, SSS rank quality.

with 14 weapons, you are bound to find one that makes this game ultra fun to play for you.

3D ENGINE: it's the RE engine, It works well, and scales well. I ran the game on a 6thread cpu with a 2080MaxQ, at 1080p. lowest settings. It's playable; but you do need a stable 60fps to enjoy the game fully.  There were at launch some issues, (I never felt them on my battlestation rig) but there were. A later patch added some settings to ease some very specific power hungry features (say the number of small animals roaming around for exemple) and those have helped a lot for people with rigs on the lower end of the requirements.
As a gamer with a powerful rig, i cannot really say anything other then this: even at 720p lowest setting, the gameplay is so fun the gameloop so addictive,  that the game is worth it. do lower you res if you have to, the game is worth it.

STORY: servicable, nice enough if you wanna pay attention to it, with a very nice payoff near the end of the MSQ. can be completely skipped and ignored if you just wanna go fight monsters, your loss imo, but enjoying the gameplay and gameloop do not require the story. The story does enhance the fun, for me. ymmv.

CHARACTERS: they're fine, the game is not woke infested, it's body type A & B, and there is a character male who seems very feminine, but it's just a trait, not a plot point or whatever. it's fine.
woke filter: a tiny bit (body a&b) but it's fine.

SOUND FX/ANIMATION: the weapons sound fx in this game are absolutely insanely good and paired with
the animations of the weapons, landing a blow, on  a monster...  feels.  it just feels. You feel it when your weapon hits your target. There is no other way to describe this: the combination of soundfx and weapon animation in this game is absolute top tier. on par with say Demon's soul. but nothing like a souls game,

MONSTER ANIMATION: again, top tier in its category. The ecosystem feels alive, the verisimilitude is perfectly achieved. when you are in that game, you are in that world, everything reinforce the escape and dive into that universe. it's very immersive.

MUSIC: I own the soundtrack on CD (6cd boxed set). It's fantastic. within the game it fits perfectly. outside of the game, because of your experiences within the game, listening to the music oustside of the game takes you back to those moments, and it's nice. Listening to the music can be very uplifting. Some tracks are on my gym playlist.

MULTIPLAYER:
the community is non-toxic. If you die in a quest, veterans will take the time to point to you things you can improove (gameplay or gear), and will offer to help you out.
the multiplayer gameplay is very satisfying: answering a distress call to help others, or have others answer your distress call feels is nice. 
The hub in this 6th mainline game has been revamped and there is something lost in the upgrade: kinda like the looking for raid option in wow destroyed one aspect of the community, the new hub with dozens of people did the same in monster hunter. Meaning in the 5th mainline game (MH World/Iceborn), I made friends while hunting, we built a clan and often played together. In the 6th mainline game (this one MH Wilds) this does not happen as much..
So gameplay wise multiplayer is good to fantastic ; but community wise it's a regression from Worlds.
IF new player, you'll find it just fine, if veteran player you'll find it a bit frustrating, but not a hindrance.

INGAME STORE, MICROTRANSACTIONS
They are PERFECTLY done. monster hunter is game as a live service done right. All mtx are cosmetics (visuals or music) none of the mtx impact gameplay, there are no boosters, nothing like that. There are no loot boxes. should you buy something from the ingame store, it will be because you want it, you choose it. no random stuff.
The live service aspect is: seasonal quests, new quests and challenges, collaborations with other (capcom, but sometime else) franchises.
Once the cycle has ended it repeats, and at end of life, all quests are made avaiable. No fomo.


OVERALL:
At full price, the game is worth its price.
And if you know you enjoy this type of game/gameplay or gameloop then the game becomes a must buy.

on sale, the game will be very much worth its price.

There are plenty of youtube videos of people having fun with the game, go watch a few, with hunters using different weapons. see if one weapon appeals to you. if you think it does, then you'll most probably have huge fun learning the weapon and hunting monsters.


ONE LAST NOTE:
Monster hunter is a a roughly 20 years old franchise. In this franchise this is the 6th mainline game. The formula for fun has been crafted for 20 years, honed, perfected, tailored. it works, it's fun. it feels real nice. 

ABOUT ME: me I'm a fiver. Meaning I came into the franchise at monster hunter world (5th mainline game 2017).
I saw the announcement trailer, enjoyed it without knowing anything about the franchise. I looked up some videos saw a weapon that interrested me, and decided to try it out.
I have since accumulated well over 5000 hours (mainly on Playstation so does not appear here on my steam account).
I am still enjoying myself, still discovering new stuff, new adrenaline rush, new setbacks, new losses, and new wins...
and I'm still asking for more ^_^

With the recent performance update playing the game has become a much improved experience in terms of quality. It rarely crashes and overall runs much more smoothly than it used to. I'm able to have the game set so that it looks beautiful without frying my PC and having the framerate drop. Great job here!

It's clear that a lot of work and love has been put into this game and it's definitely a nice introduction to people who are new to the series (for example the many improvements in quality of life mechanics are great). Wilds is for sure it's own unique experience (like every Monster Hunter game, really) but personally I've had a ton of fun with this one and I would say it's one of the best ones in the series, especially with the amount of diverse environments and mosters presented as well as the amazing soundtrack that accompanies all of this. 

The story to me was mostly satisfying, though I wish it was a bit longer and more fleshed out in some parts. I was immensely happy with the expansion of the lore and I can't wait to see more of that in a future DLC. The characters in the story are all interesting and unique but once again I wish they had just a little bit more depth to them (though I must admit not all fans of the series care much about the story and npcs that much and that's okay! everyone likes the games for different things after all). I have to say that Wilds' plot to me had a similar vibe as MH4 (which is one of my favorites in the series) so I see that as a great accomplishment of this game!

Some people might say that Wilds is "easier than the previous games in the series" but personally I am satisfied with the difficulty here and I'm glad that I do not have to do the same type of unforgiving grind that was required in some of the older games (especially when playing them solo). I wouldn't say this game is "too easy" since with the new updates and event quests there are definitely some really good challenges. I would say it's just lost that frustrating grind part, which to me is a big improvement. 

Overall this game has a huge offer of things to do and I'm sure most people will be able to find something they enjoy, wheter it's newcomers or long time fans.

I am really disappointed to see this ongoing tendency in the game dev industry, because, to be honest, most of the triple-A titles are not looking better graphics-wise and have ridiculous hardware requirements, and the same problem is related to this game. It is still not optimized enough, and the most interesting thing is that the graphics in this game is not looking better than what Cyberpunk or Elden Ring can offer with their recommended hardware requirements.

Every update has been so underwhelming. I'm happy the game is more stable for those who hadn't been able to paly it, but the issues with the game are at its core. They learned the wrong lessons from World and doubled down on what made it bad.

It's harder than ever to play with your friends, as they continue to shove a story no one cares about down your throat. There is so much railroading that no one know how to play the game anymore. The main story is so easy that when you go online, chances are someone hasn't upgraded their gear at all and gets one-shot by every monster. And I don't blame them, because there are so many messages and explosions going out at the same time that I'm not surprised players get overwhelmed and just stand in the path of incoming death.

But despite the living ecosystem being World's main draw, this game feels the most artificial it's ever been. Every hunt will at some point have a scripted portion where the monster runs under a hanging rock, or a tree, or some other kind of trap. This along with the wound system, telling the player "Attack now" makes it feel like the game is playing you instead. This was a problem in World too, where the maps were so big that you didn't need to learn anything about them, the scoutflies always told you where to go. Every zone was a forgettable wasteland. But here, is it so much worse. Now your mount automatically takes you anywhere you need to go. The maps in Wilds are so unnecessarily large and full of special paths that only mounts can go through, that half of the gameplay is just watching a cutscene. Everything feels scripted and fake. Monster Hunter Rise solved some of these issues perfectly, with smaller maps and mounts that only you can control. I can't for the life of my imagine why they made them worse.

This game is really bad.

I loved Monster Hunter World. It's been a while since I played it so I'm not sure if Wilds took huge steps backwards or if my expectations for games has just gotten a lot higher since then.

This game is infuriatingly unresponsive and inconsistent with inputs. Sometimes you press an input and it goes off first try. Sometimes it doesn't go off until your 19th button press. The UI and inputs are all sooo clunky and laggy and unresponsive it is mind boggling. Did the developers even play their own game at all? I'd love to have one watching me play for even 5 minutes so I could ask them 37 times what the f* is going on between me pressing my button and the game just doing nothing or how I need to go through 5 different animations just to jump from one state to the next or 5 different menus just to swap an item from my mount to my pouch. 

The game is not setup for skill-based combat which is a shocker since this genre (souls-like action-rpg) is loved by its demographic specifically because of the skill-based combat. Fights are not setup to challenge you in any meaningful way. The damage you take over half the time is just random BS that the player really had no reasonable way to skill-check. To account for that fact, the devs decided to make everything trivially easy. Monsters do basically no damage to the player and have almost no HP. So you just close your eyes and mash your buttons and get hit by anything you want because none of it matters and you kill the monster anyways.

There are mods that you can install to increase monster HP and the damage they deal to you which is a good idea in theory, but then you just come back to the problem of half the monster attacks being totally random BS that you aren't able to see coming. It's not a skill-check it's just you taking damage randomly so if you use a mod to crank the difficulty up, you'll just get one-shot randomly.

I play these types of games for the high skill-based challenge, but Monster Hunter Wilds completely ignores players who come to the genre for that purpose. Which is strange since that is the core demographic of people playing games in this genre. Disappointing.

I'm going to keep playing it on the off chance it gets better (simply because I'm currently waiting for some new games to release soon), but i am not optimistic.

Swayed by the comments about improved stability and performance, I decided to give the game a go and I must say that I was not disappointed. Capcom sure took their sweet time optimizing the game, I am glad I waited for a year before buying it, especially considering the massive sale going on right now

However, if you are looking to buy the game, I´ll shortly brief you why you should/shouldn´t buy it. I am neither a newbie nor a veteran to the MH series, with several hundred hours in total on MH games I´ve played

First, the good

Story - Unlike MHW, which was the last MH game I´ve played, I must say that they actually took a lot of effort of delivering a nice story this time around. Sure, it´s still going around slaying monsters, but this game has animated cutscenes, voice acting, your character actually talks, as does your Palico. Not exactly story of the year material, but it is good and a massive improvement over MHW

Visuals - Much improved over MHW. The game looks stunning. It is very pretty and I love its visuals. Sure, visuals aren´t everything, but I have yet to see a game this pretty. It truly looks wonderful

Gameplay - IMO much more fluid AND much more quicker than MHW. Good ol´ MHW was already throwing monsters with massive HP pools during the mid-end game, so hunts took wayyy longer compared to Wilds. I averaged most hunts under 10 minutes, with a couple of exceptions that were closer to 15, mostly the final bosses. Most weapons got new moves to play with and while I haven´t mastered even my main weapon (Bow), I can say that it is much more fun and makes for an entertaining experience all around. I also like that resources for crafting are much more common and plentiful, armor spheres and gems and pendants are much easier to get, not to mention that the game gives you a lot more resources from each hunt, meaning that you no longer have to slay a monster 10+ times like some in MHW to get their full set. I usually only needed to kill each monster one to three times to get everything from their tree, which when combined with faster hunts means that the game doesn´t feel grindy and respects your time more than MHW did. At no point did I feel pressured to make a supply run every couple of hunts, I simply grabbed what was in my general direction and that was enough. Try it for yourself, you´ll see

Music - Was alright. I think MHW had a few more memorable tracks, but it was good nonetheless

Performance - As of 24.2.26 when I´m writing this, no issues whatsoever. No crashes. No lag spikes. No fps drops. Sometimes the character doesn´t react to my commands from the radial menu, but I am not sure if that´s on the game or if I´m doing something wrong. I was playing on RTX 3060 with high setting and had plenty of memory to spare. The game shows you how much of your card is used depending on how you set things up, so even if you play on lower-end pc with something like 1650 or 2060, you can still likely play it on low-mid settings. The minimal/recommended settings seem like a tall order and I was also questioning whether playing it on my pc would make for an enjoyable experience, given this game´s reputation for almost a year, but they truly did improve it and you no longer need a 3k dollar setup to have an enjoyable experience

Finally, the bad

The only bad thing I can say about this game is its length. As of writing this, I have finished the main story. Yes, I have not played the epilogue/high rank, but I finished what the game presents to be the main story. If you will stay anyway and will grind monsters until the next game comes out, it doesn´t really matter. If you are someone like me who puts in 50 to 100 hours and then moves onto another game and you see that the story ended just before the 20 hour mark, you might want to wait for a big sale before buying it. 70 dollars for 20-30 hours ain´t horrible nor great since most of HR is pretty much just - "Yeah, this monster, but stronger, drops better loot"

All in all, a 9/10 from me. It improved on all aspects, was very fun, the only critique I have is that there ain´t more of it. I wouldn´t spend more than 40 bucks on it, it´s great but I dislike how pricey the games got last couple of years. 110 bucks for the premium edition is just kekw for me

Great game with fantastic art direction. The gameplay is, though fun, not what I enjoy Monster Hunter for. A shift to timing-focused mechanics and focus mode itself both simultaneously take away emphasis on positioning. In most other games' hunts, the solution to avoiding damage was to simply get out of the way, which I found more immersive than parrying the creature. This gameplay shift is not complete, as that would completely remove Monster Hunter's unique traits of animation commitment paired with positional knowledge, which means that monster tracking isn't like souls tracking; it follows you with an attack, but not to a fuller extent. This means you get to dodge the attack through positioning usually, but the monster can also miss you if you are preparing or executing a perfect-timing mechanic and the aftermath or followup to an attack will hit after you're left an overcommitted sitting duck.
The game offers good monster variety to fight and they all have unique designs and ecological explanations. In all aspects other than the combat and occasional hiccup, the game is as immersive as it can get.
As of Febrary 18th, the game has been running pretty consistently at 60 FPS without framegen with a 3070 TI, so it has finally met my expectations. Now is a good time to play.

as much as I want to like the game, I cannot in good faith recommend it. This is for a litany of issues that I will detail.

PERFORMANCE: The game performs much worse than it should - even after the latest round of performance updates on 02/18/2026. I have a 9070XT, 9800X3D, and 64GB of 6400MHz RAM. I should not be having frame drops, inconsistent frame pacing, freezes, stutters and every other issue under the sun with my specs; but Wilds makes it so.


 GAMEPLAY: Until this latest round of Gogmagios and 10* monsters being added (lol), the game was piss easy. Focus mode trivializes everything in low rank and most of high rank, with the exception of 8*s and above that they added post launch because of how stupid focus mode and wounds you can inflict are.The issues that have arisen from focus mode being so utterly broken have led to monsters already having more HP than 4P Fatalis from Iceborne. It is a stark downgrade from rise and world to attempt to appeal more people, and it has utterly rejected me and many others who have enjoyed Monster Hunter for years prior.

Do not buy this game. Play Rise+Sunbreak or World+Iceborne, or go play one of the old world games like 4U if you have access to a 3DS or an emulator.

I’ve played a lot of Monster Hunter, but Wilds hits different.

The world feels alive in a way the series has never done before. The weather shifts mid-hunt, monsters move naturally across huge open zones, and everything flows seamlessly without breaking immersion. It doesn’t feel like “maps” anymore — it feels like an ecosystem.

Combat is still heavy and satisfying, but smoother. Every hit has weight. Every dodge matters. When a storm rolls in and you’re mid-fight with a massive monster, it genuinely feels cinematic — like you’re inside a next-gen boss battle, not just grinding for parts.

Performance-wise, on a strong PC, it’s insane. The lighting, scale, and particle effects make hunts look AAA-level every single time.

For me, this isn’t just another sequel.

Rating: 9.5/10 — Easily one of the most immersive hunts I’ve ever experienced.
It feels like the future of Monster Hunter.

Monster Hunter Wilds finally fixes the performance problems that showed up early on, and now the game runs the way it always should have. Capcom also smoothed out a lot of the old, clunky mechanics, making the whole experience easier to get into and way less of a hassle. But that big push toward accessibility definitely changes the vibe—longtime fans who loved the tougher, more demanding feel of classic Monster Hunter might feel like things have been softened a bit too much in an effort to appeal to a wider audience. It’s still a fun ride, just not quite the old-school flavor some hunters were hoping for.

My only performance issue was when I'm in the Hub or Base Camp, as there's too many NPC and things to render than my CPU can handle so I was getting lower frames than I would have when I'm hunting in the field. But that's on me, not the game. Game definitely runs flawless when hunting. Getting above 100 FPS too.

I'd be damn if I said this game's iteration of Dual Blades is not my favorite. Coming from Rise, I absolutely loved the idea of having your character spin around and above the monsters, but what I despised was the how easy the fights were, you can spam Spiral Slash 10+ in 1 hunt, in which if you have a god set with Berserk Lv. 2 charm, you'd be bored. I love big numbers for damage, but it's definitely over-tuned with that iteration of a weapon. And being limited by a Wirebug to do a counter was not my taste, but hey Risebreak is all about utilizing the Wirebugs.

But with this? What do you mean my character can counter pretty much anything, and is limited by Stamina. And I love that they made the Beyblade move official, but definitely not over-tuned. You can definitely feel that they thought about how to make Dual Blades more fluid but not getting constrained by 1 move that out-damage all the rest.

New combos, new moves you can connect more. Definitely 10/10 with this game.

I love Monster. I grew up playing 3u, 4u, Gen, Gen ultimate, World, and Rise. Every single game managed to, in some way, improve on the last entry. Wilds is the first time in this franchise that everything has taken a massive leap back. There are less monsters than ever before, there are less areas than ever before, there's less interesting builds than ever before, there is more clutter and hotbar ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ than ever before, the UI/UX experience is the WORST I have ever seen in a videogame, the multiplayer experience is atrocious, the lobby experience is atrocious, the cooking is the worst its ever been in the series, the story (which was already bad) IS EVEN WORSE, The map design is so awful that they give you AUTOPILOT to navigate it for you, the areas are grey colorless slop half of the time, the weather gimmick that this game was marketed with only plays a role in THREE OF THE FIVE AREAS, There's no tracking, there's no hunt, there's no fun traversal gimmick because the Seikret just does it for you, you are the strongest hunter in the series because you are never, ever punished, and the game LOOKS AND RUNS LIKE FECES.
The ONLY way this game runs at anything above 30 is if you turn on framegen. You HAVE to use framegen, because even if you set every graphical option to the lowest setting possible, it has no effect on the game's performance.
AND IT COSTS SEVENTY DOLLARS.
AND IT HAS 500 DOLLARS WORTH OF ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ SKINS AND STICKERS.
Words cannot express how frustrating it is to try and play this game with a friend. Between the moronic lobby system, the "link party" nonsense, and the inability to play quests because you have to talk to some random NPC makes playing the game together a nightmare of checklists and google searches, and once you finally get all of the quest requirements, you sit through the stupid cutscene, you leave the quest and return to camp so you can join your friend's quest, you can't do this immediately because the game needs to tell you that you need to claim your log-in rewards and there are unread chat messages (It's a sticker someone posted in local chat and it says the word penis), so after a nightmare of menus you finally join the quest, sit through the loading screen, you walk out of the camp, AND THE GAME ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ CRASHES. 
There are some positives to this game. The way monsters interact with the environment is cool. Monsters fighting is cool. While grossly underutilized, herd monsters like Doshaguma and Hirabami are interesting when they can actually utilize the mechanic, but the alpha Doshaguma is pretty much limited to a heavily scripted fight and Hirabami are very easily separated and rarely coordinate their attacks. Xu Wu and Nu Udra are the most unique monsters the series has ever seen, and are engaging, interesting, and entertaining fights as a result. Arkveld is cool. Offset attacks are a cool feature that I hope returns in future entries. The new monsters in this game all have underutilized/ unique skeletons. Weapon swapping is the only part of the Seikret I will miss. Zoh Shia was so cool that the game launched without his armor set and you could only fight him once. 
Every MH game is a worthwhile purchase. I purchased every game, every ultimate, every DLC without a second thought.
Wilds is a day-one purchase I regret to this day, and I will not be buying the MR DLC.

I waited until a few patches (and a hefty sale) to get this, and I'm glad I did. Because now I can give this a positive rating!

After over 1000 hours in MH:World, I'm finding Wilds a fun experience for the most part. Visually, a side-grade.
Things seem a bit easier than they were in World, but the combat is a definite upgrade.

I was a bow main in World, and the bow feels even better than before with the additional mobility we have in Wilds. You also don't need Crit Ele any more, so you're not locked into the ugly Rath set for half the game. We have a much better jump attack, and popping wounds is a fun (albeit OP) addition. DP-spam is finally a valid strat. Bows are in a fairly decent spot.

Though, FYI: if you're playing multiplayer as a bow user, maybe don't go quite so wound-pop crazy, as other weapon types need wounds to build combos.

Anyhoo....
Performance is fine.
Story is, ya know... usual MH story-level.
I don't like how the camera constantly robs you of control (it does this frequently), but I think that's just during the main campaign. Minor complaint either way.

All in all, it's pretty fun so far!


Edit: end-game is brutal, man. That difficulty curve is quite something!

After the recent performance patch the game runs very smooth with a few occasional crashes. I recommend trying it out, because it's a very pleasant experience now that the game runs smoothly.

As for the game itself, it's Monster Hunter, you probably know what you're getting into. If you're a newcomer however, this is by far the most accessible entry in the series. The sheer amount of quality of life additions make it so new players can have an easier time getting familiar with the game while the veterans can enjoy things that were lacking in the previous games. The story is very well written, albeit quite short. Overall this is one of the better games in the whole series.

Not recommended purely because I think there are better ways to spend your money than this.
If you're new to monster hunter and want to try the series, go ahead check out MH World or MH Rise.
The worst thing about Wilds is the bad, bad main quest. The story is exhaustingly simplistic, but that does not stop the game from serving it to you over and over. I felt like I spent 50% of my playtime wathing mid cutscenes and riding on auto pilot listening to the one-note characters shout their catch-phrases.
This shallowness is likewise reflected in the gameplay experience, with boringly easy monsters and gameplay systems so automatic and streamlined, that I wonder why the gathering and non fast-travel wasn't removed. The  sidequests are few and simplistic, so you effectively have to sit through the boring storyline sequentially with little agency, with NPCs frequently screaming at you "I THINK WE SHOULD GO THIS WAY" and turning your character around, even if you're in the camp area and think you can do something else.

The game is not a horrible experience, the environments are quite stunning and the way the monsters interact with the environmental effects is pretty impressive, as well as the quality of combat animations.
Thankfully the game does open up in the Hunter-Rank, effectively the post game, with new, harder monsters and freedom to choose your assignments.

It's not a horrible game, but I think it's just not that great of an (expensive) purchase. I pretty much recommend this only to to people who've already played World or other games in the series and are simply hungry for more monster hunting. Just keep in mind that it'll take you ~13 hours to get through the slog of the Low-Rank storyline.

I honestly think with all the optimization updates and the completion of base game, Wilds is absolutely a fun time. I found I'm actually enjoying it more than World's base game, and when I tried going back to it, I missed my seikret so much. I'm really hoping the expansion lives up to the hype.

Despite my praises, this is definitely a game you're going to want to buy on sale. If you manage that, it's definitely worth it if your PC can run it.

In general I'm a huge fan of Monster Hunter games but after 11 hours of game time I've lost interest in this one because 2 things:
>It's too easy (I've been killing 90% of monsters in under 10 mins without carting, the other 10% in <15 mins so far only 3 carted on the Octopus and then killed him half an hour later)
>There are too many cutscenes.
These 2 facts combined meant I was spending most of my times in these gaming sessions watching cutscenes/dialogue and that's not why I play these games. I suspect it might get better in the late game where fights are hopefully harder and I feel a need to craft better equipment but right now it's not fun and I cba.

I love this game, I really do. However, despite multiple stability changes, the game STILL crashes anywhere from 5 to 50 minutes in with no discernable cause or trigger, killing ANY progress made in that time and often requiring me to restart my whole computer to get it to work again. I don't know how long or how many updates it'll take, but I genuinely cannot recommend this game on PC even if you have a overpowered system like mine because it just. crashes.

When the time comes that I can get like a 2 hour session in without the game crashing, I am happy to change my review to positive. Until then, please go play it on console if you can, it crashes once in a blue moon on xbox at least, and I have like 200 hours on there so I can personally attest to it.

Just for reference, I'm running a i9-12900KF 6700XT 32gb DDR5 system. There is no reason a computer with double the performance as my Series X should be crashing this often on ONLY this game.

After playing the game for a year since launch, I can safely say that this is a great Monster Hunter game. It is a mix of World and Rise. Lots of mobility and QoL changes. The new wounding system is a neat addition and you can still get away with no using it at all. Compared to the other games, the story is more indepth. So take account that there will be more cutscenes that what past games presented. 

Now I am not blind to the technically issues that plagued the game. I suppose I consider myself lucky as the only issues I ran into were the weird triangles and shadows appearing in my game. After a month those issues vanished. The so called "the more DLC you have, the better your game runs" is also false. As I had none of those issues and I didn't the big DLC pack until Jan. From what I have heard, the recent patch that came close to the 1st anniversary solved a lot of technically issues for PC players. 

So if you're a veteran or a new player, Monster Hunter Wilds is a great addition to the series. Just have fun!

I haven't downloaded any mods. I prefer to play games as they are, and now I can't find my save file anywhere.

Why would uninstalling the game delete my save files? It makes no sense. If I could refund the game, I would. There was never any warning about my save files not being backed up, transferred, or permanently deleted. It just gave me a notice that the game would run better on my SSD.

With the final AT monster out and the game going into stand-by and focus going towards the expansion; I can review atleast the base game.

TLDR; Atleast it performs great now, but it is not my favorite MH, but base game Monster Hunter games rarely are. Not even World was that great at the start, there was just alot more hype behind it so it felt like you were apart of a community.

And I think that's where Wilds ultimately falls short for me, the game was initially designed with the idea of going out into the WILD, so the game didn't have things like the Gathering Hub at the start and the premise was supposed to be a more out in the wilds surviving-game, but as development progressed I think even the devs realized that the engine just couldn't handle whatever they wanted to introduce, that's my feeling atleast.

And with updates they had to refocus the game back to the old systems of going into quests from a quest counter instead of hunting investigations in the wild and all of that was at a pretty big cost.

The removal of player hosted lobbies AND limiting how many players you CAN see at any given time at a lobby has made the game incredibly hollow. There is no feeling of a community almost anywhere unless you specifically search for it via Twitch Streams, Discord groups or make your own Squad and hope people discover it.

And to me Monster Hunter has always been at it's best when you have a feeling of a community, you are able to host custom lobbies where there's specific goals in mind. You can't do that in Wilds because every lobby is a bunch of random people doing a bunch of random things, so it starts to feel more like a solo experience.

Now, I am glad for the fact that I have a pretty tight friend group who loves Monster Hunter, so I was able to experience that community driven feeling through them, but as time passes and people's interests on the game wanes, so does my interest to play the game more actively, even though I would love nothing more than meet new people online and help out players in specific needs, but you can't really do that in Wilds. In a sense the game feels like it's the most isolating MH games to-date, despite it having lobbies that hold 100 people.

As for the games design, as I said they really designed the game to be more of a survival game at some point in development, this shows in the pop-up camp system being largely phased out currently with updates making them not only more robust, but for you to have more of them available. Pop-up camps were a relic of a system for a game that was never there, it would have made sense in a more open-worldly game, but as I said that clearly became a limiting factor.

I still do not like that they've phased out the need to actually keep track of monsters, I just can't get behind the idea of the hunter magically knowing constantly where the monster is. I disliked it in Rise, I dislike it here. The paintballs are in Wilds, but they really should have been for tracking, not pinging.

Gameplay is solid 9/10. I think Focus Mode is a mixed bag where alot of weapons either HAVE TO use it or suffer a huge DPS loss and on other weapons focus mode just allows purely more mobility for like 360movement during long windup animations and it really is poorly balanced, not only in terms of weapons, but in terms of what type of controls you use. Focus Mode has a very clear advantage on certain aspects; if you use mouse and keyboard as opposed to a controller, where it's really hard to do something on a controller and incredibly easy on a KB.

Wound mechanic is largely a bad game design, not necessarily because monsters get wounds, but because the wounds unlock weapons true potential. Certain weapons like Charge Blade, Insect Glaive, Hunting Horn just lean too heavily on wound breaks being their source of gauge fill or unlocking weapons unique playstyle more consistently. I was afraid of this during the beta and it's still in the game, and it just  ruins alot of the fun when you're just wound hunting to keep Pizza slicer up or something similar. The only thing Wounds needed to be were spots on monsters where the player dfidn't need Weakness Exploit to do big damage. But here we are...

In the end, the game has more potential than any other base game MH todate. That is a given, not only is the end-game of Wilds incredibly deep and you actually have to do some math if you want to figure out the best sets for yourself and not rely on online guides or community META sets.

So I am excited for the expansion, my only hope is that they refocus some of the community stuff into the game and one way to do that is to allow players to host lobbies with actual targets like World&Iceborne had. The endgame of the expansion is going to be out of this world insane, because we are already getting to the levels that Sunbreak had at end-game, not quite, but it's close, so I am hopeful.

In a perfect world all players would get housing back and housing could be used as a player made lobbies for 8-16players at a time and lobby host could allow all players to show their own house designs if they want to. That'd be amazing.

That being said, if we're talking about a normal PC port, the game went from complete utter garbage optimization to overall a bad one.

It's STILL very CPU demanding, 
STILL has huge frame drops in areas where nothing is happening,
STILL have bad texture streaming,
STILL need to play with settings to have constant frames, despite the game looking horrible with these same changes.

I do commend Capcom for trying to fix these problems, but fundamentally these will NEVER be truly fixed because REengine is amazing for linear games but AWFUL for open world ones. Dragons Dogma 2 died because of this.

If you care about visuals and have a 40series below card, I'd say don't buy it.

Note: this is more of a "mixed" review than a "no", but steam reviews does not have a "mixed" option.

Tl:dr; Game was mid. Devs were and are making updates. Game is now good (not amazing, just good). For veteran players of the franchise, this may not be the "definitive new generation Monster Hunter" game you are looking for. For new players that have never played Monster Hunter before, get the game. There are a lot of quality of life changes and difficulty (lowering) changes that make the game more accessible than ever. No need to worry about skill or knowledge, there is a great community willing to help new players.

As of posting this review (Febuary 20th 2026) the performance issues have been fixed.

The game is in a good state. However, I would personally rate this currently as a "wait for a sale" or a "wait for the incomming expansion" for veteran monster hunter players.

If you have not played monster hunter before then this is a good game to get you into the franchise.

The reason for this review is to point out a few things. These points are not things I view as necissarily good or bad, just things that should be noted.

1. The gameplay/story is extrememly linear. Despite one of the game's features being describeds as "open world exploration" the story is extremely linear and "railroaded". There are literal sections where you cannot do anything but watch as your character auto-paths and talks to npcs

2. The difficulty of the game is much easier compared to previous monster hunter titles. Part of this is due to the new "focus mode" mechanic, part is due to actual "boss" design. This is not to say that there are no difficult bosses. (Savage) Omega, [spoiler]Lagiacrus[/spoiler], and [spoiler]Seregios[/spoiler] are fun and engaging fights, but thats about it. The other "bosses" are not very challenging

3. Auto-pathing & Map design. While a lot of hate for the auto-pathing function I am personally indifferent. My problem is that I believe the auto-pathing is used as an exuse for "poor"/"lazy" map design. I have never hated manually nagivating maps this much. A lot of the maps have winding "branches" and "formations" that you climb on. [spoiler]ruins of wyveria[/spoiler] is basically just a spiral staircase and [spoiler]oilwell basin[/spoiler] has a literal fully functional elevator built into the middle of the map. Not to mention that a lot of the maps can get very dull and ugly depending on the "weather". Though it should be noted that the maps are conversly gorgeous during the "season of plenty" weather.

4. Wounds. the "wound" mechanic is essentially a weakspot a player can create on the boss if they repeatedly attack them in the same place. Players can also "pop"/"detonate" the wound to knock down the boss and create a dps opening. This adds to the second point, where the game is too easy. certain bosses can be chain staggered to a point where they literally spend almost the entire fight on the floor.

5. HP bloat. The point touches up on points 2 & 4. The developers seem to be listening to the players and are adding more difficult boss fights (which is good). But hp values of bosses seem to be going up at a staggering rate. Most end game bosses have more hp than Fatalis from "Monster Hunter World: Iceborne". Note that the game is currently still in high rank and curently does not have an expasion. Also a lot of the end game bosses ignore the knockdown of the "wound" mechanic or straight up heal their wounds. So I wonder, why even have a wound mechanic to begin with?
6. Sliding. If you hated the sliding attacks from "Monster Hunter World" they have also added it to this game.

7. Weapons swapping. Yes, you can bring more than 1 weapon into a hunt at a time. Yes, you can swap weapon on the fly whenever you want. No this does not change as much as you thing. A majority of players maximize their endgame builds for damage. This means that most players just end up bringing two of the same weapons as each weapon uses a different build to maximize for damage. There are currently only two bosses that recommend using the weapon swapping mechanic: [spoiler]omega[/spoiler] and [spoiler]gogmazios[/spoiler]. Both of them however requiring different elements. So most of the time people still bring two of the same weapon, just in different elements.

8. Skill tax. Certain weapons require certain skill to "feel" comfortable. Other weapons do not reequire certain skills to feel comfortable (this has always been the case for monster hunter, dmg vs comfort). This game seperates weapon and armor skills. As a result, certain weapons (specifically any weapon that can block) must fully commit to either be fully damage vs comfort. While other weapons do not have such restrictions and can fully build for damage.

9. The map tool. You know where all monsters are at all times now AND in the future. The map shows monsters that will spawn and where they will spawn. Again, I personally am indifferent but there are some people that dont like this. Personally, I'm curious as to how "invaders" are going to work considering you will know where and when they will spawn.

Genuinely the most disappointing Monster Hunter game I've ever played.

I've been playing monster hunter since 3u on the original 3ds. To say that these games have helped shape my personality would be an understatement. Monster Hunter is one of the only major series of games that managed to keep me attached by being good at its core principles: amazing combat, and incredibly distinct style and worldbuilding. Monster Hunter remains the core game of its genre, the king of the castle of hunting games. It blends its world to be cohesive with the act of hunting these massive creatures, and while its not always completely stable from a ludo-narrative perspective, its as close as you could possibly get to a perfect blend. 

Until this game came along and ♥♥♥♥♥♥ it all up. 

Monster Hunter Wilds is not a destruction of the game on a mass scale, but it does create so many cracks in the tried and true formulae of the series that it's impossible to ignore as a returning player. There are upsides to this game, yes, the onboarding process has been significantly improved, but it has come with detriments to nearly every system since World came out nearly ten years ago.

I even upgraded my PC to play this on medium graphics, only to have its slew of updates and drm render it nearly impossible to play for me now. It runs like ♥♥♥♥, as monster hunter games famously do. So I am also skewed against this for personal reasons, but I still beat the campaign, so I have enough experience in this to say that yes, this game sucks compared to its peers. 

The game's combat has been shafted with the edition of the Wound system, the main gimmick of Wild's combat. The ability to chain stun and go into uninterruptable animations while the monster sits there and freaks out is genuinely terrible. People have made it a point to say that the hunts last the same 'time' as any other, but the monsters have never felt more helpless than in this game. The solution? Add gimmicks. One hit kill moves, bad damage scaling, raid mechanics. These are the only true challenges that the game presents, ♥♥♥♥♥♥ gimmick mechanics that do not fit into the core system of a standard hunt. It's unbalanced dreck in both directions. At the same time!

The map design is the worst its ever been. Monster hunter maps all have specific and distinct color pallet that give the feel of unique biomes rife with flora and fauna that FIT INTO the setting. They're so memorable that I can name them off the top of my head: World had Green/Brown, Orange/Green, Red/Black/Yellow, Blue/Pink, and White/Orange/Red. Each map used each piece for different functions and hunts to create a cohesive ecosystem. Worlds went further and even added endemic life, which stood out in the environment, but still remained cohesive.

Wilds attempt at creating a cohesive ecosystem was to build a map and add seasons to them. This, if implemented properly, would have been the best addition to the games in general: creating essentially separate maps within maps to add more monsters to them, ones that you wouldn't normally see in the 'standard' map. 

Wilds did not put them in properly. Seasons range from unnoticeable to literally nothing, and the only real thing they bring to the table is neutral grays and browns on an already mediocre smattering of color pallets. The endemic life in Wilds as well is more obtrusive than it is natural. It seems like 90% of endemic life was added solely to provide buffs to you while hunting. In conception, you'd think this would make them more endearing, but all it does is add large glowing distractions that stand out in a near cartoonish capacity to the environment. Endemic life didn't NEED to benefit you while hunting, it was set dressing and an extra bit of content to do while playing. By trying to tie it closer to the core game, both create unwanted friction that pulls you out of your immersion. 

More still, the streamlining of tracking monsters has been trivialized and made obnoxious. You never develop an actual feel for the zones you're traveling through, you just ride along, bumping through every invisible wall in existence. It sucks.

Finally, the story. Oh my god the story.

So monster hunter is a long running series, and like any long running series, there's a set of in-jokes and playful sentiments that form around the games. One such sentiment is the idea that monster hunter games are 'all the same' as far as story goes, wherein there's a big monster ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ things up that you have to go kill, and that's it. Another such sentiment is that the setting exists in a world 'on the shoulders of giants' where there was a civilization connected to the monsters that existed in the past, but is gone now. The games tease this, the player base tease this, its all copacetic. 

Wilds story was like watching a newly hired writing team blunder in on 20 years of this sentiment and decide to push it to the forefront of the games in the most obnoxious way possible. Characters are annoying, unlikeable, and pointless. Your Handler Alma(the companion character that gives you quests) is so much nothing that it makes me wish for World's handler, because at least she has a personality. Every single person in this game is this upbeat peppy drivel that feels canned beyond imagination. Your own character included, because for some reason every annoying person in existence just couldn't stand the idea of a silent protagonist over "I'll save the day!" "Don't worry, I got this!" every twelve seconds. 

The single most cringe edition to this game is the Handlers new use of 'authorization': where every. single. cutscene of a monster or being in danger has them point like they're this awe-stunning power and saying "I permit you to kill this thing!" It's nonsense. It's treated with such reverence and its NONSENSE! It's a defiling of the whole core premise. You aren't on a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ nature reserve, you're in the untamed, untouched wilderness about to be attacked by monsters, and you need a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ bureaucrat to come over and go "erhm, you're all good bro!"?! In previous games, the handlers served as ecological researchers that observed and took notes on what you were doing, and that was perfect, it was the most "if its not broke, don't fix it" thing that monster hunter ever did. Trying to solve the minor issue of the dissonance of killing hundreds of these things isn't worth lampooning every cool emotion you could possibly feel.

And that's the game. Monster Hunter Wilds, for this returning player, was taking every cool emotion I wanted to feel and lampooning them beneath bloated mechanics, unwieldly dialogue, and bad design. This will be the first monster hunter I've ever skipped the expansion for. This is the first monster hunter I will not play more than a hundred hours of. I could go on for days with how many pieces of the puzzle they ruined for this series, but these main points should be enough for you to get the picture. 

If you're a new player and this vibes with you, or you thought this game was great, don't feel bad. This isn't meant to detract from your experience, especially if you enjoyed your time. But when I say it could be better, I say it with every piece of my love for the series.

For a more in-depth review:
I'm a monster hunter veteran from Tri and especially 3U and can say I've been enjoying the hell out of this game. Some things aren't perfect but overall I think it's a fantastic entry in the series and even better than World in a lot of of ways.

The hunter controls better and more smoothly than ever, and all the different weapon types feel very effective and great to use and have awesome new moves and improvements (really happy they went back to World's bow moves after Rise kind of re-nerfed the bow...). 

All the different environments are great and feel very distinct, and the new weather and season system can really change up how hunts go, as well as give a nice change of scenery. There seems to be even more of a focus on environmental tools, traps, and endemic life you can use during hunts too which I always enjoy. Even the Grand Hub feels awesome and is gorgeous, though I do miss the house from World that you could customize pretty extensively.

As for monsters, I feel like the roster is pretty solid, even if a bit sparse still. Lots of my favorites are back, like Gore Magala, Lagiacrus, and Nerscylla, and some of the new monsters are fantastic, especially the Apexes and some others like Chatacabra, Ajarakan and Lala Barina. Plus, the new Cephalopod class is very cool and certainly different. Zoh Shia and Gogmazios are awesome too but we definitely need more Elder Dragons. 
There are some monsters I'm not super impressed by though, like Quematrice (decent design but such a forgotten pushover), Doshaguma (surprisingly fun and challenging to fight, especially the Guardian version, but the design is just a lump of hair...), and Hirabami (interesting design but does it even attack?? I just completely steamroll it every time). Some of the older gen 1 and 2 returners are just ok too, it's good to see them again but there's nothing really impressive going on there.

There are a few relatively minor things I don't like about the game though:
First, I'm kind of mixed on the Seikret. It's great to have a mount to get around faster and to get through tough terrain, as well as carrying a secondary weapon for you, but it's kind of insane that it can just autopilot you to the monster. I liked the Palamutes better in that regard; they're fast and agile but you still actually need to drive it yourself (also doggies are the best.)
Second, the game really seems to shower you in materials, even rare ones all the time. This could be seen as a good thing as it reduces the grind, but that's always been a part of Monster Hunter and getting enough materials to make a full armor set after like 2 or 3 hunts just feels wrong. Wounds a big part of this I think, since breaking one always gives you some materials.
Lastly, the aforementioned sparse roster. There are a lot of cool monsters here, but it doesn't feel like many, and some are just reskins/harder versions of the same monster. Guardian versions are cool but don't really feel that much different from the normal monster, and Tempered and Arch-Tempered are really just the same monster but with bigger numbers (and OCCASIONALLY some new moves).

Overall though I love the game and will continue to play quite a bit!! I'm super looking forward to the upcoming expansion too; I can't wait to hear more!

This game is both a must-play for long time veterans of the Monster Hunter series as well as a fantastic intro point for new players. The Focus Mode mechanic both adds another layer of complexity and QOL to the game and makes certain weapons easier to play for new players (looking at you Greatsword). While the game started out with some pretty major performance issues on PC, these have all been resolved, and now with the final Festival going on before the long wait for the DLC it's a perfect time for new players to engage with all the game's content while the multiplayer base is still going strong. Only ding I give is the same ding I've given to every main series game since World: if you're a returning veteran trying to get through the main game story as fast as possible, it's tedious as hell.
there was kind of a rough release and performance issues but at least for myself those have been mostly resolved and this is a fun monster hunting experience.

Switching weapons mid fight is a great addition in my opinion and adds a lot of flexibility to a hunt while not feeling OP. You can bring a experimental weapon build or something you are not comfortable with to try out and if it doesnt work out you can just use ol reliable!

Performance was always an issue with this game since launch but now it runs on the steamdeck (turn upscaling off so it doesnt look like a smudge) At a reasonable 30 fps! On my pc it runs great as well.

Overall, its good, go get it while on sale (I HATE NATA)

I've held off on making a review despite loving this game because previously I felt like its performance issues were unreasonable for the average person, now however the game in its content complete form with the recent performance patches is an excellent experience.
