This game has a large amount of negative buzz around it due its messy release and 20+ years of series expectations, you have many other reviews to check for that so instead I will sing my praises of this game: It has one of the best base game monster rosters in the series and it feels like combat design has been adjusted to make things flow in a satisfying way ; The focus system adds further player combat expression with a particular highlight of the system being wounds that only appear after monsters perform certain attacks which further incentivises learning monster moves/behaviour ; Lots of fantastic new music with some outstanding new additions to a series with a staggering musical history ; A step in the right direction for Monster Hunter storytelling, there is still plenty of room for improvement (especially in execution while playing the game in a co-op format) but I appreciate the effort.

I have great expectations for the upcoming DLC as this game is a superb foundation to build upon and I look forward to the future of Monster Hunter in general. I do hope for a return to some of the classic friction from older titles but I imagine it may be hard from a design philosophy standpoint to reverse the quality of life they've brought into the series recently seeing as it has coincided with a big uptick in sales, we shall have to see what the future holds.

Now that the base game is finished, I can finally write a small review.
This game has ruined MH and has no redeeming qualities for the Ultimate version.
Where to begin is genuinely a difficult question; let's start with the -limited- qualities of the game.
I love this game Hammer. The Cephalopods are a really nice addition, new skeletons are always welcome.
Andddd that's it !
Now let's take a look at the bad : focus mode, wounds, monsters AI, maps, quests, content, atmosphere/world building... Let's stop here. I think this game have no qualities and being an MH just makes it worse, since when have the player base expectations been set so low ?
I will only mention the performance issues since it's an external factor to the core game design, which is the real problem here... So yeah the game doesn't run, at all.
The addition of a new weapon is also lacking, for 5 gen I can understand, now there is no excuses.

Starting with the most problematic aspect of the game, the gameplay.
Let's start by understanding what makes MH gameplay good in the first place : positioning (where your character stands), spacing (how will you use your weapon moveset according to the monster moveset) and timing. This is the core gameplay of ALL the games before Wilds; this is why the games are deep and interesting in the first place.
What does focus mode do to this ? Well, since you don't have a reason to NOT attack anymore, you just spam buttons nonstop and move around the hitboxes while attacking. 
Dealing damage is a noncommittal action. Let's take AED (thanks Capcom for killing CB once again btw) for example, a really slow attack making your character go forward. Before Wilds, you needed to adjust yourself in advance so you could snipe hitzones without getting punished. In Wilds, you just throw it randomly, your initial position doesn't matter, just focus for the head. An hitboxe is coming ? Well your AED is moving, so just focus mode out of the hitboxe. You're late ? Don't worry, just keep attacking to dodge. Every ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ interaction in the game is just focus mode out of your problems. Play without it ? Yeah, I agree, just play another MH ;)
This is part of why the game is so uninteresting, I won't cover the control you have over the monsters with wounds and environment.

I have to speak a few words about the bestiary of this game : all the ATs are just worse versions of the base monsters, Omega and Gogmazios are EXTREMELY badly designed monsters (both are solid contenders for worst fights ever), frenzy is not a fun joke and... Hirabami ???
The monsters also have terrible illnesses, they can't stay for more than 2 seconds in the same area due to their ADHD and when they're low HP they develop hyperactive behaviour and start running to the other side of the map. Don't even think about stopping them, if you try to use lure pods, they will grow tired of you quickly. If you try flash pods, it turns out the monsters are blind. Only the gentle caress of death (or actually sleep most of the time) will stop them.

The maps are the worst ever made, I suspect because of Seikret. Those are gigantic, empty and without any identity; most of the time even ugly thanks to the cool feature of turning off all the colours on your screen, "weather".
Gameplay wise it's not better, dunes, ledges, added interactions ruining the flow of the fights, tiny arenas... You have every issue you could ever imagine ! Enjoy poor gameplay with bonus free struggles fighting the maps.
I actually do really love the feature of pop up camps, you can set spawns on set locations, but not all of them, and sometimes they will get destroyed for no reason. In other words, you have multiple spawns, like before, except they disappear for fun I guess ??? Great idea.
Seamless is a great idea for immersion, no one ever said (except on twitter of course).
The world have never seemed so tiny since then, you have no room for imagination since you can "explore" (huge word for how bad the level design is) everything.
The villages are just 3 tiny boxes with the usual facilities, no unseen, no identity, nothing. There is Suja and the hub, which are the only 2 okay villages, the rest is a joke.
They are the same template with different ground texture, I can't believe in it, I'm just out.
Look at Yukumo, a tiny village lost in the mountain with hotsprings and cozy music. Look at Val Habar, a huge portuary city in the desert, so huge and crowded you see npcs walk offscreen. I can dream standing in any village except the Wilds ones.

This is also duo to the ost of the game being so poor, the only bad MH in terms of music, they are even annoying. I quickly get a headache hearing the Arkveld theme. At least Zoh Shia have the only good one in the game.

The endgame system is once again random generated quests with impossible RNG turning the game into a cheat mandator... A casino.
For the love of gods please stop with the random generated quests, it's been atrocious since their introduction in MH4. No one wants quests lock behind a useless farming wall.
It's even the worst ones by the way, resets by sleeping is by far the stupidest mechanic ever made.
All of this to get quests without a cool thematic title and description behind.
At least there is now some quests in the game, not a lot but they exist, only after a year of lifespan ^^

On the topic, the TUs are not free, the game at release was 70€ to get access to way less content and a worse game. TUs are included in the price since World, so they better keep doing them and raise the quality since the game is even more expensive than before... With a LOT of micro transactions.

I could go on for hours thinking about how bad everything is in this game, but this small review was just a cathartic exercise for me. I've been playing MH for more than 10 years almost permanently. I did speedruns, irls, tournaments, community events... I made a lot of friends along the way.
Seeing MH dying this way is something hard to accept for me.
Please do not buy this game, do not support it. Go play Rise, Portable 3rd, Frontier or any other game deserving love instead.
By the way, if you want a good story, play a visual novel like Tsukihime/Steins;Gate or read a light novel like Mushoku Tensei/Re:Zero, do not play a gameplay orientated game. It sounds obvious to me but not for everyone I guess, especially Capcom it seems.

[h1] My Monster Hunter Wilds Experience [/h1]

[h2] The Demo [/h2]
When the demo for this game became playable, I was worried that I'd never be able to play the full game due to how bad the performance was, even at the lowest settings. The game felt super under-baked and clunky which was also a worry due to the game not being super far off from release. Despite the demo scaring me, I still bought the most expensive pre-order bundle, as I've been a fan of the franchise for years and had hope that the full game would be better.

[h2] Full Release [/h2]
Upon release, the full game didn't run very well no matter what I did. It was more playable than the demo, and I could live with it because I enjoy Monster Hunter for the gameplay, not the graphics. Besides the performance, this game does feel really easy at times, some of the UI is clunky, and some parts of the game just don't feel fully baked yet. This game does do a lot of things well, though, and some things are done even better than in the previous titles.

[h2] Improvement Over Time [/h2]
This game has never by any means been the perfect Monster Hunter game; however, Capcom has been going out of their way to improve on a lot of the issues that players have had, as well as pushing lots of new content and higher difficulty parts of the game to make the experience better for veterans and more hardcore players. Just recently they even released a pretty large optimization update that not only made the performance better than it ever has been, but it also added a ton of new content to the mix. Prior to the recent update, I struggled to hit above 30 FPS on anything higher than medium/low settings; now I can play at a consistent 60+ FPS on primarily high settings, which is absolutely amazing!

[h2] Final Thoughts [/h2]
Overall, this game started off in a bad place, and a lot of players will continue to despise it because of that; however, it has evolved so much since launch, and I believe that Capcom will continue to try their best and improve where they've stumbled. We still have a lot to look forward to with this game, and I'm sure Capcom has a lot cooking in the oven right now. Previously, both World and Rise had massive content expansions that were released a while after release, those being Iceborne and Sunbreak. Those expansions added a ton of content but also fixed issues and improved the performance of both games. Wilds will most likely be getting an expansion eventually, and I'm hoping that Capcom continues to deliver on improving Monster Hunter: Wilds and making it the best game it can be!

Being straightforward, as of February 2026 the game is a solid entry in the franchise and has enough content now post-launch that any player looking to get it has enough to chew on for a while.
A lot of the criticisms are still valid in regards to things like the wounding system, difficulty or lack thereof until endgame, and things like multiplayer connectivity.  However, many of the issues we saw on PC as far as performance, optimization, and crashing, seem to have gotten a lot better with the updates over the course of the first year.  Perfect? No, but it's a good game as it is and it runs well enough for me to feel comfortable getting new friends into it.  The additional paid content doesn't provide any sort of advantage and the gameplay is consistent with what I'd expect from a Monster Hunter game. Cross-play is a plus when you're not dropping connection to the host, so no matter where you or your friends are you can all hunt together.  We'll see what the expansion has in store for us at this point.  Happy Hunting!

MH players are split across several "active" games. Generations Ultimate (the BiS), World, Rise (dope AF fr fr on god), and Wild. But also, the older games still have active communities. I 've been droppin big Bonks on Ratholos since 2004, and I think Wilds is the worst of the modern era MH games, and that's for two reasons.
First is its poor optimization (which is getting better but its still not great) it seems like they really wanted to push newer hardware to the limit, which for me personally, feels like a moot venture... Like, why are we trying to simulate every hair follicle on a monsters ass when my old man eyes still see everything as PS3 graphics? It just feels like a waste of processing power and an unnecessary limitation for something that doesn't make the game any better.
The second problem, is its millennial story telling style... if you know, you know. And, I don't think Alma is quite as bad as Handler was from World as far as just constantly talking and getting herself into bad situations, But like, telling me "oh btw you can carve monster parts off of severed tails!" AS I AM actively carving a monsters tail is annoying (also, just a side tangent... in the story isn't my character is made out to already be an experienced hunter? its why I was chosen for this mission... So, why is she telling me things that I'd cannonically already know? Especially when I clicked that I don't want tutorials), AND(x2) the story beat that I somehow, by regulation, specifically need hers and the guilds "authorization" to hunt monsters in MONSTER HUNTER? that feels dumb. ALSO(x3), wtf you mean theirs a tribe of people in a land of giant freakin monsters that have never even seen a weapon? Like they don't even understand the concept? really? That was all before I got stuck in the "Unhandled exception monsterhunterwilds.exe!0x140000000 + 0xa46a140" crash loop so MAYBE theirs more to that but I don't think I'm gonna find out for a while.
SO... if you got new hardware (in THIS economy? Mr Moneybags over here, did you sell a bridge to a dragon or somethin?) then its probably at least gonna be fun to bop monsters like the other games, But if you're somebody with older system specs, you're gonna be better off with World or Rise instead.

As someone who has already beaten Gogma on Xbox, I wholeheartedly recommend this game, for vets and newbies. Low-rank can be a slog, but ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ it is worth it, the endgame grind is addictive with an almost flawless roster of tempered high-ranks to farm. The arch-tempereds? Perfect. Title updates? Perfect. I am aware of the terrible optimization issues that this game had on launch, but now that it has been almost completely sorted, this game has shaped up into a game that eclipses base World in quality, both in area-design and roster diversity. While it has a slightly smaller monster-pool compared to base World, it has far less forgettable wyverns, thanks to its goofy event quests and unique armor transcension system. My only real complaint is the Seikret. Less reliable than wirefall, and disengages you from the areas that are so lovingly built with so much detail. Some of my favourite moments are just wandering around the cliffs or the forest, with no real goal. I found some really neat areas that normally you just wouldn't find. It saddens me that I don't know the scarlet forest like the back of my hand, an area which I love, in the same vein as I do the ancient forest from World, an area which I hate. I kinda wish they just ripped the wirebug mechanic from Rise, and maybe even the palimute.

Monster hunter wilds is a good game, but not a good monster hunter.

There are numerous things that makes the game fun (like improvement on weapons move set, barrel bowling, returning of beloved monsters and so on) but equally if not more so, removes the engagement that the hunter has in their environment.

 There is no sense to prepare yourself onto a hunt in wilds unlike previous titles. The games normally punishes you for not being prepared for the fight that you can encounter with some of the monsters, but in this game its a non-stop boss rush and you don't need anything to stop you to going to one monster to another. 

The only time the real challenge comes to play is at the post end game segment meaning that if the player really wants to find something that has to test their skills with the monster whether that be of their attacks or setting traps and bombs ahead of time they would have to grind it out in almost autopilot state of being. This may be my criticism for the game feeling just too numbing, that could also be the seikret problem as well since it literally autopilots you to whatever destination you marked on the map.

I will also mentioned that this is the worst low rank experience in a monster hunter game I've played to date. I hate how linear the game feels not letting me make my own choices with what monsters I want to hunt. I miss rise's mechanic where you can immediately hop onto a hub with friends and pick whatever monster you want for key quests not wanting to bother with the story because at the end of the day I just want to hunt some cool monsters with friends and how this game sets up it really doesn't feel like the game encourages you to do so.

(I don't think my words would relate to many people but that's how i feel, I still like the game I just wish the game gave me the freedom and critical think to approach the problem with a monster and be rewarded with a smooth hunt when having the right preparation. I'm also an individual that likes monster hunter freedom, so take that with what its worth.)

Hopefully the G rank expansion will help aid the problems I've brought up, if not I'm still looking forward towards new monster hunter titles and expansions, just please make sure its well optimized when at launched.

TLDR:
Games fun, but feels too autopilot and because of that my brain goes numb.

I have been a fan of the monster hunter franchise from since 4U released, with hundreds of hours spent between that game and worldborne. While in the run up to wilds' release I was anticipating to play it more than I have for any other game before (I went out of my way to build a new rig in part so I could run the thing), after 100 hours of playing, I am sick of contending with the continued issues that prevent me enjoying this title. At the start of the game's launch crashes were infrequent but impactful enough to sour my opinion slightly, but the bulk of these went away as time went on, and I was quite happy with most of what the game had to offer (the story and voice acting I felt were quite weak, but nothing worth getting hung up over, those are not really why most people play the games in this franchise). 

However, around the release of TU3 I stopped playing for several months, due to not liking the content introduced (purely personal preference). After a few months I came back in Feburary 2026 to try the final Title Update and see how much the game had improved, but was slightly annoyed to see that now the game is regularly crashing for me, even when I am merely adjusting graphical settings in the main menu. I am disapointed that even after efforts to improve the poor perfomance, the playability of the game is now somehow in a worse state than it was for me 6 months ago. I am giving this review as a hope that these performance issues will be solved before the release of the expansion, as there are some aspects of Wilds that are genuienly some of the best realised in the Monster Hunter series (visuals, music, monster variety). Yet I cannot recommend this title to many interested newcomers as of now, thanks largely to the dumbfounding inability to get the performance working consistently for many people, which is a shame.

Wilds is a fundamentally broken game that shifts the gameplay from Monster Hunter to Monster May Cry. It's cool that they've kinda fixed the performance, but it's still not great. But that's unimportant, because we have 40x framegen and can upscale from 240p for a definitely equivalent experience. What is important is the gameplay. It's been completely neutered down from previous entries into nothing more than a boss rush title, but because hunters now have so much available in their kits that's not as fun either. I've still gotten a lot out of Wilds just because at a base level it is entertaining and well made, but that alone does not make something good. 

The game is completely noncommittal. With almost zero exception, there is no fight taking place where you cannot simply run away to restock on items. This isn't necessarily an issue, but when you have a Seikret that allows you to ignore just about everything in the battle and heal up and sharpen at your own pleasure, the pacing takes a hit. Nothing is really dangerous unless you have a severe case of tunnel vision or are being faced with an instakill aoe attack, And then, when you play completely braindead for that entire time and take so much damage you run through all your potions, you can continue to ignore the fight, fast travel to a camp, and restock on literally everything. With the open world format this is kind of an unavoidable issue but it's made worse by the convenience of being able to place camps close to wherever you think you'll be fighting the most, reducing the amount of time that it takes to get back in the fight when you do restock. So because of all that, Wilds refuses to punish the player for playing poorly with a failed mission and instead pats them on the back as long as they can figure out how to almost never die because resource scarcity is a thing that just doesn't happen.

And then I still end up playing with people who have never played a video game before and end up failing quests anyways.

I never elaborated on the other elements, so I'm editing this now cause I remembered. The story is fine, I liked it and it's fun to go through but I'd be hard pressed to call it good. Music is great as always. But I have a bigger thing I want to ♥♥♥♥♥ about now: the gathering and preparation loop. It's been neutered with convenience after convenience to make it less of a friction point than ever and while I can appreciate wanting to reduce the friction since it can be a pain of the ass, it turns into a death by a thousand cuts scenario where you just don't need to ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ gather anything any more. No paintballs or psychoserum because you already know the monster's location 24/7, infinite whetstone use, and because hunters are already so powerful and have so many methods of toppling or staggering monsters you don't need traps for anything but capturing and flash/sonic bombs are worthless to bother getting. On top of that, gathering itself is trivial now. It's borderline instant to do and you can pick up just about everything from 20 feet away, and the seikret is so ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ fast that density isn't an issue.

This isn't necessarily an issue brand new to Wilds. World "solved" tracking by turning it into a legitimately engaging and more realistic loop of tracking down the monster, albeit highly simplified, and Rise just tells you where the monster is. But like I said, every convenience and simplification just gets piled on all the rest, and it makes it nonexistent. I'm not gonna pretend like the preparation loop is anything more than friction. The game wouldn't be better if we all returned to MH2 and spent 5x longer gathering and combining ♥♥♥♥ than actually hunting, but it still represents a gradual whittling down of the series' identity into AAA sludge.

Do I recommend this game to anyone.....I would give it a rating between “kinda” and “it depends”. This game is sort of a mess if you are looking at it as a game alone. I am NOT going to praise this game that took about a year from its release to actually be in a playable state, something that should have been the case when it was released. Is this just the culture now? Come out with a game that definitely shows aspects of being unfinished, and hope that your fan base turns a blind eye to it, or at least just deals with it.

You have individuals saying, “Well, it's playable, so stop talking objectively about it now.” Honestly, no, I won't. I can be a fan and still give criticism. There are some clear design choices about this game that I find questionable as a whole, from this base Monster Hunter to its last base title update. The overtuned wounding system, the balance between monster and hunter, the lack of a gathering hall on release, the slot-machine-induced endgame, and the overall tone of what monster hunter is now. This is coming from someone whose first Monster Hunter game was Monster Hunter Freedom Unite. 

Overall, it's a fun game, but frustrating. Time will tell if it actually becomes a “Great ” monster hunter game. Soon enough, the expansion will release, and they will either fix some of the design choices or maybe they will double down. Who knows. But for the product we have now, it's a fun, at times messy, game. 

Also forgot to mention that the monster cash shop won't get better over time. Locking things behind a paywall that used to be obtainable through event quests in other Monster Hunter games is scummy as hell. But since many people do purchase from it because of FOMO, it's only going to get worse.

Wilds while not a perfect game (no game is perfect), still a very interesting and full of potential. As the flagship of 6th generation of a such longstanding series like Monster Hunter, Wilds with a ton of new features never seen before in the series along with a new game engine. Open world map (kinda), new combat mechanic in Focus Mode, a new method in traveling with the Seikret, weather system etc etc make Wilds a fresh air for the series. Monster lineup for Wilds also very very good even at base version and especially now after 4 Title Update bringing 5 new monster (4 MH original + 1 Collab) creating a very good lineup for a base game version. The combat also felt great even if focus mode kinda make earlier monster very very easy, but they always designed to be very easy to begin with so no problem.

Wilds biggest sin is not in monster lineup or new feature or anything like that, it's biggest sin is egeregious and terrible terrible performance, the price it's need to pay for using a new engine designed for Resident Evil type of map to create an open world one. Thankfully, the issue can be said to be solved but the damage has been done. Wilds terrible performance will contiune to haunt it at least until the expansion release, at best.

With all that said, I believe this game has potential to cement Monster Hunter series as a mainstream media (whether that is good thing or not, let time be the judge) and especially with the expansion, I hope this game will become better and better.

[TLDR: Best MH so far, Better combat, Better Graphics, values your time, doesn't expect you to hunt for 45 Minutes on a single monster like older titles, doesn't run nearly as bad as people complain about (probably potato pc)]

Performance:
Don't understand the hate for the game RTX 5070 (NON TI) 5600X NVME 16GB ram, Max graphics High Ray Tracing and HD Texture pack 90+ frames per second no upscaling no dips, using Frame Gen gives me crazy smoothness and no blurry look.

Values your time:
Shorter more impactful hunts & better RNG for loot, food buffs last multiple hunts instead of once off per.
I also prefer being able to auto-pilot my mount to the Monster to stop me from spending 30 Minutes on hunting a single monster like World did, I have gotten further in less time than any other MH game (gear wise) and that is a good thing, older titles forcing you to re-grind a 45 minute monster 6 times for 1 armor set and then grind again for a weapon which you throw away within a few hunts was absolutely boring. don't even get me started on Gems or Legendary Weapons in older titles.

The Seamless world blew my mind being able to run right out of camp to hunt a monster or gather materials is amazing, all older MH games were loading screen simulators.

Wilds Vs World Content:
Can't compare the content to World as it was launched in 2018 and had massive updates and a expansion, but Knowing Capcom, Wilds will get to Worlds level of content very quickly when the DLC drops, what is there now is pretty good, easy 100+ hour time sink of content ingame right now.

Difficulty:
All Monster Hunter games are super easy until HR10 (after the main cinematic campaign ends) more so if you know how to play a MH game and muscle memory kicks in. This game just modernized The Monster Hunter experience and I am all for it I have played every single MH since the PSP era, fluid combat, blocking is rewarding instead of punishing, parrying is doable and not sweat level only for no life MH players anymore.

My Gripes:
They could've really slotted in the Original MH sound track into the last fight opening scene instead, and given us a proper Main Hub along side the camps earlier in the campaign, as I preferred the main hub areas of all the other games. 

please bring back paying for Cat Chef meals with Zenny I prefer those animations over the hunter making his own meals. 

Multiplayer isn't as seamless as it could be, needing to re-create lobbies to travel to another map is kind of a pain, I expected multiplayer to be as seamless as the rest of the content and be like Iceborne where 2 players can CO-OP to the very end with cutscenes and all.

Overall:
The game itself is a really good Monster Hunter, combat is the best it has ever been and after the Cinematic Story you start getting High Rank hunts with decently difficult monsters, content is coming but what is in now is more than enough to keep anyone interested until the DLC drops. 17 Hours in finished campaign and doing the "Meat" of the experience now and I am enjoying every minute spent.

Took quite some time for Wilds to grow on me, even from World it was a big jump. Far disconnected from the games of old where you simply slayed monsters without hundreds of cutscenes and even loads more of exposition being spoken at you. 

Overall I really enjoy Wilds spite the rather lack of challenges in the beginning and the 10 hour hand holding story.

Capcom listened and finally got the game running where more and more people can play and added some much needed more challenging fights.

[b][h1]Monster Hunter Wilds: A Wild Start[/h1][/b]

  I held off posting a review for this game for a while since while I enjoyed it myself at launch, the amount of issues the game had made it difficult for me to recommend it. Since then, the game has been updated many times fixing or otherwise applying band-aids to a lot of these issues.

[b][h1]The issues at launch[/h1][/b]

[h2][i]Optimization[/i][/h2]
  
  The most talked about issue was the optimization of the game. This topic has dominated most Wilds discussion since launch and for good reason. [b] The game simply did not run acceptably at all.[/b] Multiple stutters, crashes, poor FPS, and even not launching were some of the most common issues everyone had. 

  How has Capcom responded to this? It took many months, but now there has been multiple patches addressing performance. I can say from my own experience on my hardware the game runs smoothly. I do not get nearly the same stutters I did before, I do not have any game crashes, and lastly the FPS is more than acceptable for me. I cannot say though for certain this will be everyone's experience however. With PC gaming there are literal thousands of possible combinations to account for. I would suggest trying to lookup videos showcasing performance on current patch that best matches your hardware. All in all, I am happy with the current performance of the game and it is much better than its launch state.

[h2][i]Difficulty[/i][/h2]

  
  The next most discussed criticism of Wilds was its difficulty, or rather, lacking thereof. I will say that this topic is rather complex, and I will not be able to give the full breakdown it deserves. Discussing difficulty in games is difficult as it's something that is subjective. Everyone has a different level of difficulty they desire from a game, and differing ideas of how to tune it. 

  What I can say without a doubt is the main story progression did felt a little too easy, and this was a common complaint. What I think was the problem overall was the tuning of game mechanics compared to how the monsters can fight back. Wilds introduced 2 new mechanics for combat called focus mode and the wound system. 

  Focus mode allows us to change the direction our character faces even while attacking. This makes it so it is much easier to keep uptime on a monster and maximize DPS. During the main progression the monsters lack the HP or the move set to deal with us constantly being able to hit it. 

  The wound system allows us to create wounds on the monster that we can initiate special moves on that inflict hefty damage and even stagger the monster. The wounds were very abundant however, leading us to being bale to constantly combine stuns, knockdowns, and wound staggers to basically stunk lock the monster to death.

  When you combine the above mechanics it lead to every hunt feeling like you went in, and stun locked a poor monster for 5 minutes and left. Leading to a bland experience where it all felt the same.

  Capcom did respond to this by adding more challenging monsters over the title updates. We've seen buffs to 8* monsters, addition of 9* monsters, arch tempered monsters, and in the last update we got 10* arch tempered monsters.  I can say for myself the game now has plenty of fun challenging hunts in the endgame. While it is a shame the story progression remains the same, the fact that at the end has so many fun and hard quests makes up for it.

[i][h2]Lack of things to do at the end[/h2][/i]

  The last issue I had was lack of endgame at launch. We did have an endgame system with artian parts, however it didn't do a good job really promoting grinding, and it actually hurt the game as all the best weapons were (in my opinion) ugly green artian weapons.

  Luckily, with the title updates the endgame is a lot more expansive and fun. There is a endgame loop that has expanded artian weapons to include grinding multiple challenging monster quests. And on top of that, we have transcendence which allows us to even further improve lower tier armor. Overall, there is a lot more to do and more variety of things to hunt

[h2][i]Closing thoughts[/i][/h2]

  
  This is a great monster hunter game. While it had a lot of issues at launch, Capcom did not abandon it, and slowly over a year has vastly improved the experience. Is it perfect? No, there are still things that could improve, but I am more than happy to say this is a Monster Hunter game I would easily recommend now. It is a shame it took this long, but at least it is here now.

I've waited an entire year to make a thorough review of Monster Hunter Wild's base game + Title Updates, and it's safe to say, I can now definitely recommend this game! Despite CAPCOM potentially rushing the publish time, the developers did not give up to deliver performance updates as well as amazing Title Updates (as always.) This most recent update really delivered on that Monster Hunter charm that I was yearning for.
-
[b] Pros: [/b]
[u]-Combat[/u] feels fresh and exciting, with most weapons receiving a major playstyle change. Every weapon is now viable (yes, even hunting horn, my beloved.)
[u]-Monster skeleton diversity and creativity[/u] is very good in Wilds. This was a gripe I had with Monster Hunter World's base game that I think Wilds definitely improved on. The monsters also feel more alive, with idle/endemic animations that really show off their character.
[u]-Endemic life makes the world feel bustling and alive.[/u] The attention the detail is amazing.
[u]-The score.[/u] The music and sound effects are phenomenal!
[u]-QOL improvements[/u] such as mix and match armor, movable barrel bombs and traps, weapon flexibility, environmental links, and immediate layered armor/weapons available.
[u]-Endgame.[/u] While still never as good as expansion endgames, the tailsmans and gogma weapons are very fun and rewarding to grind.
[u]-The love and care put into this game is deeply felt[/u] personally. The environmental interactions, diva performances, lighthearted event quests, nostalgic music brought back from previous titles, transparent developers, and the [spoiler] little handcarved gift Alma gives to you for completing the final optional quest [/spoiler] made me feel rewarded for dedicating my time into this game and the community as a whole. 
[u]-Mizutsune :)[/u]

[b] Cons: [/b]
[u]-Auto mount movement[/u] being turned on by default and scoutflies makes exploration obsolete and unimmersive by default. Many players just will never see the beauty of this game because hunts are mounted to a railroad track. It feels you need to go out of your way to explore.
[u]-Lack of ailments[/u] or ailment impact. Monsters in this game give less friction than in past games when it comes to applying ailments to the hunter. I would love to see monsters use more than just raw power as difficulty in the expansion.
[u]-The main story's difficulty is much too easy.[/u] This makes for an odd difficulty curve later when fighting more difficult monsters such as [spoiler] Gore Magala, Mizutsune, Lagiacrus, Seregios, Omega, Gogmazios, and the Arch Tempereds [/spoiler] . Proper friction is key.
[u]-NPC/Tutorial dialogue.[/u] It is VERY irritating and immersion breaking when every NPC shouts exactly what a monster is about to do. There needs to be an option to turn these things OFF. Probably my personal biggest gripe about the entire game is just how hard it feels it is trying to prevent players making ANY mistakes during hunts. Friction is good, please let go of our hands a bit.
-
In the end, I fully recommend this game, especially to creature enthusiasts, build craft lovers, and Monster Hunter fans. You just may need to wait until the endgame for the really challenging hunts.

What a painful experience. I am a long time Monster Hunter fan. This was not a Monster Hunter game.

Now, not to be all negative, the game play of the fighting monsters? Great. Peak. Love it. The art is amazing. Feels gritty and looks amazing. Herds of wild beats roaming large open areas. Amazing. Weapons feel good and play smooth. Super exited about that stuff.

But the problem comes in at the rails and the story. The whole game is on rails. I feel like I'm being lead around on a leash from monster fight to monster fight. If I wanted to play a fighting game, there are better options.
There's no building up my base. There's no gathering materials. No replanning traps. No grinding materials. It feels like one great long tutorial. Told to marvel at the sights. Told to focus on the programmed set pieces. I know how to stare in awe on my own. I don't need to be guided.

And the story is painful. I'm being led around by some pretentious college educated woman who just hangs around doing nothing but yapping. Telling me how pretty everything is. I guess there's no real danger. No survival. No grit and urgency. I'm just chaperoning a fieldtrip for an undergrad. It's safe for this little weaponless woman to be having a good time here. It's a petting zoo, apparently.

So far we have rescued two different little ethnic children and all the hunting feels like protecting morons too stupid to protect themselves. They shouldn't be here, but sure, got to keep them safe. But then even while trying to keep them safe you are told "you're authorized to hunt this monster." You want to ruin any feeling of hunting? have an organization that gives you permission to do it. We're not in the wild, apparently. We're just park rangers culling the deer herd. Could not be more lame.

And what's with all the multiethnic tribes? You're telling me there's a pacifist tribe that doesn't even know what a weapon is and use wind chimes to keep monsters away which don't even work because you quickly have to save them from being attacked anyway? Is everyone stupid? Apparently.

It's all just so overpoweringly lame. Who was this made for? Someone's mom, it seems.

Started out a little rough at launch performance-wise, plus the end-game content left something to be desired, but such is the case for most Monster Hunter games at the start (post-World). That said, I still had an absolute blast with the game then, and even more so now.

There are still problems with the game, for example, multiplayer is more complicated to set up than it needs to be (though it used to be even worse) and I find the idea that a lot of end-game hunts essentially require a group of four, assuming you're not min-maxing, to be a little annoying, at least for a High Rank experience. Also, I tend to agree with those who say that more recent Monster Hunter games would be better described as "Monster Fighter" but this game does enough interesting things with its world for me to believe Capcom still has some appreciation for the ecology, atmosphere and somewhat calculated planning when it comes to interacting with the world and its monsters.

Wilds is still very much Monster Hunter, with its addicting, grindy gameplay loop, extremely well designed monsters and combat that is more fun now than it has ever been. The weapon designs are all fantastic, no more ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥-slapped-on-bones designs from World, the maps are all incredibly put together and while this is an unpopular opinion to many, I actually found the story to be very engaging and felt actual narrative drive to hunt most of the monsters, particularly the flagship and final boss.

The title update monsters have all been amazing to fight, and the monster roster only got better with time. That, along with the massive performance patch that came out recently, makes this a game I will absolutely be returning to, not only for its expansion, but for future full playthroughs even after the fact. 

Anyway Capcom I NEED you to give a ♥♥♥♥ about your old mh games too, please MHGU on PC please please please thank you

Wilds is a flawed yet fun addition to the Monster Hunter series. 

It had a VERY rocky journey to the end of it's content, as we wait for the DLC, but I personally, found it to be a fun one. Because I knew from World and Rise, and both of their DLC's, that all that stuff would get fixed with time. And currently:

- The hardest quests in the games now fully feel as such, pushing even experienced hunters to the edge
- The event quest page is filled, with most being there permanently, giving players a wide variety of silly rewards and trying challenges
- And as of the last update, optimization has improved notably, with more players able to play, without the need for external assistance

All that on top of, quite fun combat and monsters, and I think it is safe to say, that Wilds has earned it's stripes among the series. It does have a few flaws here and there, but we'll see what the DLC brings in terms of answering them. Besides, none of the games in the series is perfect, but we love them all the same.

Despite everything, I'm having fun.

Yes, I'm on (relatively) old hardware, in the form of a NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1080. Cranking everything down as low as it'll go and turning on FSR at Balanced, I can hold about 45 real frames and render at 60 with framegen. The game looks like crap, but it's playable. Genuinely playable. But that's not what this review is about.

The problem is... optimization. In the form of disk drive occupancy.

Every Title Update, no matter how much new content is added, comes in the form of a patch file, which RE Engine loads atop the basegame file. These patch files describe changes. The current Title Update includes a crossover with Stories, adds a few cosmetics, prepares to return past Events for the next month's time, and begins rolling out Arch-Tempered hunts for already existing monsters.

What size is the patch file for this update? 20 GB.

It's a 20 GB patch file describing new quest data (which should probably take up a handful of megabytes at worst), some minor new cosmetic content (between models, textures, VFX, and the related, I'm only expecting a gig max here), and the Stories crossover, which... adds a handful of models, textures, VFX, cutscene data, quests...

The new content contained within this title update certainly is not 20GB worth of content. But because it's Patch Data atop Patch Data atop Patch Data, the packed data has to be shuffled around rather violently.

Optimize how you're serving game content to us, developers. Storage space for PATCH DATA doesn't grow on trees. You've ballooned to over 100GB storage requirements for a collection of assets that reasonably should take less than half that.

Fix this.

This game has come a long way since launch. There are some negatives to it still but the performance is no longer one of them. 

The gameplay is as smooth and accessible as you can ask for and is a great entrance to the franchise while still having challenging and rewarding fights. On the content side of things Monster hunter always feels a too small to begin with.

 It suffers from the same issues that large scale games with constant update cycles all fall victim too. The last game will always have so much more to do than the most recent simply because it had time to grow. 

 Personally I find it disappoints a little on the graphics front but not in regards to fidelity.  Monster Hunter in my short time enjoying the franchise has always has their air of whimsy too it. Not that its a parody or it doesn't take itself seriously, it does. It just comes across as genuine. In this world there are cute talking cats you can dress up and fat little birds who look like cotton balls. It just so happens there are also apocalyptic terrors and walking natural disasters sharing it with them.

If you have ever been subjected to the torture that is the monster hunter movie you'll know what I'm talking about. The tone and vibes of monster hunter are completely unique and unapologetically so. Bright colours and gorgeous music and bipedal cats serving you food porn quality meals. I feel like wilds misses that a bit. 

Capcom found the mid 2000's Call of Duty piss filter and now everything is a little drab and washed out and a touch more lifeless feeling. Monster Hunter is as much the vibes of the world as it is the act of hunting monsters and engaging in the gameplay loop itself. 

 This is easily fixed with a few quick scripts of course, I highly recommend "Disable Post Processing Effects" by TonWonton and "Tweak Volumetric Fog" by TheFuzzy51 for a light face lift that keeps the game feeling vanilla and brightens everything up a bit. Its just sad you have to do that to get a little colour back in the game.

All that said I'm super hopeful for the future of the game and its expansion when it gets released. I would like to see it grow into a much bigger more content rich experience.

After a full year of horrible optimization and texture issues, CapCom finally has the game running right.  The addition of Arch-Tempered Monsters, 9-star (soon to be 10-star) monsters and the re-introduction to an Elder Dragon, Gogmazios, as the last "big" fight of the main game, make this game feel well-rounded and full of "meaningful" content to play the game long-term now.  If you're on the fence about getting this game, as of February 17th, 2026, I would say it's well worth the money, but definitely try and see if you can get it on sale as I still think the full year wait to get this game to run the way it was supposed to, without Frame Generation (♥♥♥♥♥) still leaves a bad taste in my mouth.  They need to deliver an Iceborne quality experience for the DLC WITHOUT any performance issues, Period.

recommendations to anyone who has a high end pc ( Nvidia cards owners ) 
- Turn off HAGS in windows ( trust me bro ) 
- alter config files:
Jobthread= core count ( give windows 4 threads and the rest for the game )  
parallelthread.....= same number
Raymarch....=12 ( it gives the best volumetric fog and minimizes the grainy look without removing or lowering it ) we basicaly added more rays to be calculated :)
look for graphics/raytracing and enable raytraced shadows (thank me later)

PS. WHEN UR DONE DON'T FORGET TO SAVE AND MAKE THE FILE READ ONLY so when u boot the game it won't reset ur settings.

-DOWNLOAD REFramework and the more graphics option and alter everything to 0 but leave shadows to 7 and alter shadows res to 2000+ 
do the Nvidia inspector solution for LOD Bias too ( just ask gemini she will  guide u )

after all this just delete the old shaders and start the game so it can rebuild on the highest quality and get the best performance possible.

While this game had a bumpy release, a year later it finally fixed most of its performance issues (I never experienced any, but i know a few friends who did). World had a pretty similar pc release which is something to keep in mind, so high hopes for expansion release.

The difficulty of the game even before the expansion is now at a good level, making wounds appear a lot less frequently, making wounds on legs not make the monster fall over specifically for 9 star monsters. Exactly like World and Rise, early game is a joke (yes even low rank World Anjanath was easy) but ramps up once you get to those 8 star quests.

The Focus system, while making positioning a bit less important and general gameplay a bit simpler, its still the most fun gimmick in these new gen mh games

Each end game fight feels very clean and not as repetitive as other mh end game, since theres a good chunk of them you can use to grind materials for. With Gogmazios's return, they showed they actually can make a fun and entertaining raid fight.

This game also has the best Monster Hunter fashion. No further explanation needed.

While i never cared for graphics that much in most games if not all, this game can look beautiful at times.

Story in basically all Monster Hunter games have never been that great, even tho this one has the best story, its still like a 6/10 at best. I blame Nata

The weapons movesets are the best theyve ever felt in this game aside from 1 or 2 weapons. As a hammer main in each gen, this one has been by far the most fun.

Overall while I do recommend the game, its still best to wait for a big sale or to wait for the big expansion to come out, as is tradition with these games. But, if you're impatient like i was, then go right ahead and enjoy.

I’ve put a lot of time into Wilds across multiple title updates, and at this point I honestly think this is the best base game any modern Monster Hunter has launched with. A lot of the early criticism wasn’t completely wrong, but it also didn’t tell the whole story. The game people were judging at launch is not the same game that exists now.

First, performance. Yes, it was bad. On PC especially, optimization was rough and not really acceptable for how the game looks. Some of the outrage was definitely inflated by people trying to run it on outdated hardware, but that still doesn’t excuse the launch state. The good news is that the title updates made a real difference over time. On my old 3060 Ti, I originally needed AMD FSR frame generation to sit around 90 FPS. After updates, that went up to about 110 with framegen, and eventually I could run 60 to 70 without framegen at all. I’ve since upgraded to a 5070 Ti and now I can max out my 162 Hz monitor with 2x framegen. So the game does run the way it should now if you have appropriate hardware, it just took longer than it should have.

The “this game is too easy” criticism is also pretty misleading. A lot of people clearly never reached late game. Early progression is accessible, sure, but endgame monsters absolutely hit back. 9* hunts like  [spoiler]Seregios, Lagiacrus, and Gore [/spoiler] are legitimately challenging and honestly comparable to some Master Rank fights from older games. A lot of what people remember as difficulty from older Monster Hunter titles was really just jank. Clunky controls, awkward UI, bad hitboxes, or spending forever running around a map trying to find the monster. Personally I would rather spend my time actually hunting than fighting menus or wandering in circles.

Endgame also deserves more credit than it gets. After all the title updates there’s a good amount of variety, and you’re not forced to grind the same monster over and over. There are multiple things worth hunting depending on what you need.  [spoiler]Gogmazios did get repetitive for a bit, but the addition of 10* quests helped a lot and the loop feels much healthier now. [/spoiler]

As for whether this still “feels like Monster Hunter,” that’s subjective, but to me it absolutely does. The core loop is still there. Learn the monster, improve your execution, optimize your build, repeat. 

Story has never been the main appeal for me, but I did like some of the ideas here.  [spoiler]The Gore Magala situation was interesting, and Gogmazios being hidden under the Oilwell Basin was just cool worldbuilding. That’s about all I personally need from Monster Hunter narrative. [/spoiler]

On the gameplay side, most weapons feel amazing. Combat is smooth, responsive, and satisfying. There are a couple unfortunate exceptions, sorry to Charge Blade and HBG players, but overall weapon design is one of the strongest parts of the game. The Wilds versions of returning monsters are also done really well.  [spoiler]Mizutsune is a great example of a fun returning monster. [/spoiler]

Some complaints like auto navigation on the Seikret are weird to me because it’s optional. If you don’t like it, just don’t use it. The game gives you tools and lets you decide how much help you want.

Overall, Wilds definitely had problems, especially technical ones, but the foundation was always strong. After multiple updates, it now has a challenging endgame, a varied hunt loop, and some of the best moment to moment combat in the series. If you actually stick with it past the early hours, there’s a lot here to enjoy.

[h1]A Weary 'Thumbs Up'[/h1]

I started playing with MH1 when I was 11, stopped, and then started again on MH3U later on in highschool and was hooked ever since. From 3U to 4U, to Generations Ultimate, to World, to Rise, and I can say emphatically without question, this was one of the weakest initial launches of any Monster Hunter I have ever played. Initially, my brother and I played the first 75 hours of the game, and after making it to high rank with really only Aarkvald, and Gore Magala to look forward to for real difficulty and challenge, and Zoh Shia only having the option to repeatedly hunt as an "expansion update" we were insanely disappointed. 

It was not just the optimization issues, which were glaring, and horrendous at times.

There are so many aspects of this game that should have had its features already in it, especially Zoh Shia, online gathering hubs, and so much more. We reached high rank, and we basically just felt like there was nothing to do. We finished everything. In other games, we at least had fun and felt motivated to finish every quest together, especially the multiplayer guild hall quests. 

With all of that being said, after the Gogmazios update, I have become much more happy with the game. It is a little bit better optimized, but now we actually have more challenging fights that I am happy with. Arch Tempered Jin Dahaad, Omega Planetes, and of course Gog Mazios are very welcome additions and have been loads of fun. This is how I wish the end game had been in the beginning. Hopefully this stride keeps going and Wilds can really get its second wind.

I do have other issues with some of the combat moves of the other monsters, and how you have these beautiful, or very interesting designs like La La Barina that are completely wasted because the combat is absolutely braindead, and boring, which is sad for such creative designs like her. Chatacabra being a kind of boxing frog seems very cool as a design, but his combat mechanics/ ai is so simple and dumbed down that it is god awful. I remember almost falling asleep fighting him. Why make such interesting designs only to make the fight a total snooze-fest even in high rank?

Anyways, I initially would not recommend the game at all unless you were a hardcore fan of the franchise and wanted to continue support, but even then it was still terrible. But now, I give a somewhat weary thumbs up.

Okay this review might change down the line once the expansion for Wilds releases so it'll be up until then and until I actually manage to finish it. In the games current state I can at least recommend people try Wilds and see how they feel about it before trying to get the expansion. 

Starting off however, I might be a bit discouraged to even say anything about the state of the game as the launch was ROUGH and filled with performance issues which took several months to almost a year to fix. I don't even want to say "Thanks Capcom for taking the time to fix the game" as these issues were present in a previous game that used the RE Engine for open world and currently still runs WORSE and hardly gets any updates at all to improve on its stability and performance. Only reason Monster Hunter Wilds runs better is because it's more important as an IP to Capcom than DD2. 

https://store.steampowered.com/app/2054970/Dragons_Dogma_2/ 

The funny part however though is that the open world that was marketed to us isn't at all as interesting when you transition between regions. Personally I'd trade loading screens and larger file size if it meant the game ran better but I do appreciate Capcom being ambitious with their game in trying to make it more immersive. Which, speaking of immersion, while visually the game is stunning the UI and Menu and HUD is honestly the WORST it has been in some time. Constant notifications of the changes in environment, weird placement of functions in the menu that you might even need a google search to figure out. SOS quests and quest settings being the largest offenders with now multiplayer quests flooded with people not knowing how to use the SOS system so when large title updates roll out expect to join quests where the host can't be bothered to set it to private or create a link party for their friends if you want to have fun with randoms because they will keep you hanging instead of either accepting or rejecting your quest request. All the stuff you have to read and pay attention to in the UI might be overstimulating especially when hunting which is a shame because part of the charm of say, the chat, was the callout system and stickers which mostly get ignored now as people either conditioned themselves to not looking at it or have mods to silence the chat itself. 

Now to what I actually enjoy about the game, especially as a solo experience because solo? The game is great and close to the best base game monster hunter experience, The monster roster features some amazing new monsters and old ones from previous games and some flagships of course, the ecosystem is vibrant and detailed especially with how beautiful weather changes can be in every region. The story itself may be a hit or miss depending on how you might feel about the MH Lore (personally I'm okay with it) and what direction Capcom seem to be going with it. I do also enjoy the characterization of our hunters as well leaving enough room as well for people to still identify with their character and the flagship monster Arkveld is amazing to fight. 

The combat is still smooth enough to enjoy, even better with the movement improvements and animations to a lot of weapons in the game that make dodging and engaging with the monster with your weapon have that added weight many enjoyed that they felt might have been missing in Rise. Some weapons however might have had their kit simplified which funneled a lot people to stick to one playstyle to have more impact in a hunt and I unfortunately main one of those weapons but again maybe the expansion might bring along changes that address how the weapons function as well. Overall most weapons have changed for the better, the focus system might make hunts feel a bit too easy and seem a bit disruptive when playing with someone who needs focus wounds for their weapon too but I'm hoping with the upcoming expansion the changes to the weapon balancing and abilities addresses that. 

The game is pricey, but I do recommend to fans of Monster Hunter, a lot of stuff has been streamlined and the UI is a mess but the core game is still fun and and the performance since launch has vastly improved thank goodness.

So I will start out by saying that this is my first Monster Hunter game pretty much, I tried Monster Hunter: World way back when that came out for Xbox but my Xbox couldn't really handle it so I gave up.

So far at the time of writing this I have 34.1 hours played in this game, I completed the Low Rank portion of the game and have now progressed onto the High Rank portion. Up until this point my opinion on the game was that it played nice, Monster hunts were fun, The loot made sense and the difficulty kept me interested. 

However, When i got to Mizutsune, I hit a wall. Simply put, I could not kill it or even get it to retreat to a new area once before I died 3 times, Failing the mission. So i went to the drawing board and wanted to improve my build a little bit. That's when I started to learn about the Artian Weapon system. 

From here on you will only read complaints from me: The fact that you need to not only grind the parts to MAKE the weapon in the first place, But you need to grind even MORE to get material to upgrade this weapon so that you can outperform (Even if barely) regular monster weapons.

This would have been salvageable, And at the time I thought maybe I might sink some time into this, Crafting my ideal weapon to be able to push myself further. However, That's when i learned that the Artian Weapon upgrade system is not based on RNG, This is based on a predetermined seed that you get from the moment you create your first character. That is dumb as HELL to put it in the most polite way I can.

I commend all of you that are able to muscle through that horrendous system to continue playing the game and even those of you who choose to not even interact with the system at all, Sticking with regular monster weapons. I just cant do it, I'm not going to spend how ever many hours grinding the materials to make the weapon, Grinding the upgrade material and then save scumming for 10, 20, 50, 200, 500 weapons or whatever it takes to fish for that ideal seed to get what I want. Its just not worth it. At least if this system was based on a random model, I can live with that. I play TONS of games that use an RNG system to acquire things, I'm no stranger to that but this is a first.

After having some back and forth with myself about maybe just muscling through it like people do, I found a subset of people, Like me, Who think this system is utter slop and decide to use external tools like mods to get their ideal rolls. This would have made me continue to play this game, If it wasn't for the fact that Capcom doesn't directly address whether this is bannable. What i'm referring to is a mod called Artian Editor on Nexus Mods. It allows you to pick exactly what stats you want your weapon to have. Naturally, You CAN use this mod to gain an unfair advantage by adding things to your weapon that aren't normally obtainable within the vanilla unmodded game. I did not want to go this direction, I wanted to create my ideal, One time craft, Perfect weapon that is otherwise NATURALLY OBTAINABLE WITHIN THE UNMODIFIED GAME without having to save scum hundreds of hours to get it, Like many others do. But I do not like the risk of ban.

So for now as it is, It does not seem like this system will be changed as they've updated this system to incorporate even higher tier weapons but with the same seed based model.

My rating of the game BEFORE learning about this slop system was a solid 8/10, The gameplay loop was enjoyable and challenging to me and that kept me playing this game exclusively out of my entire library. Not to mention the game is very visually appealing. 

After learning of this system, My review drops to probably a 4/10. If youre okay with save scumming and spending hundreds of hours fighting the same monsters for the same parts to make the same weapons over and over until you get what you need or modding the game and risking the ban OR avoiding the system entirely sticking to monster weapons only, Then you can disregard my entire review and call me a moron. For those of you with limited play time however, Avoid this crap at all costs, I received this game at a discount and I wouldn't even recommend it then.

If this system was implemented this way on a seed based model to retain players then I seriously question the mental capacity of the person who's idea this was. This does NOT respect player time, And is borderline predatory practice.

So I just picked up Monster Hunter Wilds and have been really enjoying the game. I haven't played any Monster Hunter since Worlds. I would recommend the game to any Monster Hunter fan to get it now while Capcom is having it's sale. 

Beyond this I have some more specific feedback below if you're interested but its mostly opinionated.

[b]Performance[/b]: I didn't play before the "optimization patch" specifically because of what I read and heard about the games performance at the time. But my experience with the patch has been the game running exceptionally well with no stutters and easily over 90 fps.

The caveat to this is I am using DLSS and AMD FRS (Frame Gen) with Ray Tracing on and motion blur off. Game settings on high so far but only because I haven't tried "ultra" yet. Either way the game looks phenomenal at these settings for a Monster Hunter game.

[b]Focus Mode[/b]: I know there is a bit of distaste for this mode. Me personally it makes the game feel more like a traditional MMO versus what is normally the Monster Hunter experience that feels closer to a Dark Souls system. Either way it seems like you don't have to use it if you just chad your movement and attacks either way.

[b]Chocobo[/b]: I straight up don't like it - but this is just a me thing as I don't like auto moving mounts. I know you can stop it but it just feels bad to me coming from more traditional MMO mount systems.

Anyways - that's all I have and if you're a Monster Hunter fan that may have not been playing due to fear of performance issues, I personally think that's in a good place now and you should check this game out.

Good Luck out there.

[h1] It's a fine game, but it's not Monster Hunter [/h1]

I'm writing this review immediately after completing Low Rank. Whether or not the game improves in High Rank is irrelevant, because the game has already failed. A game that only gets good after 10 hours is a bad game.

[i]EDIT: Having now finished all the story missions in High Rank in another mere 10 hours, I can confidently say that Low Rank was the best part of the game. I am so glad I bought this on sale; I cannot imagine paying $70 for a 20 hour Monster Hunter game. Capcom should be ashamed.[/i]


[h3]Part 1: The Maps, the Bird, and the Claw[/h3]
The maps in this game are huge, and very detailed. However, as a tradeoff, they are a [i]nightmare[/i] to navigate. Every area looks functionally indistinguishable from on another, and getting from point A to point B is a massive pain. 

You will understand very quickly that the Seikret and the claw shot were not born out of a desire to create more interesting systems, but out of necessity. Yet at the same time, both of those additions only exacerbate the problem. The Seikret's [strike]taxi mode[/strike] auto-tracking takes your hands off the wheel and makes you a passenger rather than an explorer. You won't learn the maps unless you actually walk around them, but reminder, they're [i]huge[/i]. Huge, but largely empty of actual things you would want to interact with, so you use the Seikret just to save time. Anything you see that you would want to interact with you can just grab with the claw shot. You may say, "Well why not just manually control the Seikret while exploring?" The answer is simply that the manual Seikret controls are awful.

[h3]Part 2: Cutscenes and Monster designs [/h3]
This game has too many cutscenes. While the plot is better than World and Rise by my standards, and I liked seeing some aspects of the monster hunting world in more detail, it very much overstayed its welcome. The Disney shoot-em-up dark ride portions where you get to listen to characters yap while clawing resources off the ground are kind of ridiculous. You [i]can[/i] skip the cutscenes. However, the point still stands.

I have mixed opinions on a voiced player character. I did not mind the voice, in fact, I really appreciated not having some annoying secondary character (*cough* handler *cough cough*) acting as my mouthpiece. I'm not a fan of my hunter being John Monsterhunter straight out of the gate (or at all, frankly. It doesn't feel earned.) But I think they did an OK job not making him obnoxious, even if they had to give him a cardboard personality to do it. The cutscene interactions with Nata, while generic, were fine. 

On the subject of Nata, I didn't end up hating him like many other reviews I'd read previously did. Yes, he's annoying, but he's also a traumatized 12 year old who lived in an isolated village his whole life. I took him with a grain of salt, and it was fine.

I have even more mixed opinions on the monster designs. I like most of them, but some fit in very poorly design-wise with some of the established monsters, and some of the established monsters look weird with their grittier reskins. Like, holy hell Congalala looks [i]so wrong.[/i] They also sound significantly more alien/artificial than past monsters. It was super cool to see my beloved Nerscylla again, though.

[h3]Part 3: The Monster Hunting, or Lack Thereof[/h3]
This is the biggest problem I have with Wilds.

You don't hunt monsters in this game. You fight them. The bird tracks the monster for you. The monster's location is always marked on the map, and in an effort to one-up Rise's attempt to kill Mohun's identity, a [i]giant waypoint[/i] is displayed above the monster whenever it gets more than 15 feet away from you. 
Monsters spend significantly more time downed than previous entries (and I am tactfully ignoring the Clutch Claw in this discussion). This is largely due to the new wound system.

The preparation is also gone. Just craft whatever armor pieces you have parts for. You [i]will[/i] have parts for most of them, due to the overabundance of monster drops given by wound breaks, as well as the cheaper material costs. I never once made gear because I needed an edge over a new monster, I just made whatever was available. There is zero need to do quests more than once, and as a result, I competed all of Low Rank in 10 hours.

What about the item pouch? More like I-don't-care-what's-in-my pouch. Hot/cold drinks? Eat a bug from the wall. Low health? Eat a bug off the wall. Statused? Good thing your cat is carrying the entirety of a hospital's medical stockpile on him at all times (there's also an endemic bug for that, believe it or not).

*Sigh.*

Okay, look. I have enjoyed Monster Hunter's previous entries for their unique take on what basically boils down to a "boss-fighting game." Wilds does not [i]remove[/i] these aspects. They're still there. There's just no reason to engage with them. If this changes during my venture into High Rank, it doesn't matter, because [i]you can't half-ass a Monster Hunter game[/i].

[h3]Part 4: Positives [/h3]
Despite what my recommendation says, there [i]are[/i] parts of this game that I like.

I enjoy the seamlessness of leaving camp directly into the wilds. I love the new cephalopod monsters.

I don't hate 90% of the NPCs, which is more than I can say for either of the 5th gen games. In fact, I really enjoyed fighting alongside Olivia in some of the story fights, which I've honestly always wanted from a Mohun game (Sunbreak doesn't count because I couldn't give a damn about any of those NPCs). The facial rigging and voice acting is leagues better than World. Some of the reunion scenes between NPCs after a hunt were actually touching. And dare I say, the plot as a whole is the least egregious compared with Worldborne and Risebreak. Also, the transition from walking simulator/cutscene to combat feels really good and gets me pumped to fight.

I didn't discuss combat beyond wounds, because I don't have much to say about the new mechanics, but I will say that I was apprehensive about the offset attacks, and I actually enjoyed them more than I was expecting. (Focus mode on Greatsword is stupid as hell though, lmao.)

[h3]Part 5: Final Words[/h3]
I really hope Monster Hunter can regain some of its identity with the upcoming Master Rank expansion. There are more little nuisances here and there that I found annoying, but this review is already approaching the character limit.

I did not touch on performance, because at this point, it's just beating a dead horse. It's not good. The engine Capcom has been re-using for almost the past decade is not up to this sort of task. The game, while now more optimized than at release, should never have been released at all. Unacceptable.

I did not touch on online play because I have not tried it.

[h3]In conclusion: there's a good reason why World is still competitive with Wilds in terms of player counts despite being 8 years older.[/h3]

Monster Hunter Wilds: A Beautiful Disaster
Rating: 4/5 (Gameplay) | 1.5/5 (Technical State)

The Fun: The Peak of the Franchise
When the game actually stays open, Wilds is nothing short of incredible. The transition to a fully seamless open world isn't just a gimmick; it changes the rhythm of the hunt entirely.

The Ecosystem: Seeing a weather event like the Sandtide roll in while you’re mid-fight—forcing both you and the monster to adapt—is a level of immersion the series has never reached before.

The Seikret: The new mount is a game-changer. Being able to sharpen your blade or swap weapons on the move keeps the momentum at an all-time high.

Combat: Every weapon feels like it has more weight and "crunch" than ever. The new Focus Mode adds a layer of precision that makes breaking parts feel more intentional and satisfying.

The Frustration: Fighting the Engine, Not the Monster
Unfortunately, the "True Flagship" of this game isn't Arkveld—it’s the Crash to Desktop.

Poor Optimization: Even on high-end rigs, the game is a resource hog. CPU bottlenecks are frequent, and the VRAM management is aggressive, often leading to "origami" textures or massive frame drops in the Scarlet Forest.

Stability Issues: Frequent crashes during transitions or high-particle fights (like the lightning storms) turn a 20-minute epic hunt into a total waste of time.

Upscaling Dependency: The game relies way too heavily on DLSS/FSR to be playable, which often leaves the image looking blurry or "shimmery" unless you have top-tier hardware.

They definitely fixed the performance problems that plenty of people had with the game. But "optimizing" is a double-edged sword and instead of creating a more granular set of graphical options to better include a variety of hardware, they made a LOT of downgrades the the graphics. A lot of people are seeing an increased 10~20 fps, but I'm seeing a lot fewer polygons, all around lower textures even on the high res texture pack (which they cut the size of in half, and not by compression, mind you), and all around made the game graphically look like it belongs a generation behind where the game was at during release.

I'm not gonna comment on performance because you'll likely read more than enough about it in the reviews. All I'm gonna say is I have a pretty beefy rig myself and experienced some pretty bad drops in performance which isn't exactly ideal when the game looks fairly muddy when you start looking up close. That being said.

I think what killed MH: Wilds the most for me was how trimmed down everything felt compared to previous titles. I'm not one for artificial difficulty but if I don't have at least some challenge, then parts of the game just feel boring. There's no researching monsters to identify more things about them to aid in hunts, there's no expeditions to stock on needed supplies for hunts, there's just nothing to add to the hunt other than breaking out a stick and whacking away at it. This might work for the people who want nothing other than a monster arena style game but I don't think those types dominate the franchise.

Another glowing problem with the game is the absolutely boring level design, it's not even close to other games and those who say otherwise are straight up lying. Windward Plains is a wide and empty desert with very little visual variety, you have a small oasis, some caves, lots of wide open spaces, and just overall nothing else going on. Compare this to Wildspire Waste which has the same desert style areas and caves, but also a riverlands, an isolated swamp, some steppes, and more. On the surface they seem similar but it comes back down to how EMPTY the worldspaces of Wilds feel. You have the same issue with Scarlet Forest, Oilwell Basin, Iceshard Cliffs, and the Ruins of Wyveria. But what feels worse is that each respective map starts to feel small and more like a corridor than a map which is a stark contrast to the prior games which were either much more open or at least had more flow to them with intertwining paths. Not to mention a total lack of unique locales such as Coral Highlands or Rotten Vale. It took me a relatively short time to become bored of the world it was making me inhabit.

I hope the expansion truly breathes some life back in the game but I honestly don't have much faith that they will. What a monumental fumble of what could've been a title that surpassed everything before it.
