I'm a huge fan of the franchise, back to the original (2004). It was such a diamond in the rough. I was lucky enough to meet one of the head developers and despite a big language barrier, you could tell this was his baby. There was legit glint of happiness when I cooked in the forest and laughed. Trying to figure out great swords, more like driving a semi than a the standard immediately responsive "spam buttons for combos" of the time. The dev struggled explaining timing and pacing in English. I said "oh ♥♥♥♥" multiple times as Rathalos wrecked me and he just looked at me like "Yeah, he's tough right?". As if this was the point of the demo. To punish but make it clear, victory was always within my grasp. And that was new.  You could tell this was a passion project made by lifetime gamers.

Fast forward to mega corporate sales and dlc era where AAA need to keep up with EA or die, a world where most indy shops introduce innovation and captivating stories, with low overhead and 1, 5, less than 100 man teams. Where is MH now? In many ways it's more accessible and Quality of Life seems to be a staple. Some complexity remains. I'm concerned that "accessibility" is taking more of a front seat, or I'm just seasoned now and the game feels "easy". Either way, it feels "easy". 

Gathering as a chore, for example helps with pacing. Now, just spam the collection key. Don't worry about crafting, you can just auto craft. Cook perfectly? Get 12 perfect stakes, not one. Most probably like the QoL add, but I liked feeling a bit like a juxtaposed gun toting caveman in MHW and prior. This iteration definitely pushes you to more of the "hunt giant monsters quickly" path.

Despite that, I'm only 8 hours in and hoping the difficulty scale increases, so it goes back to those punishing roots as the game goes on. Not just because "one hit you die" but because the monster pattern and range of attacks keep me on my toes and careful with overextending.

I waited until reviews state "system patch" was in. I'm on a moderate machine updated 5 years ago, all settings to default, no texture pack, and running very smooth. It's visually captivating. I'm glad I skipped the rocky launch and I do this method with any game I'd bother to buy these days (buying on steam reviews, works for me. Thanks reprobates.). Kicker, it usually comes with a sale.

If you're new to the series, this is a prefect entry point. If you're the "I even played the PSP games" type, you may find it a bit like a long "tutorial". Either way, if it ramps up and at least has the MHW level of end game, I'll update my review to reflect it.

While I consider this to be one of the weaker entries to the franchise the performance gain from one of the recent updates did solidify this game as a game worth playing overall. As with every modern monster hunter game, the game isn't really complete until the release of an expansion which has yet to come for Wilds.

I do think the game lost some aspects that made the prior games special. As well as new additions not really serving the monster hunter series as we love it. Some of it is stylistic choices and some are gameplay.
+ Gameplay itself is smoother than ever. Game feels for the most part appropriately responsive where it needs to.

+ Anything animated, like monsters or weapon attacks, is better than it's ever been. This is the one thing Capcom consistently nails and improves upon each entry.

+ Audio design is great and music is stellar.

+/- Visuals are great, art direction is pretty good but not as special as MHWorlds. However Capcom has completely ignored performance until quite recently and even then it's severely at the cost of fidelity. It will always be a balancing act between smooth framerate vs good visuals but in this case you have to massively sacrifice one aspect for the other, there's no balance to speak of.

+/- The quests/hunting is for the most part almost entirely seamless, you don't get sent back to the hub after every hunt but that is as much of a blessing as it is a curse. You tend to have to fast travel back anyways to restock on supplies, perhaps build/upgrade weapons/armor and put away the extra accumulated items you've picked up along the way that is just taking up your inventory. The idea of reducing downtime as much as possible is set back by the reality of it.

- The food prep mechanic feels like it lost it's magic. It went from a buff cat serving you the best grilled meat ever and an old grandma cat making the best stew to just you alone with a frying pan. This kind of also has been affected by the less downtime approach I spoke of earlier.

- The game has lost some of its apparent difficulty and it's due to a lot of small adjustments everywhere that ultimately have made it easier. Heavy weapons feel less clunky and faster (so they're easier to use and require less strategy). Monsters in general feel more predictable in attacks and design. Being staggered or knocked down provides you with temporary invulnerability which makes it more fair in a sense but it means you'll always get out of a bad situation. Difficulty tends to go up with expansions both in fair and unfair ways so this might change in the future.

- The environment and world design doesn't really feel alive. They nailed this with MHWorld but I'm starting to believe that was a fluke, which is a shame because this is the same dev team as that game.

- They technically made an interconnected world this time around for the most part but don't utilize it, like at all. It's like how The Last of Us technically has no loading screen but it's because they lock you into a cutscene or animation that masks a level transition. MHWorld had the guiding lands which was an endgame map that was the combination of every previous location and while execution was shoddy, the concept isn't. Make the world expansive and interconnected with paths to other locations spread across it.

- The linear bombastic story is genuinely the worst part of the entire MH franchise and they've doubled down on it. MH Rise in my opinion did it better but it's likely due to their more limited budget and thus it wasn't as deeply developed. The world itself is interesting, the monsters are interesting, some dumb poorly written child being the chosen one isn't. The protagonists have never really been the main character of Monster Hunter so to speak, it's always been about the world, the monsters, the hunt and the gear you can create from hunting said monsters.

Overall by now it's an entry worth playing and my negative points of the game is from the lens of someone who's grown up with the series. 

The perfect MH game doesn't exist and will never exist because they insist on changing things up between every entry and that will never not be frustrating lol but it is what it is. Enjoy what makes this entry uniquely good and say goodbye to it in the next entry even if it's by the same studio like World and Wilds (Rise was made by the secondary studio). The end result will always be Monster Hunter but they will be different flavors of Monster Hunter, and Capcom will probably ignore or forget the special ingredients that made the previous games uniquely good. I didn't find this entry as good and addicting as World or Rise but that doesn't rule out that I might love the next one.

[h1] Monster Hunter Wilds - Veteran Review (High-Crit Taste) [/h1]

[b]Veteran players:[/b] I’m genuinely impressed — they did it again.
Monster Hunter: World was the only other game in the franchise I played, and once again the [b]level of production, care, and attention to detail[/b], especially in the [b]Areas[/b], is something you rarely see today.
Everything feels [b]alive[/b].

Last year I had been busy with other things and only returned to the game now in February 2026. From what I read at some point last year (2025), there was a period where an update caused problems for many players, but I was lucky I wasn’t exposed to that window — and from what I can see, whatever happened there is not present anymore. [b]Nothing negative stood out[/b] to me when I came back.

The solid foundation — gameplay, Areas, Quality Of Life for mechanics, diverse ways of fight — is all [b]there[/b], and it’s strong enough to carry the entire experience.

[h2] PROS [/h2]

[b]– The Areas are a MASTERPIECE.[/b]

Honestly, [b]Top 2 / Top 3 of all time[/b], together with
Red Dead Redemption 2 and Monster Hunter: World.
They’re alive, extremely well planned, crafted, and produced.
If you have high standards and good taste, you’ll immediately understand what I mean (together with the other points I mention).

[b]– Gameplay, map system, and playstyle variety are excellent.[/b]

Everything feels intentional and flexible. The combat system is very interesting and dynamic and TOTALLY changes according to the weapon of choice, it's another game and another set of combo options for each weapon,  It's a noticeable improvement vs Monster Hunter World, that wasn't that bad. It takes a little bit of time to adapt tho, if it's your first Monster Hunter game, but when it becomes second nature to you, the fight vibe, fun, and etc. becomes so good and cinematic, man.

[b]– Quality of Life (QoL) for the menu mechanics is solid.[/b]

There are a few small points that could be improved, but overall they gave a very interesting amount of attention to it, and the system supports the experience instead of fighting against it.

[b]– The game is already great, and only needs small refinements to be PERFECT.[/b]

[h2] CONS (nuances — not exactly "flaws", but points that would make this game Top 3 of all time) [/h2]

[b]– More Areas at this quality level would elevate the game even further.[/b]

If Capcom releases one new Area every ~6 months with the same craftsmanship and attention to detail?
I’d gladly pay for it without thinking twice! — and I’m not someone who buys DLC unless it’s well-earned.
And this would be one of those cases.
From a writing perspective, it could be called Expeditions, Exploration trips, etc., or another region, I don't know. But the expansion of this world would be amazing. 

[b]– The Map as whole is impressive and truly remarkable. But Map filtering could be more efficient.[/b]

Filtering for things like traps or gathering points currently takes 4–5 clicks (with controller).
If the icons could be displayed horizontally instead of stacked vertically, this could drop to 2–3 clicks (with controller).
A simple toggle between compact and expanded styles would make the map perfect. And don’t get me wrong, the map is AMAZING — if not the most impressive one in the game industry. Easily Top 3 of all time, not only in terms of visuals, but system-wise too. But there’s still room for improvement, like this point right here; without it, the experience kind of breaks sometimes because, as an example, you need to switch from 'Traps' filtering to 'Gathering' filtering, and this flow could be way better with this subtle ‘vertical compact to horizontal expanded’ toggle suggestion.

[b]– Multiplayer improved vs World, but lacks the old MMO-social feeling.[/b]

I miss when players actually lived in the world together. Entering the city together or whatever.
And a good reference, could be a system like Dying Light’s co-op (to activate the next part of the story, etc, there's only one way: everyone standing in the activation zone, 2m square, to progress missions) would solve the issue of one impatient player triggering things for the group, and everyone missing the context and ruining the "Co-Op" experience — the Borderlands 2 problem.

Maybe something for the next entry in the World/Wilds branch, but the feedback stands.

[h2] FINAL THOUGHTS [/h2]

All my “cons” are micro-refinements.
This is already an [b]excellent game[/b], and the foundation is strong enough that a few improvements would push it into [b]legendary territory[/b].

They genuinely crafted something special here.

95% of all reviews you will see all say the same thing: "Optimization is bad, fix pls." This is no longer an issue. These reviews can be completely ignored as they are out of date. 

I have decided to edit my review in light of me replaying Monster Hunter: World.

I still highly recommend Wilds, even though there are things that it falls short on compared to World. For example, the setting of Wilds feels very small. All the areas you go to are nowhere near the size of any area in MH:World. They are nowhere near as interesting and as refined either. And another thing to go along with that is the actual hunting and tracking system. In Wilds, you know where the monsters are at all times. It's like you have a SR-71 Blackbird marking enemies on your mini-map at all times, and your Seikret also functions as an Uber straight to the monster. In World, you must find tracks first in order to know where the monster is (Unless you fully research a monster, which is done through tracking it enough and hunting it). Which at least for me feels significantly more interactive and leads to me getting lost and just exploring (In a good way). You can find tracks in Wilds, but they only reward you with Guild Points and nothing else. Its almost like they wanted to implement the same system but never got around to it. 

Now for the Canteen. Wilds has the superior Canteen for one reason only: You don't lose all buffs when you cart (Except for insurance). In World, you lose ALL buffs when you cart, making it significantly more punishing. This wouldn't be an issue if the health buff was given no matter what you ate but in order to receive it you MUST eat fresh ingredients which are limited once you are in a hunt meaning you cant get back to maximum health unless you use max or ancient potions which are hard to come by in early to mid game. Wilds canteen is permanent until it runs its timer out, a much better and more forgiving food system. though I do miss getting random things to get new food items. You are also able to cook your own food at any point during the hunt, you just do not have access to the really good extra food buffs if you do this. Also the Canteen in Wilds is just Doordash since you use Vouchers to order your meals from the villages and a bunch of Palicos run it all the way to the Gathering hub for you to eat. You can be invited by a village to eat for free if you are lucky. I hope we get a different canteen where the Palicos can cook for us again because I miss it. (There are cutscenes for when the villages make you food, I just want to see the Palicos do it because it feels more whimsical).

There isn't really a reason to fully explore each area multiple times for a few reasons. Mainly, they are all so small, but there is no extra incentive to do so. In World, there were random rare gathering points (That weren't too convoluted to harvest). And multiple other kinds that felt rewarding to harvest and farm. Wilds has some of these, but they don't feel nearly as rewarding and fun to harvest.  

In World, you had access to your own garden boxes that you could use to farm any plant or mushroom in the game in bulk to save some of the more annoying grinds and was also not too hard to upgrade or upkeep. In Wilds, you have to ask the villages you find if they can find these materials for you, but its not guaranteed if they'll get it depending on the item, and this changes at seemingly random, this system super sucks and I want my garden boxes back.

Even with all the "bad" things I have said about Wilds, it still has a huge leg up in the most important department: Gameplay. There is a lot of new things they have added to all weapons to make them significantly easier to use for the average player, and new ways to aura farm for those that truly understand their weapon of choice. All weapons feel much smoother and more powerful than their World counterparts with new mechanics for weapons capable of Guarding, and a new Counterstrike system for weapons that cant block, adding to the list of tools at your disposal. 

Both games are good. Monster Hunter is good. You should play more Monster Hunter, you will be satisfied with either one.

This game might be not just my favorite Monster Hunter, but [b] one of my favorite video games of all time. [/b]

Most of the problems people seem to have with this game come from the fact that this game just does things very differently than World, most notably the sanding-down or outright removal of a lot of mechanics. While enough people seem to have those opinions that I don't feel right making fun of them, I do feel like they're wrong.

My first Monster Hunter WAS World. I tried to give it a fair shake, but kept getting bogged down by the mechanics that Wilds sanded down. Why do I have to pick at poop and tracks just for the game to outright tell me where the monster is? Why do the fights last so long that I wonder if the game is genuinely broken? Why does just hitting the monster feel clumsy and unintuitive?
When I brought these up to the friend that was trying to introduce me to the game, they responded "it'll click for you after the first 20 hours of gameplay."

[b] EXCUSE ME? [/b] If someone tried to say that about any other game, I'd throw a cinderblock at them for being stupid and defending an objectively bad game. Wilds is absolutely correct for addressing all of those mechanics.

But enough about World, let's talk about why I love Wilds.
-The fights START easy and properly get harder at a steady pace instead of just having a series of difficulty spikes, mirroring a player's growing experience. It might be TOO easy at first, but the challenge is absolutely present in the late game with monsters like Omega Planetes.
-Every weapon is fun and responsive, large in part due to Focus Mode. I don't feel like a bumbling idiot just trying to swing a sword, but hitting things like True Charged Slash still feels earned because the commitment to each hit isn't any smaller.
-The grind is a lot shorter for almost all gear, which I like. This is a pain point for some, but to that I say: How many times did you have to fight that same monster for its 1-2% drop in older games, and how much of that was spent wanting to do literally ANY other content? You've ALL had that moment, don't act like it didn't happen. And it's STILL a thing in Wilds with Gog Artian weapons and grinding for random god rolls if you want a wall to bash your head on.
-The game is just approachable. I want to play this WITH someone, to share the game with them. It's a good "first Monster Hunter" for people wanting to try it, which World absolutely is not.

FINALLY the PC optimizations have happened. 
The game didn't run well before the multiple performance patches, requiring framegens AND upscaling, and was bottlenecked on the most powerful CPUs that you could buy (and that was BEFORE the CPU shortage!)
Now that Capcom has put in some work to make it run, it runs well. 

TIME FOR THE REVIEW.
STORY-
The story is ass. But you're not here for it, are you? There's a bunch of unskippable cutscenes with some kid whining about monsters and circle of life and all. Sure it's realistic; a sheltered kid who knows next to nothing about nature is going to draw some erroneous conclusions.
Still annoying as hell, and Nata needs to let the professionals do their job.
It's basically a similar story to World and MH4 but done WAY worse. The entire plot can be summed up as "Who would've thought that this natural creature we found in the ecosystem... was a natural part of the ecosystem!? It's a mystery!"

GAMEPLAY-
Every weapon feels absolutely amazing. There's some pain points here and there, like Great Sword not being able to Overhead > Slinger > True Charge like they could in World, or LBG/HBG having NO recoil skills, so their spread 3 still makes you fall flat on your butt every shot. But every weapon has some incredible new moves, including their Wound mechanic moves which do a large chunk of damage and almost always guarantees a good 10s knockdown.
Some weapons rely on wounds much more than others- Great Sword gets a free True Charge, Longsword gets an attack boost, but others like Dual Blades only get a cool animation, and lance just deals slightly more damage.
Knowing which weapons most benefit from a wound and which can pass them up will be the biggest multiplayer friction point for a while, I think.
Also, the whole "Capture/Kill" debate. At this point, with how easy it is to get Carver, and how often it procs, killing is going to be WAY better 99 times out of 100.

DIFFICULTY/GRIND-
Until you hit endgame, the game is a cakewalk. People have made youtube videos showing that the NPC helpers can play and win the game for you.
Once you hit endgame, the difficulty spikes hard, and the grind becomes insane. If you want to make 10 weapons, you need 50 hunter tickets. At 1-2 per quest (3 if you're lucky/use a voucher) that's going to take a LONG while.
Of course, if you look up a guide on what is "The meta", and make ONLY that armor set + your 1 meta weapon, you'll be sitting around whining there's nothing to do in short order, aside from your deco RNG grind.

FUN-
The game is super fun. Even after putting 200+ hours into this on PC (and another 100 on console), I still log in just to help randoms with their low rank quests just for the fun of it, trying out new weapons and having a good ol' time.
Plus I'm sure it feels great for the low level guy to be struggling on that one monster, dying over and over, getting frustrated, and then someone comes in and gives it the beatdown of a lifetime. I know it felt that way for me when I was new to the game.

As a New-gen hunter (getting fairly decently into world and iceborne) and putting in a solid amount of time into wilds (450 hours as of the time of writing), I feel very comfortable in saying that this game is truly solid and great and would be an excellent starting point.

The fluidity of combat with the help of Focus Mode, while not something that was emphasized in previous entries (which focused more on your exact positioning), is something that really adds and emphasizes the character fantasy aspect of each of the weapons. To me, each weapon has something that makes it feel COOL to go at each of the monsters in this game, so that when you master the weapon, the monster, or even both, its a wonderful dance each time you hunt.

I think visually it is a bit hit or miss. I might have to fiddle with my settings more but the colors are a bit too washed out for me at time, or that the way the game is stylized is maybe a TAD too realistic. The game was truly incredible visuals when it wants to though. Rey Dau's blue crystals on its wings, Uth Duna's body is a very intoxicating and envigorating shades of red and blue, and certain enviroments like the Windward plains during the day time can be very breathtaking. 

I personally have not had an insanely big issue with the games performance throughout it's entire time being out, not in a way that impeded what I wanted from the game (hunting and feeling cool as I practiced and mastered certain weapons and monsters). But even on that front they've improved it so much with the recent patches.

If you enjoy your grinding and you enjoy feeling cool, with maybe some multiplayer on the side, its worth your time.

If you asked me, say... Six months ago if Monster Hunter Wilds was worth the money, I would've told you no. I've been playing this series for twenty years, played every version that was localized to the west and even played some that weren't. Capcom still has a long, LONG way to go before they've earned my trust back as a whole, but that said, they have made great strides in optimizing the game and making it actually, genuinely playable without me needing to shell out several hundred dollars just to even upgrade my GPU to something from as late as a 30XX series-era card. If they can manage to release more games in this series to PC in an actual polished, optimized state (looking at you DLC frame checks...) in the future, then I will be more than happy to buy those as well. That said, this is about Wilds, and honestly Monster Hunter has never been better, even as a grumpy old veteran hunter who remembers when 'these games were hard, blahblahwahwah'. This is genuinely a bonified treat, the story is a little weak but it's still the best monster hunter's ever had (outside of Stories, of course). Making a build for your favorite weapon has never been so approachable, and even if the early game feels like you're just kinda slapping these poor beasties around, eventually it'll be your turn. As someone who has loved monster hunter since 3U I do think wilds is a very very good game, but one that is sadly flawed in a lot of small ways that stem from their desire to Modernize the gameplay

Combat: The weapons feel truly at their best in wilds, bouncing from one to the other left me very interested in almost every weapon, HOWEVER. Monster health, especially in Low Rank tends to leave you hungry for more action as the monster plops dead around 7 minutes in. I have no gripes with the Difficulty of low rank, I am an old hand, obviously to me its going to be a cake walk, but I do believe it is an active disservice to new players to give them monsters that act as simple punching bags instead of something to make them plant their feet a little more and adapt

Map Design: The focus on Verticality reaaaaally hurts this games maps man, the map i most enjoy playing on is the Windward Plains, a map that feels much more like World's map design, a large sprawling landscape with small secrets and areas in areas that trail up and down. The heavy focus on this makes maps a bit of a pain to navigate and makes readibility kind of a challenge when just trying to find items or things like Campsites

Story: Its alright, I dont have as much of a hate boner for it as most others, its quaint and simple as all monhun stories tend to be, HOWEVER. What I absolutely despise about this AND World's stories is the utter insistence that your hunter is an old hand at hunting. Ripe with experience and a hard-assed personality. Only to get mopped a low rank monster. This concept to me, is sooooo boring, alot of the charm of old monhun was being a greenhorn, fresh off the boat, in nothing but your skinnies or Iron armor and everyone viewing you as this fresh meat to be chewed up and spit out only to work your way up through the ranks and prove them all wrong for doubting you. As well, Giving the hunter a personality was a POOR DECISION. In a series known for allowing you to create literal monsters in character creator why would you shoe horn such lovingly crafted freaks of nature to be the Straight man in any situation. Finally, STOP RELEASING THESE GAMES WITH NO REPEATABLE FINAL BOSS QUEST OH MY ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ GOD, This is the SECOND ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ GAME WHERE THE FINAL BOSS WAS LACKLUSTER, THIS IS A GAME LITERALLY ONLY ABOUT BOSS FIGHTS FOR ♥♥♥♥ SAKE GIVE THE DEVS MORE TIME TO PROPERLY FINISH THEIR FINAL CHALLENGE I LOVE ZOH SHIA BUT JESUS.

Visual Design: Genuinely, all the monsters look so ♥♥♥♥♥♥ fantastic, I adore the overall alienistic vibe the whole of the Forbidden Lands gives off, you really do feel like youre stepping into unfamiliar territory and that these new beasts are something that no hunter before has even had the chance to spar against.

Characters: Simple, fun, Alma needs to stop talking sometimes after i cart though I be getting tilted. But i genuinely do like most of them. Its cool that Werner looks like OneyPlays

Music/Sound Design: Oh my ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ goooooooooooooooooooooooooood radiant cry of life is soooooooooo good. Wilds compositions are top notch It really brings the whole thing together. Monster noises? Top notch as always, every noise these freaks creak out really brings it home just how obtuse the Forbidden Lands are, I mean, the SOUNDS Rey Dau makes as he flies are almost hypnotic, theyre so strangely pleasant to the ear. We always be praising MonHun Sound Effects team.

Graphics: Look Gorgeous after the most recent update! But before? Yikes dude... i feel like it was sony more pushing to really make this series a graphical powerhouse so I truly cant blame them but still I woulda been fine if this game was just a slightly more polished World Look than Computer Melter 8 Million

I've abstained from giving my thoughts publicly for sometime on Wilds as I am a hugely biased MH fan. But I can say with how many of my friends I have introduced to not only Wilds but the series as a whole that this game is massively worth it for anyone even mildly interested in the franchise. One of the best starting points for a new hunter, narratively its weak but has points that makes it much more interesting than previous titles. The weapon system/focus system are as advanced as ever and yet seamless in gameplay, to the point I dare call it 'immersive' despite having the reticle in front of you. The ecology aspect is a huge selling point and it delivers better than previous titles but falls short due to the lackluster 'hunting' mechanics that were present in its predecessor (MH World).

All in all this game rates a non-biased 7/10, and a personal score of 9/10. I love every aspect of this game, the monetization is a bit much & they took forever to make sure that it is playable to a larger audience due to performance, but I am at a point where I can guilt free recommend this game to others.

My honest review of the game so far. As a Monster Hunter fan since MH: World, it's a great entry, especially after all the title updates for the base game. Capcom listens to the community of this game and I am excited for the expansion--whenever that may be...
Pros: 
- Pretty lighting 
- Extremely fun combat
- Great animation
- Pretty good story for a main MH entry
- Really good base game
- Honorable mention: the expansion will probably be incredible if its as good as MH World: Iceborne and provide so much more content, including higher difficulty (Master Rank or G-Rank). 

Cons: 
- Map designs are fine, but can feel a bit dull (especially compared to World). Also, the whole "open world" aspect that they pushed is pointless because the maps are just connected via long corridors that you truly never will use except for story. I fear this takes away much needed performance but it may not so idk. 
- Game is pretty heavy for low-end systems so good luck (it is a lot better than at launch)

I wanted to like the game.  I loved "Rise."  "Wild," however...maps are too big.  That's not a bad thing, but...they're boring.  Sand..sand...sand.  Or ash,..ash...ash.  Or water...water...water.   They're too big (essentially) to learn routes around...and why bother when the mount will automatically take you where you need to go.  I play Insect Glaive...love it.  BUT---it's nigh impossible to get all my colors.  I aim the bug at the body part of the color I want...but by the time my bug gets there, the mob has moved ALL OVER THE PLACE!  Yes, I know "marking" but that's (for me) shaky at best and hard to do given the mob NEVER STOPS MOVING.  Right now, I'm doing the alien robot and the "oil dragon."  WHEN WILL DEVS LEARN?!?!?  Putting the players in a space they cannot escape, against a monster that can cross that space in 1 jump in 1/2 a second (while it takes me 7+ seconds to make the same journey on foot)--is not "fun" and is not "balance."  And that monster does inescapable area-wide AoE blasts.  Can I do that?  No...I cannot.  That is not "fun" nor is it "balance."  And when I have to stop and drink a healing potion...that takes 7+ seconds to achieve, during which time the mob leaps across the room 3x and does 3 inescapable AoE blasts on each landing....That is not "fun" nor is it "balance."

Challenge is one thing.  Stupid "punish the players for playing" is quite another.

As someone who played every Main Title since 3U I really dont get the hate for the game.

The campaign is ok, but MH was never meant to have deep story. The game structure is more in line with MH: World.

You have to play the story and unlock more and more hunts for the multiplayer hub. It might feel more like the campaign is a huge tutorial but for those who like to coop campaigns it is a bit easier to do than in World (no longer need to both start the mission separatly and than rejoin after the unskipable monster cinematic). 

I like the old structure with separate story and multiplayer hubs but at least the cinematics are skipable now :-)

Base Wilds is a bit easier than Base World difficultywise and more in line with Base Rise.

I like the monster lineup alot. Battle feel more fluid. Weapons got some good quality of life. Early Decoration Armor (I hate to look like a clown until after the base game or worse after the expansion).

You now get deco armor mid story and deco weapon near the end of the story.

Farming for gear and other mats got alot easier but as someone who has a job and responsibilities I welcome that ;-)

I am eager to see what the true endgame after master rank looks like.

I can guess the minimal requirements are a bit off though. You should at least bring some rig that is comparable to a PS5.

Have some mid class pc myself and after updating drivers the game ran smooth (even better now after the recent patch) from the get go.

On a side note: Set your game language to Japanese and the text to whatever you a comfortable with. Japanese Palicos are cuteness overload :-)

Monster hunter wilds feels like it sanded down every rough edge monster hunter had until all that's left is combat. Grinding has been reduced to the point where farming gear takes around four hunts for an entire set. Exploration and map knowledge where made irrelevant by the Seikret autopiloting you to the monster. The focus mode makes positioning irrelevant because you can track your attacks in any direction all the time. Gathering is made irrelevant by the ability to gather at range while auto piloting. Making weapons is irrelevant because they added RNG weapons that outclass everything else at the third village.  Every possible sticking point between you and doing the story quest is gone and without it you are left with a short easy boring campaign. Friction in games is good, you need barriers to overcome and goals to work towards. Part of the satisfaction of monster hunter is learning how to get around these hurdles. Making a set to gather ingredients and doing side quests to unlock the farm was infinitely more satisfying than yoinking them from Seikret back. Grinding to get gear slows you down makes you learn the monster movesets and adds to the play time. Learning the map makes you feel more immersed and rewards people who put in the effort. Wilds looked to MH: World and MH: Rise and learned the wrong lessons from both. The immersion, charm, and attention to detail pail in comparison to World. The combat and buildcraft can't hold a candle to Rise. MH: Wilds, is a caricature of Monster Hunter with a boring story, forgettable character and a pathetic runtime. The game also runs terribly even if in the future this game is patched into a playable state we can't forget that this is what people spent 70$ on. Just play Rise and World they are infinitely better than this sludge.

Played it on release for a week or two, was fun but it ran so bad it made me feel motion sick so I put it down for months. Recently there was a patch supposed to update the performance and I heard that it was great, reinstalled it to try it myself and... it runs slightly better, but still not what I'd consider acceptable. On 3060 and 5700x3D running at 2560x1080 (21:9) I can barely get stable 60fps when I set absolutely everything to lowest with the most intense upscaling. At which point the game looks like a blurry muddy mess. And the 60fps is only stable when there's nothing happening, in combat it dips to 40 and even below that. If I slap FSR framegen on top of it I can get 90ish frames out of combat and 60-70 in combat but the blurry mess turns into a smeary blurry mess to the point where my eyes hurt when I try to play it for more than a hunt or two. I guess it will either look bad or run bad, most likely both at once. Not as bad as on release but even with the performance "fixes" I still regret buying it and would not recommend it to anyone. A pity because there are some gameplay systems I really do enjoy relatively to earlier entries, but I can't see myself picking this over Rise or GU when I feel like playing some MonHun.

Gogmazios fight sucks in general, but whoever came up with phase 3 should never come up with ideas for the rest of their lives. The monster constantly plants bombs on the ground, but you have to look up to attack it. If you don't attack enough, the DPS check will wipe you out. But if you focus on shooting, the bombs on the ground will kill you. This requires fast camera movement, but the game is designed to be played with a controller, which sucks at camera movement. Not to mention you have to aim to damage the monster, and aiming with a controller sucks. 

Also, many weapons are designed to make use of timing moves with monster attacks, either with offsets or well-timed dodging. This monster rarely targets you for attacks. As a DB user, I spend like 70% of the fight without the dodge buff because the monster almost never targets me, and I have to go out of my way to wherever it's attacking to dodge and get the buff. Who thought having 8 hunters in the same fight was a good idea? I can't even get rid of them to solo it.

"proof of a hero" being played in phase 3 is an insult to the great soundtrack.

The rest of the game is decent, although "hunting" elements that were in older games are almost nonexistent here. The game used to be about tracking, studying and making use of the monsters and the environment, and equally about fighting. It is now about riding a bird taxi to the monster then hitting it until it can't move anymore. Still fun, but way less fun than the former. I don't even remember the last time I went on an expedition to gather minerals and bones. The constant fighting is wearing me out very quickly.

Also, water monsters changing areas by entering water is one of the worst things in the game. Huge waste of time because they always move to the area above the waterfall, which is usually very far.

It's not a bad game, but it's a disappointing experience that feels like Monster Hunter with the teeth filed off. Combat feels stale and unrewarding, unable to decide if monster fights should be a cake-walk or a 1-hit-kill slog. Gearing is at it's worst in the end game, as it depends on rolling well on the gear as you make it, which sucked in World and they doubled down on it here thinking they could do wrong.

 Stutters, crashes, inconsistent frame rates; Wilds launched like it was intended not for modern hardware, but some sort of dream rig that doesn't exist (and probably will never thanks to component shortages.) The game devs still haven't really been able to sort this out, and basically blew it's initial good will with some fans waiting for a fix

I would love to have to eat my words here, but I don't even think a future expansion is going to be able to turn this game around. Many of these problems are core to this entry. As a whole; Wilds is a miss for me. I'd much rather play Rise, World, or even Generations.

Monster Hunter Wilds – A Wild, Rewarding, and Deep Hunting Experience

Recommended ✔

TL;DR:
Monster Hunter Wilds delivers amazing combat, huge weapon and build variety, creative monsters, challenging hunts, smart use of environments, full offline play, and a crazy-good story. Despite some pacing issues with monster travel, occasional forced party content, and minor performance problems (now fixed), the overall experience is extremely rewarding. Beating a tough creature never gets old.

Monster Hunter Wilds is one of those games that truly understands what progression and gratification mean.

From the very beginning, you’re thrown into a massive world full of incredible creatures, each with unique behaviors, weaknesses, and fighting styles. The diversity of weapons alone is insane — every weapon feels like a completely different game. Combine that with armor skills, decorations, and builds, and you get near-endless customization.

You’re never locked into one playstyle. Want to be aggressive? Defensive? Support? Pure DPS? Everything is viable if you learn your weapon.

 What I Loved

Huge weapon and build diversity
Every weapon has depth, personality, and mastery curves. Builds matter a lot and reward experimentation.

Creative monsters and encounters
The creatures feel alive. Each hunt is memorable, and learning their patterns is part of the fun.

Smart use of the environment
Traps, terrain, verticality, and map features actually matter. Positioning and awareness are rewarded.

Offline mode (big win for me)
Being able to play fully offline is a massive positive. Not everyone wants or can play online all the time.

Crazy but awesome story (no spoilers)
The story is surprisingly engaging — weird in the best way — and blends perfectly with the gameplay loop.

Real sense of achievement
Defeating a hard monster after multiple attempts feels AMAZING. Few games deliver this level of satisfaction.

Difficulty that feels fair
The game challenges you, but rarely feels cheap. Success comes from learning, not luck.

What Could Be Better

Monster travel downtime
Sometimes monsters spend too long moving between map areas, which hurts pacing.

Some content feels party-dependent
A few hunts are noticeably harder solo, almost pushing you into multiplayer.

Performance issues (mostly fixed now)
There was noticeable slowdown caused by DLC-related problems, but this has been corrected after discovery.

Unnecessary mod usage by some players
Not a game issue directly, but some players use mods that feel pointless or immersion-breaking.

Final Thoughts

Monster Hunter Wilds is a deep, challenging, and incredibly rewarding experience. It respects your time, your skill, and your creativity. If you enjoy mastering systems, experimenting with builds, and earning your victories through effort, this game is absolutely worth it.

Despite a few pacing and technical hiccups, the core experience is rock solid.

Highly recommended for both veterans and newcomers to the series.

This is the first Monster Hunter game I really got into and is currently my favorite. I first started with Rise which didn't feel great initially, and that kept me off the Monster Hunter radar for a while. When this game released I was skeptical if I'd like it as much as Rise. Beautiful. The staff have outdone themselves. I bought a 4060 RTX laptop for this and it didn't disappoint me. Lots of things to do for oldies like me (though the GogArtian grind is a little steep).

Don't get me wrong, I'm still very miffed that it took a year to get the game into a playable state for everyone. While I did not experience performance issues big enough to prevent me from playing at all, I know that was not the case for a lot of people. Launching in that state and then taking a year to fix it was completely unacceptable. Not only for the obvious reasons but it also sets a precedence to other game developers that this kind of behavior is ok. The only reason I am giving a pass this time, is because the latest patch to fix the performance issues was so good that I can now play at 4k at max settings and still get 120 FPS. Whatever you guys did, you did it very well and the game runs INCREDIBLY well. this wasn't just a stability patch, it feels like the game was completely fixed and tuned up from the inside out. a year ago, I finished the main story and did a little post game but i have been sitting here and WAITING for a reason to get back into the game and dive full force into what the rest has to offer.

As for the rest of the game, its fun, it's addicting and I know the bar was low to begin with, but this was the most tolerable plot to a monster hunter game by far. past games had decent plots but INSUFFERABLE characters like the Handler. Or decent characters but crap plots that didn't matter or grab any interest.
This game is much improved on many fronts. Alma is a sweetheart, Gemma is awesome and I normally find the cutesy animal sidekick annoying in most cases but my palico is pretty cool so I'm fine with him. he needs to get off his ass and heal me more in battle though. Hot take, but I don't hate Nata. I get why others do, he can be annoying but I've seen far worse and there are a couple heart touching moments later in the story. The story, while nothing to write home about, serves it's purpose and isn't too intrusive to the fun of the gameplay. Except for the cats in the jungle, i hate those guys they're annoying, but their section of the game is pretty easy to power through and then you don't really have to think about them ever again.

There are two core monster hunter aspects thought that have prevented me from being able to play previous games that are still present here:
1) The UI is THE WORST UI IN GAMING HISTORY. I'm mostly talking about past Monster Hunter games here but the UI in this entry is barely better.
Even after having a friend guide me through the horrific amount of poorly designed menus and charts, my eye still has trouble figuring out where to land on these screens and there are far too many design choices that make it hard to even figure out what I'm looking at half the time. This game is slightly better than all previous games, but oh my god, there has to be a better way to organize the sheer volume of information in a way that's actually digestible.
Finally the sheer atrocious volume of text has thankfully been toned way down for this game. Past games used to have more reading in it than just about any other game i can think of, and that includes interactive novel games. All to teach you basic mechanics. What one game would take a few sentences and a little GIF or photo to teach the player, past Monster Hunter games would take 4 full pages of pure text for some reason. There are still a few moments like that in the beginning of the game, but it's far more tolerable in this entry.
2) The grind. Again, I'm more talking the previous Monster Hunter games here, the grind in this game is better than past entries but it's still a big ask Even if you don't try to create sets for all the weapons and just focus on a few like i did, there is still a lot of grinding you have to do just to make future hunts slightly more manageable. If you want to explore everything in this game, be prepared to put in some serious hours. if you want to max out more than a handful of weapons and armor sets, be prepared to square that number of hours...im exaggerating but the crazy thing is, not by much...

I know it sounds like I've been complaining a lot and I have, but all this to say; Monster Hunter games used to be the least welcoming franchise to new players in the past and this game finally learned how to not turn away newcomers and I respect the effort for that. If you've always wanted to try a Monster Hunter game, this is your best bet to learn the gameplay loop and mechanics without being overwhelmed.
This is easily my favorite entry in the franchise and while I'm still not happy it took a year to fix it, I am happy the wait is finally over and I can start the post game and do some grinding and just enjoy the game again going forward.

PERFORMANCE:

Alright. Time to leave my review now. Seeing how Capcom handled most of World, I expected issues to settle eventually and I'm glad to say they lived up to that expectation. I'm writing this review to contribute to the wealth of positive reviews claiming significantly improved performance, finally. 

I feel my case is worth stating among them as I'm running a Ryzen 5600x, 16 DDR4 RAM rated for 3200mhz, and a 1070 ti. I have a feeling many might be in my position and wanting to know if their aged rig can run this game. I'll tell you this TL;DR yes. Longer answer, depends on what you can be content with. I run with most setting medium to low with low-impact settings such as bloom on high. I used to get only around 15-20 frames without AMD frame gen and a rough 30-40 on absolute lowest settings anywhere I could squeeze performance. Now I'm getting 24-30 fps no frame gen, and a very solid 48-55 fps with frame gen on low-medium settings. I'd say that's a huge improvement. 

If you haven't been following the development, good news is that the team is rolling out even more updates after this one at the time of writing. At this point, any rig with relatively updated CPU and GPU could run this just fine and at solid settings at 1080p. 

GAMEPLAY:

As an experienced Monster Hunter player who's been following since MH Freedom Unite, the feel of this game is a breath of fresh air. If you played MH Rise/Sunbreak and didn't enjoy it, but rather enjoyed World, you'll be delighted to know that this game is built on the skeleton of World and takes it to a whole new level with the active environments switching not only day/night cycles, but also weather, a persistently changing ecosystem that feels alive, and a buttery smooth quest system that feels natural to move in and out of. You have the option of taking on quests the old way; pick a paper from your handler and initiate, but now you have the option of opening your map and selecting any monster you see (showing bonus reward info) and creating an investigation right then and there! You can immediately choose to hunt it from the map. You can also encounter the large monsters in the wild which will initiate a hunt as well. 

Combat feels pretty much as weighty and polished as World does. If you played World and enjoyed the combat, you'll enjoy it here most likely as well. Not much to say in detail for the newcomers other than the combat is top notch. This is why you play Monster Hunter games. If there's any room for doubt about whether or not to pick up the game, it shouldn't be here. 10/10, won't find another game like it that does this point nearly as well, yet. 

Graphics, assuming your PC can handle them, are stunning. The world is so vibrant and dynamic. Fights feel like watching an epic movie. Battling the flagship monster feels like a movie every time it uses its strongest attack. The shifting weather, sand, storms, special effects from monsters, and textures for the most part, all look amazing. Apart from possibly not being able to run this (if you have a 2600x, 1070ti or lower/equivalent don't bother), you won't find much room for doubt here. This game looks magical and highly immersive.

Music, OH 10/10 don't even get me started. Absolute zero room for doubt. If you hate the sound track, I hate you. Your opinion is factually wrong and you stinky.

Difficulty, hard to say. Right now the game is not difficult as of high rank quests. I'm HR37, but I'm also experienced with these games. Comparing to other Monster Hunter games, I'd say World at times was harder. This is so far one of the easiest Monster Hunter games unfortunately. This is where a lot of the room for doubt could be for many. If you're used to souls like games, Action RPG as a whole, or have played other Monster Hunter games, you're gonna have to make your own difficulty here. There's not much of a challenge for much of the game if you're keeping up with your equipment that's available to you at any given time.

VERDICT:
Game is a certified 8/10 at the lowest. Easy 10/10 at the highest points. This is the kind of game you could put hundreds of hours into and not even realize it. The story is not a horrible slog to get through like World's story was. The environment and feel of the game is nothing short of amazing. You won't experienced anything else like it. Go in with a healthy expectation that the game will not be that hard for experienced ARPG players or that it will have a relatively steep learning curve at times for new ARPG players and you're in for an amazing ride with one of the best series of games there is to offer.

It's really, really good. Most fun I've had in Monster Hunter, having previous experience with Generations Ultimate, Rise, and World. I recommend it entirely.

Pre optimization patch, I was getting 30 frames on medium settings at 1080p. Post patch, it's at a constant 60 on high at 1440p. my PC has a 5060, and I imagine anyone who's only previous concern was performance now has nothing holding them back from enjoying this great game.

I will say though;
- The game is very easy. To be fair, I haven't seen G rank, but I'm maybe 60% through the main content playing solo and I haven't even been close to carting. I feel like this is due to a major compilation of minor "quality of life" features, which I'll get into, rather than an actual removal of difficulty, but for me it's very thumbs-to-the-side. Maybe a plus for new players.
- Not sure how I feel about focus mode. It allows easy access to most weapons coolest moves, like that thing with dual blades where you spin all the way down the length of a monster. It's fun and definitely flashy, but focus strikes on wounds provide such high damage, stun, and potential to create more wounds that it speeds up hunts by a lot. I found most on-tier fights lasting 10 minutes or less.
- The story is very good. At times it feels slow, but I prefer it to the boss rush simulator of previous titles. Nata is a very compelling character, chasing the box art monster through story missions feels great, and I like that my hunter is voice-acted. Play in Japanese with Palico language turned on and you'll have fun.
- Your seikret basically eliminates the danger from anything that doesn't one shot you. Fall on the ground? It's fine, press up on the d-pad and your chocobo companion whisks you away from (almost) all harm. It literally auto-runs by default so you can focus entirely on healing. It's like the wolf companion from Rise (but more OP) and I think that's a big reason why the community finds that game so easy.
- The grind is gone. Completely. Monsters provide so many materials that if you do your job and break enough parts, you WILL be able to craft a monster's weapon after fighting it once. Even high rank gems and plates and stuff almost always show as investigation rewards, which are no longer RNG but rather always available if a monster's in the locale. Use a lucky ticket and you can craft a whole armor set after one hunt. Big negative for me.
- Open world is good. It's not an overbearing open world, but rather a seamlessness between camp and the outside environment. Directly riding into the hunt from camp rather than dropping in via bird delivery is a great feeling. Sometimes this makes the map feel a bit small, but the way the environments change fixes that pretty quickly.
- Monster variety is incredible. My biggest unpopular opinion about World was that it felt like Jurassic Park. Too many stereotypical dinosaur-like brute wyverns. In Wilds though, there's huge arachnids, cephalopods, TRUE water dwelling creatures, and so much more. It's really unique, and the spectacle of seeing so many of these monsters for the first time is unmatched.

Overall, I think this game is really amazing, and for a new player, it's the best monster hunter experience you'll have. Most of my problems lie with the decreased difficulty, but again, I haven't seen G rank or the post-game event quests. If you're a returning veteran, I think the nuance has been taken away. No more tracking or grinding makes it feel like a lot of the true monster hunter experience has been removed in favor of convenience in excess. If you're one of those super hardcore guys playing first gen in 2026, you probably won't like Wilds.

TDLR; Do I recommend it? Yes. I recommend it at full price, and to most everyone. Very good game. Might update with more complete thoughts after I've finished the main story.

Monster Hunter veteran with 2,000+ hours across the series.
Monster Hunter Wilds is an outstanding entry with some questionable design choices 

The attention to detail in monster behavior, environments, and animation is genuinely next level. The world feels alive in a way the series has never achieved before. Watching monsters interact with each other and their surroundings adds a huge amount of immersion, and I don’t think this aspect is being praised enough.

Monster and equipment design is fire. Some of the best in the franchise in my opinion 

The story is a mixed. as a lore goblin in any game I appreciate the effort, and outside of Nata it’s actually pretty solid i think Nata’s anime style personality was cringe inducing . That said the cutscenes are really damn good, and the monster encounters in them are cinematic. every time the handler authorizes a hunt i bust a lil 

I really like the Focus system and the new wound system conceptually, but they currently feel overtuned.
Hunters are way more op than before but many monsters don’t seem to have been given enough new tools or reaction speed to compensate. Some monsters feel too slow and end up getting stun locked for large portions of the fight due to constant wound breaks 

This is most noticeable in lowrank but Once the real game begins after finishing the low rank story, difficulty does improve and monsters become more threatening  but the Focus system still feels stronger than it probably should be.

Good systems overall, but they need tuning.

Specs: RTX 4080 / AMD 5950X 64 gigs of DDR4

At launch performance was rough even on highend hardware. After the January patch, it’s a completely different experience. I’m now running at a smooth 120–140 FPS at max settings, native resolution. Major improvement.

 here are some Criticisms i have 

I don’t like the Seikret mount.

Default controls are poorly mapped and require adjustment.

Auto movement being enabled by default is ass.

I dislike the removal of traditional tracking mechanics.

Always seeing monster locations makes paintballs feel pointless.

The pack mechanic is a good idea but feels underutilized.

I haven’t reached endgame yet, so I can’t comment on that portion of the experience.

Despite some questionable changes to the classic formula, Capcom has improved what they built in world/iceborne with this release. i would say Wilds improves upon Monster Hunter in most areas that matter.

Highly recommended. Hyped for the next expansion

After 400+ hours into the game, safe to say I deeply enjoy it! 

Compared to other MH, I really enjoy the flow of combat and wealth of endgame content for a base-game. Of course, having played on launch, it wasn't always that way, but title updates felt paced well with enough content to keep me satiated. At first, there wasn't too many challenging hunts, but with the addition of Omega and 9* monsters, there are difficult encounters worth doing as well. If you like MH in general, you're sure to enjoy the game. 

Of course, there's things I could criticize, like the GPS seikret or too many people to a lobby, and I hope Capcom is listening to those criticism, but I do want to be a voice for the positive because this game gets more hate than it deserves.

As for performance, at first I had to use a cloud gaming service, as the game was un-runnable on my laptop. For reference, I have a 3050 Ti laptop GPU and a AMD Ryzen 7 5800U CPU as well as 16 GB of RAM. I didn't expect to be able to run it, and was grateful for the wealth of platforms that allowed me to. However, with the most recent performance update, my janky laptop with only 4 GB of VRAM is able to run the game with consistent 40-60 fps, albiet on the lowest graphical settings. I am happy to see that they are addressing these issues before the expansion, and can only hope that they learned their lesson and delay any future releases long enough to iron out these issues ahead of time. 

But yeah, overall, solid addition to the MH roster of games.

I recommend this game with an asterisk beside it. I started with World and fell in love. Bought Icebourne as soon as it released. Wilds is a great game, however it's missing a lot of elements that I loved in World. 

For instance, in World, you had to research the monster, investigate it, find it's trail before it would show up on the map. This made it feel more immersive and wasn't just a rinse and repeat hunt, like it feels with Wilds. The Seikret is an overpowered tool, IMO. It's too easy to get around the map. In World you had to run or fast travel, which if you ran, forced you to slow down and really look at the map. It slowed the game down so it didn't feel overly repetitive during hunts. On the Seikret, it's so easy to get around and with it's auto navigate feature, it makes the hunts go even faster since you no longer have to look for the monster.

I don't think the difficulty of the game is a problem. Some people say the monsters are too easy. Well, I guarantee those people have played a Monster Hunter game before and don't seem to understand that once you learn how to play the game (using your weapons, monster timing, counters/guard/buffs) it becomes easier. No matter which MH game you play. For example, I solely play with the Insect Glaive. I have used other weapons but that is my go-to weapon. When I first started, I sucked lol, but eventually I mastered the weapon in World. When Wilds came out, there were some new moves to the weapon and some new things to learn but for the most part, the weapon operated the same. Same for all the other weapons as well. I don't think people understand they've just gotten good at the game and choose to complain about how easy it is. Also, if you look at the history of MH games, Wilds is no different from the others. The base game is never supposed to be super difficult. It's supposed to help you learn how to play and basically get your feet wet for the real game - the DLC. The DLC is always where the games get challenging.

I have loved all of the Title updates and appreciate the fact that Capcom does it for free instead of making you pay for it like other companies do. The customization of weapons and armor are great. I do wish Capcom would bring back the "Apartment/Room" feature they had in World. I loved having my own space to show off all my trophies and all of the endemic life I had caught. I know my friends loved it too. Please bring this back for the DLC Capcom!

Now for the asterisk part of my recommendation...performance issues. I did not have any issues until the update after TU4. I am running a i9 12900k CPU, RTX 3090 GPU, and DDR5 64GB RAM. Respectfully, I should not being having issues. I check for driver updates about twice a month and update accordingly. Now my wife runs almost the same setup as I do, except her RTX 3090 died two years ago and so I replaced it with a 3070 Ti. Same CPU, same RAM, all of it. She has had nothing but performance issues since the launch of the game. Somewhere around TU3 the game became unplayable for her. It would freeze, crash, polygons wouldn't load, etc. Even when we turned all the settings down, nothing helped. Being perfectly honest, we have not played since the new patches came out (after TU4), so I can't speak to if they have fixed these issues or not. I just want people to be aware it's not just people with old outdated PCs having issues. If you buy the game, you might have issues, you might not. 

With all my gripes and the serious performance issues, I would still recommend this game. Even with it's problems, it's a lot of fun to play, especially with friends. There is definitely replay value and you will get your money's worth. My wife and I will be buying the DLC when it comes out.

Monster Hunter Wilds Review

Monster Hunter Wilds feels like the bold evolution the series has been building toward for years. Capcom has taken the core hunt–craft–conquer loop that fans love and dropped it into a living, breathing world that feels more dynamic and immersive than ever before.

The environments are the real star here. Wilds doesn’t just give you beautiful maps—it gives you ecosystems. Weather shifts mid-hunt, monsters interact with each other in natural ways, and the world feels unpredictable in the best sense. Hunts no longer feel isolated; they feel like you’re stepping into an ongoing struggle for survival where anything can happen.

Combat remains deeply satisfying, with each weapon retaining its unique identity while benefiting from smoother animations and smarter monster AI. Battles feel more intense and reactive, rewarding positioning, preparation, and adaptability. Whether you’re a longtime Great Sword main or experimenting with something new, every hunt feels earned.

Quality-of-life improvements make the experience more accessible without sacrificing depth. Traversal is faster and more fluid, tracking monsters feels more intuitive, and the game does a better job respecting your time—something both veterans and newcomers will appreciate.

Most importantly, Monster Hunter Wilds captures the spirit of discovery. It brings back that sense of awe you feel when facing a massive creature for the first time, learning its patterns, and finally bringing it down after a hard-fought battle. It’s familiar enough to feel like Monster Hunter, yet ambitious enough to feel genuinely fresh.

If this is the future of the franchise, Monster Hunter fans have a lot to be excited about.

I’ve been playing Wilds since launch, and I agree with the optimization complaints at release. I’m running on RTX 4070 with an AMD Ryzen 7800X3D, and I experienced noticeable frame rate drops and rendering glitches early on.

Despite the PC optimization issues, I stuck with the game because it’s genuinely fun. I also held off on posting a review until I had spent enough time with it. Now, at 498 hours of playtime, I feel it’s finally time to share my thoughts.

[u]Quality of Life Improvements I Noticed:[/u]
1. Palico AI - My Palico actually feels useful now instead of idling around so much. 
2. NPCs on Quests - They finally brought back the Rise feature where NPCs can assist you on hunts. It’s a great addition and made me care more about the supporting characters.
3. Improved Handler - This is a personal pet peeve, but I’m glad Alma is far less irritating than the handler from MHW. She actually helps and doesn’t constantly put herself in danger like the MHW handler did.
4. Map Improvements - The maps feel much more detailed and thoughtfully designed.
5. Character Voice Acting - Having our hunter fully voice-acted during quests and cutscenes is a welcome change after years of voiceless protagonists.
6. Gold Crown Hunting - They definitely improved this area unlike in previous titles where you have to manually observe and measure a monster if its up to par for gold crown size. With Wilds, you just have to use the binoculars and an indication will appear on the monster if its gold crown. I personally like this improvement because it saves a lot of time and back and forth. 

[u]Things I Still Don’t Like[/u]
1. Main Quest - The main story was honestly pretty mid. And don’t get me started on Nata—I was genuinely relieved when he got grounded and stopped showing up in side quests after the story ended.
2. Artisan and Gogma Weapons - You really have to pray to the RNG gods to get a good roll. Because of this system, there’s very little motivation to hunt specific monsters just to craft their weapons anymore.
3. Open World Design - I can see what the developers were going for with the interconnected open-world structure, but outside of the main quest, I barely used it. Most of the time, I just fast-traveled between camps.

[u]Things I Miss from MHWorld:[/u]
1. Quest Board - I miss being able to grab quests directly from the board instead of relying solely on the handler. Alma tends to wander around camp when idle and I think keeping both options would’ve been ideal.
2. Canteen / Cafeteria - Now meals are handled inside your tent or palicos deliver meals in the Gathering Hub. The dedicated canteen area is gone. I really miss having Palicos cook meals for us —and yes, I miss Granny Meowster.
3. Poogie - I miss having Poogie appear in each town like in MHWorld. Now, he is only in the Suja area. 

We haven’t received the endgame expansion yet (similar to Iceborne in World), so my opinion may change over time. That said, the game has improved significantly since the optimization updates. I no longer experience rendering glitches, and performance has been stable. I plan to keep playing.

I've waited writing a review to see what Capcom will do with the game. I very much appreciate the performance improvements updates (of which 2 have arrived since writing) but at this rate I fear the performance will never match the visuals. In general the visuals seem to be quite "muddy" or "sad" which I'm personally not a fan of.

Most of my review is written mostly with MH:World as comparison in mind, with some other and smaller stuff I noticed and felt like mentioning, my review elements are all over the place, sorry about that. 
The more open open-world of wilds is a bit disappointing, it still feels very much like areas just that they were stretched out to appear larger, and feel relatively empty to me at times. MH:World here maybe wasn't as open, but it was a lot denser which to me felt nicer. Many of the areas also feel quite flat. In the Scarlet Forest the water is just a bent plane for slopes... the idle water in general doesnt really look particularly nice in the forest especially in the flat areas higher up. I never felt the loading screens in older titles to break the immersion, I feel like loading screens here would've been fine, considering more than the other half of travel actions still have loading screens. 

A minor issue: On occasion running down a slope the hunter will have the animations and sounds like they are about to slide, but the slope is not nearly steep enough for that to happen, it seems very random, even happens in the Hub.
The hub is fine, seasonal event designs are very nice, as always. I also like the small animations of everything throughout the hub. Wondering why it was only released in TU1 and not on release though, constantly hearing the same camp theme everywhere kind of killed the momentum a bit for me at release, but it was mainly the the awful FPS at the time.

This is another minor issue but, for the Gogmazios fight, the player is told to bring dragon element weapons, amongst fire weapons, for the first phases. This is clearly noted to be an important aspect by the game, however playing it solo with only support hunters made me wonder: why do all the support hunters not use any dragon weapons? 
In general I find the Gogmazios fight to be a bit lame, or annoying even. It doesnt quite feel like a good fight, though I'm just simply not a fan of suddenly having to switch to this "slingshot minigame". First phase felt a bit lame/slow, but that's all I guess. Overall, imo not a good quest for grinding current endgame materials. Also why is the quest successfully completed music of the event quest version different from the assignment version? Would be cool to be able to set custom music for that using existing tracks so that the one MH4U theme is not just present in the assignment version which is not replayable unless you join an SOS quest.

Omega fight was hard, but pretty fun and quite challenging, I liked it. The support hunters on that quest also do a nice job. Though in the last phase I feel like Omega can spam the charge/dash forward attack far too much. The music was also good, for all the phases.

On the topic of music, I don't have much to complain about, though overall I feel like the music in Wilds sounds rather soft and, in my opinion, not fitting the game. There are outliers like Rey Dau's theme which is pretty good, but otherwise it feels like the music doesn't have much of an impact during fighting as it probably could have had.

With that said, I am quite disappointed that each main camp in each region just has the exact same theme and there aren't villages or village-like areas that is the "base of operations" for a specific area with their own unique theme like in old monster hunter titles, especially ones before MH:World. Neither is it possible to use earned BGM for them except your personal tent.
For the next "tradition" thing, I miss having a cat-chef cooking for the hunters.

By the way, Normal MHWilds at this time takes up as much space as MH:World + Iceborne + High Res. Texture pack (~98GB) 
Maybe it's normal, but it doesn't quite feel like it considering Wilds isn't even finished and has no expansion DLC yet, and it's getting close to RDR2 with ~119GB

Endemic life; The endemic life in this game is... ok, feels static in the wild, doesn't do much and doesn't interact with the environment really besides some things, can't really use caught stuff for anything either. In this aspect MH:World felt a lot livelier.

I had, and still have fun, just not as much as I and likely others could have had. In it's current stage, thinking about the game feels sad/regretful in the end. If I forget to update the review should the game be good enough for me to recommend it, see it as a reminder at how different this game was in the first and possibly coming year since release and that this shouldn't have happened
The latest optimization patch has made this game the best version of itself.

Hey, Third Fleeter here.
As of the patch that released on the 28th of January i can finally recommend this game. Stable frames with no stutters, even on lower end hardware. Finally you can enjoy it fluently.
The lack of difficulty, that was present in the early release days, is gone with savage omega and 9☆ quests, for example. The Grand Hub is a very nice and open place, way better than what we got in base World. They brought my boy Lagiacrus back and he is just absolutely beautiful. I also love that they brought back RNG-Talismans and greatly increased the drop rates for decorations, not like in Worldborne, so you can finally enjoy almost fully optimized builds (I am looking at you Attack-Jewel -.-)

But i still got my gripes with this game.

What i don't like is the amount of DLC's they are pushing, fckin 15 glasses from Event-Quests, the Hunter-Symbols for crafting weapons (<-this is the true endgame grind), the "Wilds" aspect of the game (where is it? You just drop in the Quest, know every location of monsters, slap the ♥♥♥♥ out of everyone and done), the soundtrack is not as good as older titles (except Omega, damn), and most of the maps are way too confusing in my opinion. Also you MUST use your Seikret to traverse parts of the maps, which is just a selfdriving taxi and thats why you will never fully learn the maps.

Still goin to recommend it, you will have a lot of fun. Now we wait for the G-Rank expansion to fix all of the problems above, hopefully.
